Notebook

Multi-Agent Systems (Crash Course)

A Comprehensive Learning Notebook

Course Level: Advanced Undergraduate / Graduate

Prerequisites: Basic AI, Logic, Probability

How to use this notebook: Read each chapter carefully. Study the mathematical foundation. Answer the questions yourself before reading the answers. Revisit sections when you encounter complex algorithms.

Chapter 1: Introduction to Multi-Agent Systems

Chapter Learning Objectives

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Define what an agent is and describe its core properties
  • Distinguish between single-agent and multi-agent systems
  • Explain the mathematical underpinnings of rationality and autonomy
  • Identify and classify real-world applications of MAS
  • Understand the advantages and fundamental challenges of MAS

1.1: Fundamentals of Intelligent Agents

1.1.1: Definition of an Agent

An agent is anything that can be viewed as perceiving its environment through sensors and acting upon that environment through actuators.

This definition, introduced by Russell and Norvig, is deceptively simple but profoundly powerful. Let us unpack it carefully.

Formal Definition

An agent can be formally defined as a tuple:

$$\mathcal{A} = \langle P, A, E, f \rangle$$

Where:

  • $P$ = a set of percepts (inputs from the environment)
  • $A$ = a set of actions (outputs to the environment)
  • $E$ = the environment the agent inhabits
  • $f : P^* \rightarrow A$ = the agent function mapping percept histories to actions

The agent function $f$ is the mathematical brain of the agent. It tells us: given everything the agent has perceived so far, what action should it take?

Percept Sequence

Let the percept at time $t$ be $p_t \in P$. The percept sequence up to time $T$ is:

$$\mathbf{p}_{1:T} = (p_1, p_2, p_3, \ldots, p_T)$$

The agent function then maps this sequence to an action:

$$a_T = f(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_T) = f(\mathbf{p}_{1:T})$$

This means the agent's decision can be influenced by its entire history of perceptions, not just the current input.

Agent Program vs. Agent Function

An important distinction:

ConceptDescriptionLives in
Agent FunctionAbstract mathematical mapping $f : P^* \rightarrow A$Mathematics / Specification
Agent ProgramConcrete implementation of $f$Computer Memory / Code

The agent function is infinite (all possible percept sequences), but the agent program must be finite and computable.

Simple Example: Thermostat Agent

  • Percept: Current temperature $T_{current}$
  • Action: $A = \{\text{heat_on}, \text{heat_off}\}$
  • Agent Function:

$$f(T_{current}) = \begin{cases} \text{heat_on} & \text{if } T_{current} < T_{desired} \\ \text{heat_off} & \text{if } T_{current} \geq T_{desired} \end{cases}$$

This simple example captures the essence: sense, decide, act.

FAQs: 1.1.1

Q1: What is the difference between an agent function and an agent program?

A: The agent function $f : P^* \rightarrow A$ is an abstract mathematical specification of how every possible percept sequence maps to an action. It is conceptually infinite. The agent program is the finite, computable implementation of this function that runs on real hardware with limited memory. The agent program approximates the agent function within resource constraints.

Q2: Why does the agent function take a percept sequence rather than just the current percept?

A: Because the current percept alone may be insufficient to determine the best action, the agent may need memory of past perceptions. For example, a robot navigating a maze needs to remember where it has already been (past percepts) to avoid revisiting dead ends.

Q3: Can an agent function be non-deterministic?

A: Yes. A stochastic agent function maps a percept sequence to a probability distribution over actions: $f : P^* \rightarrow \Delta(A)$, where $\Delta(A)$ is the probability simplex over actions. This is useful when randomization improves performance in adversarial or uncertain environments.

1.1.2: Characteristics of Intelligent Agents

Not every computational system qualifies as an intelligent agent. Intelligent agents possess a specific set of properties that distinguish them from simple programs.

The PAMA Properties (Wooldridge & Jennings, 1995)

The four core properties of intelligent agents:

$$\text{Intelligent Agent} \iff \{\text{Proactiveness} \cup \text{Autonomy} \cup \text{Reactivity} \cup \text{Social Ability}\}$$

Let us define each formally:

1. Reactivity

An agent must perceive its environment and respond in a timely fashion to changes.

$$\text{Reactivity}: \exists \delta > 0 \text{ such that } \forall e \in E, \text{response time} < \delta$$

A reactive agent always responds to environmental stimuli. The response time $\delta$ must be bounded and appropriate to the problem domain.

2. Proactiveness (Goal-Directedness)

An agent must exhibit goal-directed behavior, it does not merely react to its environment but takes initiative to achieve its goals.

$$\text{Proactiveness}: \exists G \text{ (goals) s.t. agent takes actions } a \in A \text{ to maximize } P(\text{achieve}(G))$$

A proactive agent plans and initiates actions even when no external stimulus is present.

3. Social Ability

An agent must be able to interact with other agents (and possibly humans) through some agent-communication language.

$$\text{Social Ability}: \exists L \text{ (language) s.t. agent can send/receive messages in } L$$

Social ability includes cooperation, negotiation, and coordination behaviors.

4. Autonomy

An agent must be able to operate without direct human intervention, with control over its own state and actions.

$$\text{Autonomy}: \frac{\partial a}{\partial \text{human_input}} \approx 0 \text{ (decisions are internally generated)}$$

This informal "derivative" expresses that actions should depend minimally on human instruction and maximally on the agent's own reasoning.

Extended Properties

Many researchers add further properties:

PropertyDescriptionFormal Notion
MobilityAbility to move across networks$\exists$ migration function $\mu : N \times A \rightarrow N$
VeracityAgent does not knowingly communicate false info$\forall m \in M_{sent}: \text{believes}(m)$
BenevolenceAgent tries to help others$U_{agent}$ includes $U_{others}$
RationalityAgent acts to achieve its goals$\arg\max_a U(a)$
AdaptabilityAgent learns from experience$f_t \neq f_{t+1}$ after learning

Measuring Intelligence: The Performance Measure

Agent intelligence is measured by a performance measure $P$:

$$P : \text{Environment_History} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$

This assigns a real-valued score to every sequence of environment states. A more intelligent agent achieves higher scores on average:

$$\text{Intelligence} \propto \mathbb{E}[P(e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_T)]$$

FAQs: 1.1.2

Q1: An agent that only reacts to stimuli but never initiates action, is it intelligent?

A: No, not fully. While reactivity is necessary, it is not sufficient. True intelligence (by the Wooldridge-Jennings definition) requires proactiveness, the agent must also take initiative to achieve goals even in the absence of external prompts. A purely reactive thermostat is not considered intelligent; an agent that also anticipates temperature changes and pre-heats a room is.

Q2: How do we distinguish a "smart" simple program from a genuine intelligent agent?

A: The key differentiators are social ability and autonomy. A smart program (e.g., a sorting algorithm) has no social ability and is entirely dependent on its programmer's instructions. An intelligent agent can communicate with others, handle unforeseen situations, and make decisions independently. The agent must satisfy all four PAMA properties simultaneously.

Q3: Why is veracity important in multi-agent systems?

A: In a multi-agent system, agents share information and rely on each other's communications to make decisions. If agents communicate false information (even unintentionally), the entire system can malfunction. Veracity ensures the integrity of the shared information space, which is the foundation of coordination and cooperation.

1.1.3: Rationality and Autonomy

Rationality is the cornerstone of intelligent agent design. An agent is rational if it does the right thing given what it knows.

Formal Definition of Rationality

A rational agent is one that, for each possible percept sequence, selects the action that maximizes the expected value of the performance measure:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \mathbb{E}[P \mid \mathbf{p}_{1:T}, a]$$

Where:

  • $a^*$ = the optimal action
  • $P$ = the performance measure
  • $\mathbf{p}_{1:T}$ = the percept sequence
  • $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ = expectation over uncertain outcomes

Rationality vs. Omniscience

This is a crucial distinction:

PropertyAgent knowsActs
OmniscientTrue outcomes of actionsAlways optimally
RationalExpected outcomesOptimally given knowledge
Short-sightedOnly immediate outcomesGreedily

A rational agent is NOT omniscient. It maximizes expected utility based on available information:

$$\text{Rationality} \neq \text{Omniscience}$$

$$\text{Rationality} = \arg\max_{a} \mathbb{E}_{e \sim P(e | \mathbf{p}_{1:T})}[P(e | a)]$$

Information Gathering as a Rational Action

Sometimes the most rational action is to gather more information before acting. Formally, the value of information $VI$ is:

$$VI(X) = \mathbb{E}[\max_a U(a | X)] - \max_a \mathbb{E}[U(a)]$$

Where $X$ is the additional information. It is rational to gather information $X$ if and only if $VI(X) > 0$.

Bounded Rationality (Simon, 1957)

In practice, agents have limited computational resources. Herbert Simon introduced the concept of bounded rationality:

$$a^*_{bounded} = \arg\max_{a \in A'} \mathbb{E}[P \mid \mathbf{p}_{1:T}, a]$$

Where $A' \subset A$ is the feasible action set given computational constraints. The agent does not find the global optimum but satisfices, finds an action that is "good enough."

Satisficing threshold $\theta$:

$$a_{satisficing} = \text{first } a \in A' \text{ s.t. } \mathbb{E}[P \mid a] \geq \theta$$

Autonomy: A Deeper Look

Autonomy exists on a spectrum:

$$\text{Autonomy Level} = 1 - \frac{\text{Human_Control_Exercised}}{\text{Total_Decisions_Made}}$$

LevelNameDescription
0Fully ManualHuman controls everything
0.25SupervisedHuman approves agent suggestions
0.5Semi-AutonomousAgent acts; human can override
0.75Mostly AutonomousAgent acts; human monitors
1.0Fully AutonomousAgent operates independently

Autonomy and Trust Relationship:

$$\text{Appropriate Autonomy} = f(\text{Trust}, \text{Competence}, \text{Risk})$$

Higher trust + higher competence + lower risk → higher appropriate autonomy.

Expected Utility Theory

Rational agents maximize expected utility (EU):

$$EU(a) = \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

Where:

  • $S$ = set of possible states
  • $P(s' \mid s, a)$ = probability of reaching state $s'$ from state $s$ via action $a$
  • $U(s')$ = utility of state $s'$
  • $EU(a)$ = expected utility of action $a$

The rational action is:

$$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} EU(a) = \arg\max_{a \in A} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \cdot U(s')$$

FAQs: 1.1.3

Q1: An agent always picks the action with the highest immediate reward. Is it rational?

A: Not necessarily. Rationality requires maximizing expected cumulative performance, not just immediate reward. A rational agent considers long-term consequences. An agent that always picks the immediately rewarding action may be "greedy" but not globally rational, for example, it might eat all food resources now and starve later.

Q2: How does bounded rationality affect the design of real-world MAS?

A: In real-world MAS, agents cannot compute the true optimal action due to time, memory, and computational limitations. Bounded rationality forces designers to use heuristics, approximations, and satisficing strategies. This means agent behavior is "good enough" rather than perfect, and system designers must carefully balance solution quality against computational cost.

Q3: What is the expected utility of a fair coin toss where heads gives $\$100$ and tails gives $\$0$?

A: $EU = 0.5 \times 100 + 0.5 \times 0 = \$50$. A rational agent would prefer any certain payoff greater than $\$50$ over this coin toss.

1.1.4: Agent Environments

The environment is everything outside the agent. Understanding environments is critical because the environment determines the complexity of the agent's decision problem.

Formal Environment Definition

An environment can be modeled as a state transition system:

$$\mathcal{E} = \langle S, A, T, R, O, \Omega \rangle$$

Where:

  • $S$ = set of environment states
  • $A$ = set of actions available to agents
  • $T : S \times A \rightarrow \Delta(S)$ = transition function (probability distribution over next states)
  • $R : S \times A \times S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ = reward function
  • $O$ = set of possible observations
  • $\Omega : S \times A \rightarrow \Delta(O)$ = observation function

Environment Classification Taxonomy

Environments can be classified along 7 dimensions (Russell & Norvig):

1. Fully Observable vs. Partially Observable

A fully observable environment: the agent's sensors give complete access to the state of the environment.

$$\text{Fully Observable} \iff \forall s \in S, \exists o \in O \text{ s.t. } o \text{ uniquely identifies } s$$

A partially observable environment: the agent cannot directly observe the full state.

$$\text{Partially Observable} \iff \exists s_1, s_2 \in S (s_1 \neq s_2) \text{ s.t. } P(o | s_1) = P(o | s_2)$$

2. Deterministic vs. Stochastic

Deterministic: The next state is completely determined by the current state and action.

$$T(s, a) = s' \text{ (a single specific state, no probability)}$$

Stochastic: The next state is a probability distribution.

$$T(s, a) = \Delta(S) \text{ (a distribution over states)}$$

3. Episodic vs. Sequential

Episodic: Each episode is independent. The agent's action in one episode does not affect future episodes.

$$\text{Episodic}: (s_t, a_t) \perp (s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) \text{ for each episode}$$

Sequential: Past actions affect future states and decisions.

$$s_{t+1} = T(s_t, a_t) \Rightarrow s_{t+2} = T(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) = T(T(s_t, a_t), a_{t+1})$$

4. Static vs. Dynamic

Static: The environment does not change while the agent is deliberating.

$$\frac{\partial s}{\partial t} = 0 \text{ during deliberation}$$

Dynamic: The environment changes independently of the agent's actions.

$$\frac{\partial s}{\partial t} \neq 0 \text{ even when no agent action is taken}$$

Semi-dynamic: The environment doesn't change, but the agent's performance score does (e.g., time pressure).

5. Discrete vs. Continuous

Discrete: Finite or countable states, actions, and time steps.

$$|S| < \infty, |A| < \infty$$

Continuous: Uncountable (real-valued) states/actions.

$$S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n, A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m$$

6. Single-agent vs. Multi-agent

Single-agent: One agent acts in the environment.

Multi-agent: Multiple agents act simultaneously; each agent's environment includes other agents.

$$\text{Multi-agent}: \mathcal{E} \text{ contains } n > 1 \text{ agents, where } a^i_t \text{ affects } s_{t+1} \text{ for all agents}$$

The joint action space is:

$$\mathcal{A}_{joint} = A^1 \times A^2 \times \cdots \times A^n$$

7. Known vs. Unknown

Known: The agent knows the transition model $T$ and reward function $R$.

Unknown: The agent must learn $T$ and/or $R$ through experience.

Complexity Summary Table

Environment TypeComplexity for Agent
Fully Observable + Deterministic + Episodic + Static + DiscreteLowest (simple lookup)
Partially Observable + Stochastic + Sequential + Dynamic + Continuous + Multi-agentHighest (requires full ML/planning stack)

FAQs: 1.1.4

Q1: A chess-playing agent, classify its environment.

A: Chess is: Fully Observable (both players see the board), Deterministic (moves have certain outcomes), Sequential (each move affects future positions), Static (board doesn't change while player thinks), Discrete (finite states and moves), and Multi-agent (two players). This makes chess hard due to the large discrete state space, but not due to stochasticity.

Q2: Why does partial observability make an agent's problem fundamentally harder?

A: With partial observability, the agent cannot directly observe the true state $s$. It must maintain a belief state, a probability distribution over possible states: $b(s) = P(s | \mathbf{o}_{1:t})$. The agent now optimizes over this belief space, which is infinite-dimensional even when the original state space is finite. This is the Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework, which is computationally far more complex than the fully observable MDP.

Q3: Give an example of a semi-dynamic environment.

A: A timed chess game (speed chess) is semi-dynamic. The board doesn't change while you think (static environment), but your performance score decreases as you use more time on the clock. The agent's performance degrades with deliberation time, incentivizing faster (possibly suboptimal) decisions.

1.2: Multi-Agent Systems Overview

1.2.1: Single-Agent vs. Multi-Agent Systems

A Multi-Agent System (MAS) consists of a collection of agents that interact within a shared environment. Understanding how MAS differs from single-agent systems reveals why MAS is necessary and powerful.

Single-Agent System (SAS)

In a single-agent system:

$$\mathcal{SAS} = \langle \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{E} \rangle$$

Where $\mathcal{A}$ is a single agent and $\mathcal{E}$ is the environment. The agent has a well-defined goal and optimizes independently:

$$a^* = \arg\max_a \mathbb{E}[P_{\mathcal{A}}(s) | a]$$

Multi-Agent System (MAS)

In a multi-agent system:

$$\mathcal{MAS} = \langle \{\mathcal{A}_1, \mathcal{A}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{A}_n\}, \mathcal{E} \rangle$$

Each agent $\mathcal{A}_i$ must now consider that other agents are also acting in the environment. The environment transition depends on the joint action of all agents:

$$s_{t+1} \sim T(s_t, a^1_t, a^2_t, \ldots, a^n_t)$$

The Fundamental MAS Problem

In MAS, no single agent controls the entire system. Each agent $i$ optimizes its own utility $U_i$, but the outcome depends on all agents' actions simultaneously:

$$U_i = f(a^1, a^2, \ldots, a^n, e)$$

This creates a fundamentally game-theoretic problem. Agents must reason about the strategies of other agents.

Key Differences

DimensionSingle-AgentMulti-Agent
Optimization$\max_a U$$\max_{a^i} U_i$ for each $i$
AdversaryEnvironment onlyEnvironment + other agents
KnowledgeOwn stateOwn + others' (possibly)
Decision ModelMDPGame / POMDP + Game
GoalSingle global goalMultiple (possibly conflicting) goals
CommunicationNot requiredOften essential
ScalabilityLimitedHighly scalable

When MAS is Necessary

MAS is necessary rather than merely useful when:

  1. Problem is inherently distributed: No single point of data or control
  2. Speed requires parallelism: Problem is too large for one agent
  3. Expertise is specialized: Different domains require different competencies
  4. Robustness is required: No single point of failure can be tolerated
  5. Competitive interests exist: Different stakeholders have different goals

    6. FAQs: 1.2.1

    Q1: In a chess game between two AI agents, is it a single-agent or multi-agent system?

    A: It is a multi-agent system with two agents, each with opposing objectives. Each agent must model and predict the other's strategy to play optimally. This is a zero-sum game where one agent's gain is the other's loss, making the optimal strategy a minimax solution rather than a simple maximum.

    Q2: Why can't we just use a single powerful agent to control everything in MAS?

    A: Several reasons make this impossible or impractical: (1) Communication overhead, transmitting all information to one center is prohibitively expensive; (2) Computational bottleneck, a single agent cannot process all information fast enough; (3) Reliability, a centralized agent is a single point of failure; (4) Ownership, in real systems, different organizations own different parts; (5) Privacy, agents may not wish to share their internal state.

1.2.2: Distributed Intelligence

Distributed intelligence is the core paradigm of MAS, intelligence emerges from the interaction of many simpler agents, each solving part of the problem.

Emergence

Emergence is the phenomenon where complex global behavior arises from simple local interactions:

$$\text{Global Behavior} = \mathcal{F}(\text{Local Rules}_1, \text{Local Rules}_2, \ldots, \text{Local Rules}_n)$$

The function $\mathcal{F}$ is not a simple sum, the whole is greater (or at least different) than the sum of its parts.

Classic Example, Ant Colony:

  • Each ant follows: "If you find food, leave a pheromone trail"
  • No ant has a global plan
  • Collectively, ants find the shortest path to food

This is emergent optimization. The pheromone trail strength $\tau_{ij}$ on edge $(i,j)$ evolves as:

$$\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\rho \tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$$

Where:

  • $\rho$ = evaporation rate (ensures pheromones fade over time)
  • $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k$ = pheromone deposited by ant $k$ on edge $(i,j)$
  • $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q / L_k$ if ant $k$ used edge $(i,j)$, else $0$
  • $Q$ = constant, $L_k$ = total tour length of ant $k$

Shorter tours result in more pheromone per edge → ants prefer shorter tours → shortest path emerges.

Swarm Intelligence

Swarm intelligence is distributed intelligence applied to large groups of simple agents:

$$\text{Swarm Intelligence} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \text{Collective Intelligence of } n \text{ simple agents}$$

Key properties of swarm intelligence:

  • Decentralization: No single agent is in charge
  • Self-organization: Structure emerges without a blueprint
  • Scalability: Performance improves with more agents
  • Robustness: System works even if some agents fail

Distributed Problem Solving (DPS)

In DPS, a large problem $\Pi$ is decomposed into subproblems:

$$\Pi = \Pi_1 \cup \Pi_2 \cup \cdots \cup \Pi_n$$

Each agent $\mathcal{A}_i$ solves its subproblem $\Pi_i$:

$$\mathcal{A}_i \rightarrow \text{Solution}(\Pi_i) = \sigma_i$$

The solutions are then combined:

$$\text{Solution}(\Pi) = \mathcal{C}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \ldots, \sigma_n)$$

Where $\mathcal{C}$ is a combination/aggregation function.

Conditions for effective DPS:

  1. Decomposability: $\Pi$ can be meaningfully split
  2. Combinability: Solutions $\sigma_i$ can be combined without loss
  3. Independence: Subproblems have minimal interdependency (or interdependencies can be managed)

    4. FAQs: 1.2.2

    Q1: Can emergent behavior be predicted mathematically?

    A: Partially. Local rules and their mathematical consequences can sometimes be analyzed (e.g., pheromone dynamics in ACO, velocity update rules in PSO). However, truly emergent behavior is often difficult to predict analytically because the system exhibits nonlinearity, small changes in initial conditions or rules can lead to dramatically different global behaviors (sensitivity to initial conditions). Statistical mechanics and mean-field theory are sometimes used to approximate emergent outcomes.

    Q2: What distinguishes swarm intelligence from parallel computing?

    A: Parallel computing assigns tasks to processors with explicit coordination, each processor knows exactly what to do, controlled by a master program. Swarm intelligence involves autonomous agents following local rules with no central coordinator, no explicit task assignment, and emergent (unplanned) global behavior. Parallel computing is designed efficiency; swarm intelligence is emergent efficiency.

1.2.3: Advantages of MAS

Multi-agent systems offer compelling advantages over monolithic single-agent approaches.

1. Scalability

MAS scales horizontally, adding more agents increases capacity:

$$\text{Capacity}(n) \propto n \cdot \text{Capacity per Agent}$$

In ideal cases (no communication overhead), performance scales linearly. In practice, the Amdahl's Law analogy applies:

$$\text{Speedup}(n) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{n}}$$

Where $p$ is the fraction of the task that can be parallelized. As $n \to \infty$, speedup $\to \frac{1}{1-p}$.

2. Robustness and Fault Tolerance

In a MAS with $n$ agents, if $k$ agents fail, the system continues with $n-k$ agents:

$$\text{System Failure} \iff k > n - n_{min}$$

Where $n_{min}$ is the minimum number of agents required for basic function. Contrast with a centralized system:

$$\text{Central System Failure} \iff \text{Central Agent Fails} \Leftrightarrow P(\text{failure}) = p_0$$

In MAS:

$$P(\text{failure}) = P(\text{more than } n - n_{min} \text{ agents fail}) = \sum_{k=n-n_{min}+1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$$

For small $p$ and large $n - n_{min}$, this probability is negligibly small.

3. Speed via Parallelism

Solving a problem of size $N$ with $n$ agents:

$$T_{parallel} \approx \frac{T_{serial}}{n} + T_{comm}$$

Where $T_{comm}$ is communication overhead. For $T_{comm} \ll T_{serial}$:

$$\text{Speedup} \approx n$$

4. Solving Inherently Distributed Problems

Some problems have no meaningful centralized solution, they are intrinsically distributed:

$$\exists \Pi : \text{No centralized algorithm can solve } \Pi \text{ efficiently}$$

Examples:

  • Internet routing (each router is an agent)
  • Power grid management (each substation is an agent)
  • Supply chain coordination (each company is an agent)

5. Specialization and Expertise

Agents can be specialized for different domains:

$$\mathcal{A}_i \text{ is an expert in domain } D_i$$

The system $\{\mathcal{A}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{A}_n\}$ covers all domains $\bigcup_i D_i$, solving problems no single agent could handle.

Advantage Summary Table

AdvantageMeasureFormula
ScalabilityThroughput gain$T \propto n$
RobustnessFailure prob.$P(fail) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$
SpeedTime reduction$T_{MAS} \approx T_{serial}/n$
AdaptabilityFlexibility score$F \propto$ agent diversity
NaturalnessProblem fitProblem-to-model distance $\to 0$

FAQs: 1.2.3

Q1: Does adding more agents always improve MAS performance?

A: No. There are diminishing returns and sometimes negative returns due to: (1) Communication overhead that grows as $O(n^2)$ in fully connected networks; (2) Coordination cost, more agents require more synchronization; (3) Conflict, more agents competing for resources can create bottlenecks. The optimal number of agents $n^*$ minimizes total cost = problem-solving cost + coordination cost.

Q2: Calculate the probability of MAS failure if each agent has a 5% failure probability, we have 10 agents, and we need at least 6 to function.

A: We need more than 4 agents to fail for system failure. $P(\text{fail}) = \sum_{k=5}^{10} \binom{10}{k} (0.05)^k (0.95)^{10-k}$. The dominant term is $k=5$: $\binom{10}{5}(0.05)^5(0.95)^5 \approx 252 \times 3.125 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.7738 \approx 6.1 \times 10^{-5}$. Total ≈ $6.1 \times 10^{-5}$, extremely small. A centralized system with 5% failure probability would fail 100× more often.

1.2.4: Challenges in MAS

Despite their advantages, MAS introduce fundamental challenges that do not exist in single-agent systems.

Challenge 1: Coordination Problem

When multiple agents act simultaneously, their actions can interfere with each other:

$$\text{Interference}: \exists i \neq j \text{ s.t. } a^i \text{ negatively affects } U_j$$

Avoiding interference requires coordination, a distributed consensus problem.

Example, Resource Contention:

Two agents want to write to the same resource. If uncoordinated:

$$P(\text{conflict}) = 1 - P(\text{agent 1 not using resource}) \cdot P(\text{agent 2 not using resource})$$

With $n$ agents, each using a resource with probability $q$:

$$P(\text{at least one conflict}) = 1 - (1-q)^n$$

This grows rapidly with $n$, necessitating coordination mechanisms.

Challenge 2: Coherence vs. Autonomy Trade-off

Agents that are fully autonomous may pursue local optima that conflict with the global optimum:

$$\text{Global Optimum} \neq \sum_i \text{Local Optima}_i$$

This is formalized as the Price of Anarchy (PoA):

$$PoA = \frac{\text{Social Optimum Welfare}}{\text{Nash Equilibrium Welfare}}$$

A PoA $> 1$ means that selfish agent behavior leads to a suboptimal global outcome. Famous example: the Braess Paradox in traffic routing.

Challenge 3: Communication Overhead

In a fully connected MAS with $n$ agents, every pair communicates:

$$\text{Communication Links} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)$$

Message volume grows quadratically with agents. For $n = 100$: 4,950 potential communication links. For $n = 1000$: 499,500 links.

Challenge 4: Incomplete Information and Uncertainty

Agents often have only local knowledge:

$$K_i \subsetneq K_{global}$$

Where $K_i$ is agent $i$'s knowledge and $K_{global}$ is all knowledge in the system.

This leads to the knowledge gap problem:

$$\text{Knowledge Gap}_i = K_{global} \setminus K_i$$

Decisions based on incomplete knowledge can be suboptimal.

Challenge 5: Security and Trust

In open MAS (where agents can join/leave), malicious agents can:

  • Lie (send false information)
  • Defect (not cooperate as promised)
  • Cheat (violate protocol)

Trust can be modeled as a probability:

$$\text{Trust}(A_j \rightarrow A_i) = P(A_j \text{ acts as promised} | \text{history})$$

This is often computed using Bayesian updating:

$$\text{Trust}_{t+1}(j) = \frac{P(\text{observation} | \text{trustworthy}) \cdot \text{Trust}_t(j)}{P(\text{observation})}$$

Challenge 6: Computational Complexity

Computing the optimal joint policy for $n$ agents is PSPACE-hard in general:

$$\text{Optimal Joint Policy} \in \text{PSPACE-hard}$$

Even with $n = 2$ agents, coordination games can be NP-hard. This forces designers to use approximate or heuristic methods.

FAQs: 1.2.4

Q1: What is the Price of Anarchy and why does it matter?

A: The Price of Anarchy (PoA) measures how much system performance degrades when agents act selfishly compared to the socially optimal outcome. A PoA of 2 means selfish behavior is twice as costly to the system as coordinated behavior. It matters because it tells designers how much to invest in coordination mechanisms, a low PoA means coordination provides little benefit, while a high PoA (e.g., in traffic networks) indicates strong incentives for regulation.

Q2: How can we reduce communication overhead in a MAS with 1000 agents?

A: Several approaches: (1) Hierarchical communication, organize agents into a tree structure with $O(n \log n)$ total communication; (2) Broadcast suppression, agents only communicate when their state changes significantly (event-driven); (3) Gossip protocols, each agent randomly shares information with a small subset, achieving eventual global dissemination in $O(\log n)$ rounds; (4) Blackboard systems, a shared workspace where agents post and read information; (5) Local communication only, agents only communicate with neighbors in a predefined topology.

1.3: Applications of MAS

1.3.1: Robotics

Multi-robot systems are one of the most vibrant applications of MAS. Teams of robots collaborate to achieve tasks that would be impossible or impractical for a single robot.

Multi-Robot Coverage Problem

Given a region $\mathcal{R} \subset \mathbb{R}^2$, the coverage problem asks: how should $n$ robots partition $\mathcal{R}$ to minimize total coverage time?

Voronoi Partitioning: Each robot $\mathcal{A}_i$ covers the Voronoi cell $V_i$:

$$V_i = \{x \in \mathcal{R} : \|x - p_i\| \leq \|x - p_j\| \text{ for all } j \neq i\}$$

Where $p_i$ is the position of robot $i$. Each robot covers all points closer to it than to any other robot.

Optimal Deployment: The optimal robot positions minimize the maximum coverage distance:

$$\{p_1^*, \ldots, p_n^*\} = \arg\min_{\{p_i\}} \max_{x \in \mathcal{R}} \min_i \|x - p_i\|$$

This is the minimax facility location problem.

Formation Control

For robots moving in formation, each robot $i$ maintains:

$$x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + v_i(t) \cdot \Delta t$$

Where velocity $v_i(t)$ is determined by:

$$v_i(t) = \sum_{j \in N_i} w_{ij}(x_j(t) - x_i(t)) + v_{leader}(t)$$

$N_i$ = neighbors of robot $i$, $w_{ij}$ = interaction weights.

Swarm Robotics Example: Boids Model

The Boids model (Reynolds, 1987) produces flocking behavior from three local rules:

  1. Separation: Avoid crowding neighbors

    2. $$\mathbf{v}_{sep} = -\sum_{j \in N_i, \|d_{ij}\| < r_s} \frac{\mathbf{d}_{ij}}{\|\mathbf{d}_{ij}\|}$$

    1. Alignment: Steer toward average heading of neighbors

      2. $$\mathbf{v}_{align} = \frac{1}{|N_i|} \sum_{j \in N_i} \mathbf{v}_j$$

      1. Cohesion: Move toward center of mass of neighbors

        2. $$\mathbf{v}_{coh} = \frac{1}{|N_i|} \sum_{j \in N_i} \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_i$$

        Final velocity: $\mathbf{v}_i = w_s \mathbf{v}_{sep} + w_a \mathbf{v}_{align} + w_c \mathbf{v}_{coh}$

        Three simple local rules → complex emergent flocking behavior.

        1.3.2: Smart Grids

        Smart grids use MAS where each household, generator, and storage unit is an agent cooperating to balance supply and demand.

        Supply-Demand Balance

        At each time step $t$, the grid requires:

        $$\sum_{i \in \text{generators}} P_i^{gen}(t) = \sum_{j \in \text{consumers}} P_j^{con}(t) + P^{loss}(t)$$

        MAS achieves this balance through automatic demand response. Consumer agent $j$ modulates demand $P_j^{con}(t)$ based on real-time price signals $\lambda(t)$:

        $$P_j^{con}(t) = D_j^{max} \cdot \left(1 - \alpha_j \cdot \frac{\lambda(t) - \lambda^{ref}}{\lambda^{ref}}\right)$$

        Where $\alpha_j$ is the price sensitivity of consumer $j$.

        Energy Auction Mechanism

        Generator agents submit supply bids $(P^{gen}_i, c_i)$ (quantity, cost), and consumer agents submit demand bids $(P^{con}_j, v_j)$ (quantity, valuation). The market-clearing price $\lambda^*$ satisfies:

        $$\sum_i P_i^{gen}(\lambda^*) = \sum_j P_j^{con}(\lambda^*)$$

        1.3.3: Healthcare Systems

        MAS in healthcare coordinates among patient agents, doctor agents, hospital resource agents, and insurance agents.

        Patient Scheduling Optimization

        Given $n$ patients with arrival rates $\lambda_i$ and $m$ doctors with service rates $\mu_j$, the optimal assignment minimizes total expected waiting time:

        $$\min_{\text{assignment}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$

        Where $W_i$ is the expected wait for patient $i$. Using queuing theory (M/M/c queue):

        $$W = \frac{\lambda C(c, \rho)}{c\mu(1-\rho)} + \frac{1}{\mu}$$

        Where $\rho = \lambda / (c\mu)$ is the traffic intensity and $C(c, \rho)$ is the Erlang C formula.

        Epidemic Control with MAS

        Each person is an agent with health state $s_i \in \{S, I, R\}$ (Susceptible, Infected, Recovered). The SIR model:

        $$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I / N$$

        $$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I / N - \gamma I$$

        $$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$$

        Where $\beta$ = transmission rate, $\gamma$ = recovery rate, $N$ = population. The basic reproduction number:

        $$R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma}$$

        If $R_0 > 1$: epidemic spreads. MAS interventions (vaccination, quarantine agents) aim to reduce effective $R_0 < 1$.

        1.3.4: E-commerce and Trading

        Automated trading agents in financial markets are sophisticated MAS applications.

        Automated Trading Agent

        An agent submits orders based on its beliefs about future prices. A simple mean-reversion agent:

        $$\text{Buy signal}: P_t < \bar{P} - k\sigma$$

        $$\text{Sell signal}: P_t > \bar{P} + k\sigma$$

        Where $\bar{P}$ = moving average, $\sigma$ = standard deviation, $k$ = sensitivity parameter.

        Market Impact Model

        An agent trading large volumes impacts the market price. Almgren-Chriss model:

        $$P_{t+1} = P_t + \sigma \sqrt{\Delta t} \cdot \epsilon_t - \eta \cdot v_t$$

        Where:

        • $\sigma$ = price volatility
        • $\epsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ = random price shock
        • $\eta$ = market impact coefficient
        • $v_t$ = trading rate (shares/time)

        The agent must balance: trade faster (to avoid price drift) vs. trade slower (to minimize market impact).

        Double Auction Market

        In a double auction, buyers and sellers submit bids $b_j$ and asks $a_i$. The auctioneer matches:

        $$\text{Trade occurs} \iff \exists i, j : b_j \geq a_i$$

        Clearing price: $p^* = \frac{b_j + a_i}{2}$ or by dominant-side pricing.

        Equilibrium: In a competitive double auction with many buyers and sellers, the price converges to the Walrasian equilibrium $p^{eq}$ where supply = demand.

        FAQs: 1.3

        Q1: Why is MAS particularly suitable for smart grid management rather than a centralized controller?

        A: Smart grids have thousands to millions of heterogeneous participants (households, businesses, generators, storage). A centralized controller would face: (1) Communication bottleneck, collecting data from millions of meters in real time is infeasible; (2) Computation bottleneck, solving a million-variable optimization in real time is impractical; (3) Privacy issues, households may not share detailed consumption data; (4) Reliability, central failure = grid failure. MAS handles this naturally: each agent makes local decisions based on price signals, and the market mechanism coordinates globally without central control.

        Q2: In epidemic modeling, how does MAS improve upon the standard SIR model?

        A: The standard SIR model assumes a homogeneous, well-mixed population, everyone interacts with everyone equally. Agent-based MAS models allow: (1) Network structure, infections only spread along social contact networks; (2) Heterogeneous behavior, different agents have different compliance with interventions; (3) Spatial modeling, geographic location matters; (4) Dynamic responses, agents change behavior based on perceived risk. This provides much more accurate predictions for real epidemics, especially for targeted intervention strategies.

        Q3: What is the risk of a high-frequency trading MAS in financial markets?

        A: High-frequency trading MAS can cause flash crashes, rapid cascading market failures where agents react to each other's trades in feedback loops. On May 6, 2010, the Dow Jones dropped 1000 points in minutes due to HFT interactions. The risk arises from positive feedback loops: falling prices trigger sell algorithms, which cause more falling prices. This is a systemic risk property of MAS, local rational behavior can create globally catastrophic outcomes. Modern exchanges implement circuit breakers (automatic trading halts) as coordination mechanisms to break these feedback loops.

        Chapter 2: Types of Agents

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        By the end of this chapter, you will:

        • Understand the full spectrum of agent types from reactive to learning
        • Formally define rule-based, deliberative, hybrid, and mobile agents
        • Apply mathematical models to describe each agent type
        • Analyze the trade-offs between different agent architectures

        2.1: Reactive Agents

        Reactive agents are the simplest class. They respond directly to environmental stimuli without internal state or planning.

        2.1.1: Rule-Based Systems

        A rule-based reactive agent uses a set of condition-action rules:

        $$\mathcal{R} = \{(c_1, a_1), (c_2, a_2), \ldots, (c_k, a_k)\}$$

        Each rule $(c_i, a_i)$ means: IF condition $c_i$ holds in the environment, THEN perform action $a_i$.

        Formal Semantics

        Let $e \in E$ be the current environment state. The agent selects the action corresponding to the first (or highest priority) matching rule:

        $$a = a_i \text{ where } i = \min\{j : c_j(e) = \text{True}\}$$

        This is called a priority-ordered rule system. The agent function is:

        $$f(e) = a_i \text{ where } i = \arg\min_{j : c_j(e)} j$$

        Production System Architecture

        A production system has three components:

        1. Working Memory (WM): Current facts about the world

          2. $$WM = \{\text{fact}_1, \text{fact}_2, \ldots, \text{fact}_m\}$$

          1. Rule Base (RB): Set of if-then rules

            2. $$RB = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_k\}$$

            1. Inference Engine: Matches rules to facts, selects and fires rules

              2. Rete Algorithm (complexity): Naive matching is $O(k \cdot m)$ per cycle. The Rete algorithm reduces this to $O(\text{changes in WM})$ using a discrimination network.

              Forward Chaining

              Starting from current facts, derive new facts by firing rules:

              $$WM_{t+1} = WM_t \cup \{a_i : c_i(WM_t) = \text{True}\}$$

              Repeat until no new facts can be derived (fixed point).

              Fixed Point Theorem: Forward chaining converges in finite time if the rule set is acyclic:

              $$WM^* = \lim_{t \to \infty} WM_t$$

              Example: Robot Navigation Rules

              PriorityConditionAction
              1Obstacle ahead within 0.5mTurn right 90°
              2Goal to the leftTurn left 15°
              3Goal to the rightTurn right 15°
              4AlwaysMove forward

              The agent checks rules in priority order and executes the first matching rule.

              FAQs: 2.1.1

              Q1: What is the conflict resolution problem in rule-based systems?

              A: When multiple rules have their conditions satisfied simultaneously, the system must choose which rule to fire. This is the conflict resolution problem. Common strategies: (1) Priority, fire the highest priority rule; (2) Specificity, fire the most specific (most conditions) rule; (3) Recency, fire the rule that matches the most recently added facts; (4) First match, fire the first rule in the list. Different strategies lead to different behaviors and must be chosen carefully for the domain.

              Q2: What is the time complexity of matching $k$ rules against $m$ facts naively, and how does Rete improve this?

              A: Naive matching checks each rule against all facts: $O(k \cdot m^p)$ where $p$ is the maximum number of conditions per rule. For rules with multiple conditions, this is polynomial in $m$ and can be very slow. The Rete algorithm builds a discrimination network that indexes facts by their properties. It updates incrementally, only re-checks rules affected by the most recently changed facts. This reduces the per-cycle cost to $O(|\Delta WM|)$ (proportional to changes), making it much faster for systems where facts change slowly.

        2.1.2: Event-Driven Behavior

        Event-driven agents respond to discrete events rather than continuously polling the environment.

        Event-Condition-Action (ECA) Rules

        ECA rules extend basic if-then rules with an event trigger:

        $$\text{ECA Rule} = (\text{Event}, \text{Condition}, \text{Action})$$

        $$\text{ON Event } e : \text{IF Condition } c : \text{DO Action } a$$

        The agent is dormant until event $e$ occurs, then evaluates condition $c$, and fires action $a$ if $c$ is true.

        Formal Event Processing

        Let $\mathcal{E}_{events}$ be the event stream:

        $$\mathcal{E}_{events} = \langle e_1, t_1 \rangle, \langle e_2, t_2 \rangle, \ldots$$

        Where $e_i$ is the event type and $t_i$ is the timestamp.

        Event correlation: Complex events are composed of simple events. Example:

        $$E_{complex} = E_A \text{ followed by } E_B \text{ within } \Delta t$$

        $$P(E_{complex}) = P(E_A) \cdot P(E_B | E_A) \cdot \mathbf{1}[t_B - t_A \leq \Delta t]$$

        Complex Event Processing (CEP)

        CEP operators allow sophisticated event pattern matching:

        OperatorNotationMeaning
        Sequence$E_A \rightarrow E_B$$E_A$ before $E_B$
        Conjunction$E_A \wedge E_B$Both occur
        Disjunction$E_A \vee E_B$Either occurs
        Negation$\neg E_A$$E_A$ does not occur
        Window$E_A[W_t]$$E_A$ within time window $W_t$

        Response Time Constraints

        For hard real-time event-driven agents, the response must complete within deadline $D$:

        $$t_{response} - t_{event} \leq D$$

        Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) analysis ensures:

        $$WCET(f_{agent}) \leq D$$

        2.1.3: Advantages and Limitations of Reactive Agents

        Advantages

        AdvantageExplanationFormal Basis
        SpeedNo planning = fast response$t_{response} = O(1)$ rule lookup
        SimplicityEasy to design and verifyFinite rule set $|\mathcal{R}| < \infty$
        RobustnessNo internal state to corruptNo state = no state corruption
        NaturalnessMimics biological reflexStimulus → response

        Limitations

        LimitationExplanationFormal Basis
        No MemoryCannot learn from past$f(e_t)$, not $f(e_1, \ldots, e_t)$
        No PlanningCannot reason about futureNo model $T$ used
        Rule ExplosionComplex domains need many rules$|\mathcal{R}| = O(|E|)$
        No LearningFixed behavior$\mathcal{R}$ is static

        The Frame Problem: Reactive agents struggle with the frame problem, which facts remain unchanged after an action? Without explicit representation of persistence, the agent must re-verify all facts after each action.

        FAQs: 2.1.3

        Q1: For a task requiring 1000 rules, what is the risk of a rule-based reactive agent?

        A: The primary risks are: (1) Rule conflicts, with 1000 rules, the probability of conflicting conditions grows combinatorially; (2) Coverage gaps, it is difficult to ensure all situations are covered; (3) Maintenance, adding a new rule may unexpectedly interact with existing rules (rule interactions scale as $O(k^2)$); (4) Brittleness, a situation not anticipated by any rule leaves the agent without a response. The designer must exhaustively enumerate scenarios, which is impractical for complex environments.

        2.2: Deliberative Agents

        Deliberative agents maintain an internal model of the world and use it for planning and reasoning about future states.

        2.2.1: Goal-Oriented Agents

        A goal-oriented (deliberative) agent has:

        • An internal model of the world (beliefs)
        • A set of goals it wishes to achieve
        • A planning mechanism to generate action sequences

        Formal Definition

        $$\mathcal{A}_{deliberative} = \langle B, G, P, f \rangle$$

        Where:

        • $B$ = beliefs (agent's model of the world state)
        • $G$ = goals (desired world states)
        • $P$ = plans (sequences of actions)
        • $f$ = planning function that generates plans from beliefs and goals

        Belief-Goal-Plan Cycle

        The deliberative cycle:

        $$B_t \xrightarrow{\text{perceive}} B_{t+1} \xrightarrow{\text{plan}} P_{t+1} \xrightarrow{\text{execute}} a_{t+1}$$

        1. Update beliefs: $B_{t+1} = \text{update}(B_t, o_t)$
        2. Generate plan: $P = \text{plan}(B_{t+1}, G)$
        3. Execute first action: $a = P[0]$

          4. Goal Types

          Goal TypeNotationMeaning
          Achievement$\Diamond g$Eventually reach state $g$
          Maintenance$\square g$Always maintain state $g$
          Query$? \phi$Find value satisfying $\phi$
          Avoidance$\square \neg g$Never reach state $g$

          Using temporal logic notation: $\Diamond$ = eventually, $\square$ = always.

          Goal Representation

          Goals are represented as states or state conditions:

          $$G = \{\phi_1, \phi_2, \ldots, \phi_k\}$$

          Where each $\phi_i$ is a predicate over world states. A state $s$ satisfies goal $g$ if:

          $$s \models g \iff g(s) = \text{True}$$

          FAQs: 2.2.1

          Q1: How does an achievement goal differ from a maintenance goal?

          A: An achievement goal $\Diamond g$ requires the agent to eventually reach state $g$, it's a one-time accomplishment. Once achieved, the goal is dropped. A maintenance goal $\square g$ requires the agent to continuously keep the world in state $g$ at all times, it must be perpetually monitored and defended. A robot's goal to "reach the charging station" is an achievement goal; a robot's goal to "always have battery level > 20%" is a maintenance goal that requires ongoing action.

        2.2.2: Planning Mechanisms

        Planning is the process of finding a sequence of actions that transforms the current state into a goal state.

        Classical Planning Problem

        A classical planning problem is a tuple:

        $$\Pi = \langle S, A, s_0, G, T \rangle$$

        Where:

        • $S$ = state space
        • $A$ = action space with preconditions and effects
        • $s_0 \in S$ = initial state
        • $G \subseteq S$ = goal states
        • $T : S \times A \rightarrow S$ = deterministic transition function

        A plan is a sequence of actions $\pi = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k)$ such that:

        $$T(T(\ldots T(T(s_0, a_1), a_2) \ldots), a_k) \in G$$

        STRIPS Planning

        STRIPS (STanford Research Institute Problem Solver) is the foundational planning language.

        Action representation:

        $$\text{Action}(a) = \langle \text{PRE}(a), \text{ADD}(a), \text{DEL}(a) \rangle$$

        • $\text{PRE}(a)$: Preconditions, must be true before $a$ is applicable
        • $\text{ADD}(a)$: Add effects, facts made true by $a$
        • $\text{DEL}(a)$: Delete effects, facts made false by $a$

        State transition:

        $$s' = (s \setminus \text{DEL}(a)) \cup \text{ADD}(a) \text{ if } \text{PRE}(a) \subseteq s$$

        Example, Move-Robot action:

        $$\text{Move}(\text{robot, loc1, loc2}) = \langle \{\text{at(robot, loc1)}, \text{connected(loc1, loc2)}\}, \{\text{at(robot, loc2)}\}, \{\text{at(robot, loc1)}\} \rangle$$

        Forward Search (Progression Planning)

        Start from $s_0$, apply actions, search for $s \in G$:

        $$\text{Frontier} = \{s_0\}$$

        $$s' = T(s, a) \text{ for each } s \in \text{Frontier}, a \in A, \text{PRE}(a) \subseteq s$$

        Complexity: The state space has $|S| = 2^{|\text{facts}|}$ states (exponential in the number of facts). Planning is PSPACE-complete in general.

        Backward Search (Regression Planning)

        Start from $G$, work backward to $s_0$:

        $$G_0 = G$$

        $$G_{t+1} = \text{regress}(G_t, a) = (G_t \setminus \text{ADD}(a)) \cup \text{PRE}(a) \text{ if } G_t \cap \text{DEL}(a) = \emptyset$$

        Heuristic Search: A* Algorithm

        A* uses a heuristic $h(s)$ to guide search toward the goal efficiently.

        Evaluation function:

        $$f(s) = g(s) + h(s)$$

        Where:

        • $g(s)$ = cost from start to $s$ (actual cost so far)
        • $h(s)$ = estimated cost from $s$ to goal (heuristic)
        • $f(s)$ = estimated total cost through $s$

        Admissibility condition: A heuristic is admissible if it never overestimates:

        $$\forall s : h(s) \leq h^*(s)$$

        Where $h^*(s)$ is the true minimum cost from $s$ to goal.

        A Optimality Theorem: If $h$ is admissible, A finds the optimal solution. The time complexity of A* depends on the heuristic quality:

        $$T_{A^*} = O(b^{\epsilon d})$$

        Where $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = solution depth, and $\epsilon = 1 - \frac{h}{h^*}$ (error ratio).

        Planning as Satisfiability (SATPlan)

        Planning can be encoded as a SAT problem. For a plan of length $k$:

        Variables: $a_{t,j}$ = "action $j$ is taken at step $t$", $s_{t,i}$ = "fact $i$ is true at time $t$"

        Clauses:

        • Initial state: $s_{0,i}$ for each $i \in s_0$
        • Goal: $s_{k,i}$ for each $i \in G$
        • Action semantics: $a_{t,j} \Rightarrow s_{t+1, \text{ADD}(j)}$, $a_{t,j} \Rightarrow \neg s_{t+1, \text{DEL}(j)}$
        • Frame axioms: $s_{t+1,i} \Leftrightarrow s_{t,i} \vee \exists j: (a_{t,j} \wedge i \in \text{ADD}(j))$

        FAQs: 2.2.2

        Q1: Why is planning PSPACE-complete and what does this mean practically?

        A: Planning is PSPACE-complete because the state space is exponential ($2^n$ states for $n$ facts) and the plan length can also be exponential. Practically, this means: for large problems, finding an optimal plan is computationally intractable. Practitioners use: (1) Heuristic search (A*, greedy best-first) that sacrifices optimality guarantee for speed; (2) Plan decomposition, break the goal into subgoals and solve independently; (3) Partial-order planning, don't commit to a total order of actions; (4) Hierarchical task networks, abstract planning at multiple levels of detail.

        Q2: Explain the difference between forward and backward search in planning. When is each preferred?

        A: Forward search (progression): starts at $s_0$, applies actions to generate reachable states, seeks $G$. Best when the initial state is small and specific, and there are many goal states. Backward search (regression): starts at $G$, applies inverse actions to generate predecessors, seeks $s_0$. Best when the goal is small and specific, and there are many initial states. In practice, forward search with a goal-directed heuristic (like FF's relaxed plan heuristic) tends to outperform backward search for most benchmark planning domains.

        2.2.3: Decision Making

        Decision making under uncertainty is the core challenge for deliberative agents operating in stochastic environments.

        Markov Decision Process (MDP)

        The standard model for sequential decision making under uncertainty:

        $$\mathcal{M} = \langle S, A, T, R, \gamma \rangle$$

        Where:

        • $S$ = state space
        • $A$ = action space
        • $T(s' | s, a) = P(s_{t+1} = s' | s_t = s, a_t = a)$ = transition probabilities
        • $R(s, a)$ = immediate reward for taking action $a$ in state $s$
        • $\gamma \in [0,1)$ = discount factor (future rewards are worth less than immediate rewards)

        Markov Property:

        $$P(s_{t+1} | s_t, a_t, s_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, \ldots) = P(s_{t+1} | s_t, a_t)$$

        The future depends only on the current state and action, not the full history.

        Value Function

        The value function $V^\pi(s)$ of policy $\pi$ is the expected discounted cumulative reward starting from state $s$:

        $$V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}^\pi \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t) \bigg| s_0 = s \right]$$

        Bellman Equations

        The Bellman optimality equation defines the optimal value function $V^*(s)$:

        $$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s' \in S} T(s' | s, a) V^*(s') \right]$$

        This is a system of $|S|$ equations in $|S|$ unknowns. It can be solved iteratively.

        Value Iteration Algorithm

        Initialize $V_0(s) = 0$ for all $s$. Iterate:

        $$V_{k+1}(s) = \max_{a \in A} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s' | s, a) V_k(s') \right]$$

        Convergence: Value iteration converges geometrically:

        $$\|V_{k+1} - V^*\|_\infty \leq \gamma \|V_k - V^*\|_\infty$$

        After $k$ iterations: $\|V_k - V^*\|_\infty \leq \gamma^k \|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$.

        Optimal Policy

        Given $V^*$, the optimal policy is:

        $$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in A} \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s'|s,a) V^*(s') \right]$$

        Q-Value Function

        The Q-function (action-value function) $Q^*(s,a)$:

        $$Q^*(s,a) = R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s'|s,a) \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')$$

        Relation to value function: $V^*(s) = \max_a Q^*(s,a)$

        Optimal policy: $\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_a Q^*(s,a)$

        FAQs: 2.2.3

        Q1: Why do we discount future rewards in MDPs? What is the effect of $\gamma \to 1$ vs $\gamma \to 0$?

        A: Discounting serves several purposes: (1) Mathematical convergence, without discounting, infinite-horizon sums may not converge; (2) Time preference, rewards now are worth more than rewards later; (3) Uncertainty modeling, there is always a chance the agent might not survive to collect future rewards. As $\gamma \to 0$: the agent becomes myopic, only caring about immediate reward. As $\gamma \to 1$: the agent becomes far-sighted, equally valuing near and distant future rewards. High $\gamma$ requires longer planning horizons and is computationally more expensive.

        Q2: How many iterations does value iteration need to achieve error $\epsilon$?

        A: From the convergence bound $\|V_k - V^*\|_\infty \leq \gamma^k \|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty$, we need $\gamma^k \|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty \leq \epsilon$. Solving: $k \geq \frac{\log(\|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty / \epsilon)}{-\log \gamma} = \frac{\log(\|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty / \epsilon)}{\log(1/\gamma)}$. For $\gamma = 0.9$, $\|V_0 - V^*\|_\infty = 100$, $\epsilon = 0.01$: $k \geq \frac{\log(10000)}{\log(10/9)} = \frac{9.21}{0.105} \approx 88$ iterations.

        2.3: Hybrid and Learning Agents

        2.3.1: Hybrid Architectures

        Hybrid agents combine reactive and deliberative components to get the best of both worlds: fast response to immediate stimuli AND long-term planning.

        Three-Layer Hybrid Architecture

        The most common hybrid architecture has three layers:

        $$\mathcal{A}_{hybrid} = \langle L_{reactive}, L_{planning}, L_{strategic} \rangle$$

        LayerNameFunctionResponse Time
        $L_1$ReactiveImmediate stimulus-responseMilliseconds
        $L_2$DeliberativeShort-term planningSeconds
        $L_3$StrategicLong-term goal managementMinutes/Hours

        Priority: Lower layers have priority over higher layers. If the reactive layer detects imminent danger, it overrides the deliberative layer's plan.

        InteRRaP Architecture

        A well-known three-layer architecture:

        Layer 1 (World Interface): Reactive behavior layer

        $$a = \text{reactive_rules}(\text{percept})$$

        Layer 2 (Local Planning): Plans for local goal achievement

        $$\pi = \text{plan}(B_{local}, G_{local})$$

        Layer 3 (Cooperative Planning): Plans involving other agents

        $$\pi_{coop} = \text{plan}(B_{social}, G_{social})$$

        Control Flow in Hybrid Agents

        Bottom-up escalation: If $L_i$ cannot handle the situation, escalate to $L_{i+1}$:

        $$\text{If } \neg \text{handled}(L_1, e) \Rightarrow \text{invoke}(L_2, e)$$

        $$\text{If } \neg \text{handled}(L_2, e) \Rightarrow \text{invoke}(L_3, e)$$

        Top-down goal delegation: $L_3$ sets goals for $L_2$, which sets goals for $L_1$:

        $$G_{L_1} = \text{delegate}(L_2, G_{L_2})$$

        Performance Trade-off Analysis

        Let $\alpha$ = fraction of time spent in reactive mode, $1-\alpha$ = deliberative mode.

        • Reactive Performance: $P_r(\alpha) = \alpha \cdot Q_r$ (fast but low quality)
        • Deliberative Performance: $P_d(\alpha) = (1-\alpha) \cdot Q_d$ (slow but high quality)
        • Hybrid Performance: $P_h = \max(P_r(\alpha), P_d(\alpha)) + \text{synergy term}$

        Optimal $\alpha^*$ depends on environment dynamism $\delta$:

        $$\alpha^* = \frac{\delta}{\delta + c} \text{ where } c = \text{deliberation cost}$$

        2.3.2: Learning Agents

        Learning agents improve their performance through experience by modifying their agent function.

        Architecture of a Learning Agent

        $$\mathcal{A}_{learning} = \langle \text{Performance Element}, \text{Learning Element}, \text{Critic}, \text{Problem Generator} \rangle$$

        • Performance Element: The current behavior function $f_t$
        • Learning Element: Updates $f_t$ based on feedback to produce $f_{t+1}$
        • Critic: Evaluates performance against the performance standard
        • Problem Generator: Suggests exploratory actions to gather experience

        Reinforcement Learning Framework

        The agent learns from scalar reward signals:

        $$\text{Goal: find } \pi^* = \arg\max_\pi \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=0}^\infty \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1}) \bigg| \pi\right]$$

        Q-Learning Algorithm

        Q-Learning is an off-policy temporal difference method:

        $$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

        Where:

        • $\alpha \in (0,1]$ = learning rate
        • $r_{t+1}$ = received reward
        • $\gamma$ = discount factor
        • The bracketed term = temporal difference (TD) error $\delta_t$

        $$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

        Convergence Theorem: Q-learning converges to $Q^*$ if:

        1. Every state-action pair is visited infinitely often
        2. Learning rates satisfy: $\sum_t \alpha_t = \infty$ and $\sum_t \alpha_t^2 < \infty$

          3. Exploration vs. Exploitation Dilemma

          The agent must balance:

          • Exploitation: Use current knowledge to maximize reward
          • Exploration: Try new actions to discover better strategies

          $\epsilon$-greedy policy:

          $$a_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q(s_t, a) & \text{with probability } 1-\epsilon \\ \text{random action} & \text{with probability } \epsilon \end{cases}$$

          UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) policy:

          $$a_t = \arg\max_a \left[Q(s_t, a) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}}\right]$$

          Where $N_t(a)$ = number of times action $a$ has been taken. The $\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ term is an exploration bonus for rarely-tried actions.

          Regret Analysis

          Regret measures the cost of learning vs. always playing optimally:

          $$\text{Regret}(T) = T \cdot V^* - \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=1}^T R(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

          For UCB, regret grows logarithmically: $\text{Regret}(T) = O(\sqrt{T \ln T})$, which is near-optimal.

          FAQs: 2.3.2

          Q1: Why is the exploration-exploitation trade-off fundamental to learning agents?

          A: Without exploration, a learning agent may permanently commit to a suboptimal strategy that happened to work initially. Without exploitation, the agent never uses what it learned and performs poorly. The trade-off is fundamental because: (a) the agent cannot simultaneously maximize reward and gather information about unexplored actions; (b) the value of exploration is delayed, the agent pays a short-term cost for long-term gain. The optimal balance depends on: time horizon (finite vs. infinite), uncertainty in the environment, and the magnitude of potential improvements from unexplored actions.

          Q2: Prove that the TD error $\delta_t$ is zero when $Q = Q^*$.

          A: When $Q = Q^*$, the Bellman optimality equation holds: $Q^*(s,a) = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s',a')$ in expectation. So the TD error $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1},a') - Q^*(s_t,a_t) = Q^*(s_t,a_t) - Q^*(s_t,a_t) = 0$ in expectation. This confirms that $Q^*$ is a fixed point of the Q-learning update, learning stops when the optimal Q-function is found.

        2.3.3: Adaptive Behavior

        Adaptive agents modify not just their Q-values or policies, but their fundamental behavior patterns, world models, or even their own architectures.

        Types of Adaptation

        Adaptation LevelWhat ChangesExample
        ParameterNumeric weightsQ-values, neural network weights
        StructureRules or plansAdding new rules
        GoalObjectivesReprioritizing goals
        Meta-levelLearning strategyChanging learning rate

        Model-Based Reinforcement Learning

        Instead of just learning $Q$-values, model-based agents learn the environment model $(T, R)$:

        $$\hat{T}(s' | s, a) = \frac{\text{count}(s, a, s')}{\text{count}(s, a)}$$

        $$\hat{R}(s, a) = \text{mean reward when taking } a \text{ in } s$$

        Then plan using the learned model:

        $$\hat{V}^*(s) = \max_a \left[\hat{R}(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} \hat{T}(s'|s,a) \hat{V}^*(s')\right]$$

        Advantage: More data-efficient, can learn from simulated experience using the model.

        Dyna-Q Algorithm: Interleaves real and simulated experience:

        1. Take real action, observe $(s, a, r, s')$
        2. Update $\hat{T}$ and $\hat{R}$
        3. Run $n$ simulated steps using $\hat{T}$ and $\hat{R}$, update $Q$ from each

          4. Policy Gradient Methods

          Instead of learning $Q$, learn the policy $\pi_\theta(a|s)$ directly (parameterized by $\theta$):

          $$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$$

          Where $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\left[\sum_t \gamma^t R_t\right]$ is the expected return.

          Policy Gradient Theorem:

          $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}\left[\nabla_\theta \ln \pi_\theta(a|s) \cdot Q^{\pi_\theta}(s,a)\right]$$

          This is estimated from sampled trajectories (REINFORCE algorithm).

          FAQs: 2.3.3

          Q1: What is the advantage of model-based RL over model-free RL?

          A: Model-based RL can reuse past experience by planning with the learned model, a single real transition $(s, a, r, s')$ can be replayed many times in simulation to update the policy. This makes model-based methods far more sample efficient (need fewer real-world interactions). The downside is that an inaccurate model can introduce systematic errors ("model bias"), causing the agent to optimize for a wrong objective. Model-free methods like Q-learning are unbiased (they use real experience) but require many more samples. Dyna-Q combines both: real experience updates the model, and simulated experience from the model updates the policy.

        2.4: Mobile Agents

        2.4.1: Agent Mobility

        Mobile agents are software agents that can migrate from one host to another in a network, carrying their code, data, and execution state.

        Formal Definition of Mobility

        A mobile agent is a tuple:

        $$\mathcal{A}_{mobile} = \langle \text{Code}, \text{State}, \text{Itinerary}, \text{Current Host} \rangle$$

        • Code: The agent's program (executable)
        • State: All internal variables and memory at time of migration
        • Itinerary: The planned sequence of hosts to visit
        • Current Host: The network node where the agent currently executes

        Migration Decision

        An agent decides to migrate based on a cost-benefit analysis:

        $$\text{Migrate} \iff B_{migration} > C_{migration}$$

        Benefits:

        $$B_{migration} = \text{Computation Savings} + \text{Communication Savings} + \text{Latency Reduction}$$

        Costs:

        $$C_{migration} = \text{Migration Cost} + \text{Execution Overhead} + \text{Security Risk}$$

        Strong vs. Weak Mobility

        TypeCode Migrates?State Migrates?Execution Continues?
        Weak MobilityYesPartiallyRestarts at new host
        Strong MobilityYesFullyContinues at migration point

        Strong mobility requires capturing and transferring the complete execution state, including program counter, stack, and heap, a significant technical challenge.

        Itinerary Planning

        An agent with $k$ hosts to visit plans an optimal route:

        $$\text{Itinerary}^* = \arg\min_{\text{permutation}} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \text{cost}(h_{\pi(i)}, h_{\pi(i+1)})$$

        This is the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), which is NP-hard. Heuristic solutions (greedy nearest neighbor, genetic algorithms) are used in practice.

        2.4.2: Migration Mechanisms

        The migration process involves capturing, transmitting, and restoring an agent:

        $$\text{Migration} = \text{Capture}(\mathcal{A}) \rightarrow \text{Transmit}(\mathcal{A}) \rightarrow \text{Restore}(\mathcal{A})$$

        Migration Protocol Steps:

        1. Suspension: Agent execution is paused at source host
        2. State Capture: Agent state $\sigma = \langle code, data, execution_state \rangle$ is serialized
        3. Transmission: $\sigma$ is sent to destination host $h'$
        4. Verification: Destination verifies agent integrity
        5. Restoration: Agent state is deserialized and execution resumes

          6. Migration Time:

          $$t_{migration} = t_{suspend} + \frac{|\sigma|}{B} + t_{restore}$$

          Where $|\sigma|$ = state size in bits, $B$ = network bandwidth.

          Example: If agent state = 1 MB, network bandwidth = 100 Mbps:

          $$t_{transmission} = \frac{1 \times 10^6 \times 8}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.08 \text{ seconds}$$

          2.4.3: Security Concerns in Mobile Agents

          Mobile agents face unique security threats in both directions:

          Threat 1: Malicious Host (Host vs. Agent)

          A malicious host may:

          • Read/modify agent's internal state (privacy violation)
          • Terminate the agent prematurely
          • Return incorrect results (integrity violation)

          Protection: Cryptographic State Sealing

          The agent's state is encrypted before transmission:

          $$\sigma_{encrypted} = E_{K_{pub}}(\sigma)$$

          Only the agent (with $K_{priv}$) can decrypt. The host cannot read internal state.

          Partial Homomorphic Encryption: Allows the host to compute on encrypted data without decrypting, the agent can request computations without revealing its state.

          Threat 2: Malicious Agent (Agent vs. Host)

          A malicious agent may:

          • Consume excessive resources (denial of service)
          • Access unauthorized files
          • Exploit system vulnerabilities

          Protection:

          1. Sandboxing: Agent runs in isolated environment

            2. $$\text{Resources}(\mathcal{A}) \subseteq \text{Permitted Resources}(\mathcal{A})$$

            1. Resource Quotas: Limit CPU, memory, network:

              2. $$CPU_{agent} \leq CPU_{max}, \quad RAM_{agent} \leq RAM_{max}$$

              1. Digital Signatures: Each agent carries a signed certificate:

                2. $$\text{Verify}(\text{sig}, \text{owner_cert}) \rightarrow \{\text{valid}, \text{invalid}\}$$

                FAQs: 2.4

                Q1: Why is the mobile agent paradigm attractive for network management?

                A: Traditional centralized network management suffers from: (1) high communication overhead, all data must travel to a central manager; (2) scalability limits, the central manager becomes a bottleneck; (3) latency, decisions require round-trip communication. Mobile agents migrate to where the data is, process it locally, and return only the results. This dramatically reduces network traffic: instead of sending raw data $D$ of size $|D|$, the system sends the agent code (much smaller) and receives a summary. Communication savings ≈ $|D| - (| ext{code}| + | ext{result}|)$, which can be orders of magnitude.

                Q2: Why is strong mobility technically challenging?

                A: Strong mobility requires capturing the complete execution state, including: (1) Program counter, exactly where in the code execution is paused; (2) Call stack, all active function frames with their local variables; (3) Heap, all dynamically allocated memory with correct pointers; (4) Thread state, for multi-threaded agents; (5) Open resources, file handles, network connections (which may not be transferable). Most operating systems and virtual machines don't natively support this. Solutions include: using interpreted languages (like Java bytecode), custom virtual machines, or continuation-passing style compilation.

        Chapter 3: Agent Architectures

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Understand the fundamental principles guiding agent architecture design
        • Formally describe BDI, layered, blackboard, and subsumption architectures
        • Apply mathematical models to each architecture type
        • Compare and contrast architecture trade-offs

        3.1: Agent Design Principles

        3.1.1: Modularity

        Modularity is the principle of decomposing an agent into independent, self-contained modules.

        Formal Definition

        A modular agent is decomposed as:

        $$\mathcal{A} = M_1 \oplus M_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus M_k$$

        Where $\oplus$ denotes composition, and each module $M_i$ has:

        • Well-defined interface: $I_i = (\text{Inputs}_i, \text{Outputs}_i)$
        • Internal state: $\sigma_i$
        • Local function: $f_i : \text{Inputs}_i \times \sigma_i \rightarrow \text{Outputs}_i \times \sigma_i'$

        Coupling between modules $M_i$ and $M_j$:

        $$\text{Coupling}(i,j) = \frac{|\text{shared variables}(M_i, M_j)|}{|\text{variables}(M_i)| + |\text{variables}(M_j)|}$$

        Good design: $\text{Coupling}(i,j) \to 0$ (low coupling)

        Cohesion of module $M_i$:

        $$\text{Cohesion}(i) = \frac{|\text{related functions in } M_i|}{|\text{total functions in } M_i|}$$

        Good design: $\text{Cohesion}(i) \to 1$ (high cohesion)

        Benefits of Modularity

        BenefitFormula
        Testability$\text{Test Cost} = O(\sum_i |M_i|^2)$ vs. $O(|\mathcal{A}|^2)$
        Reusability$\text{Reuse} = $ fraction of $M_i$ used in other agents
        Maintainability$\text{Change propagation} = f(\text{Coupling})$

        3.1.2: Scalability

        An architecture is scalable if its performance degrades gracefully as the problem size increases.

        Formal Scalability Requirement

        Let $N$ = problem size (number of agents, tasks, etc.). An architecture is scalable if:

        $$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{T(N)}{N^c} < \infty \text{ for some small constant } c$$

        Ideally, $T(N) = O(N \log N)$ or $T(N) = O(N)$.

        Horizontal Scalability: Add more agents of the same type.

        $$\text{Throughput}(N) = N \cdot \text{Throughput}(1) - \text{Coordination Overhead}(N)$$

        Vertical Scalability: Increase individual agent capability.

        The Scalability Trade-off:

        $$\text{Global Optimality} \times \text{Scalability} \leq C$$

        You cannot have both perfect global optimization and unlimited scalability, there is a fundamental trade-off.

        3.1.3: Fault Tolerance

        A fault-tolerant architecture continues to function correctly even when some components fail.

        Fault Model

        A fault is modeled as a Bernoulli random variable:

        $$F_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_i), \quad F_i = 1 \text{ means module } i \text{ has failed}$$

        System reliability:

        $$R_{system} = P(\text{system functions}) = f(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_k)$$

        Series system (all components required):

        $$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{k} R_i = \prod_{i=1}^k (1-p_i)$$

        Parallel system (any one component sufficient):

        $$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{k} (1-R_i) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^k p_i$$

        k-of-n System (at least $k$ of $n$ modules must work):

        $$R_{k/n} = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} R^j (1-R)^{n-j}$$

        (assuming identical reliability $R$ for each module)

        Redundancy Strategies

        1. Active Redundancy: All redundant modules run simultaneously, take a majority vote
        2. Passive Redundancy: Standby modules activate on failure
        3. N-version Programming: $N$ independent implementations, majority vote

          4. Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):

          $$MTBF = \frac{\int_0^\infty R(t) dt}{n} = \frac{1}{n \lambda}$$

          Where $\lambda$ = individual module failure rate (exponential failure model).

          FAQs: 3.1

          Q1: A system has 5 modules, each with 99% reliability ($R_i = 0.99$). What is the system reliability for (a) series, (b) parallel?

          A:

          (a) Series: $R_{series} = 0.99^5 = 0.951$ - 95.1% reliable. Each failure causes system failure.

          (b) Parallel: $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-0.99)^5 = 1 - 0.01^5 = 1 - 10^{-10} \approx 100\%$, nearly perfect. This illustrates why redundancy is so powerful: 5 parallel modules with 99% each gives 99.9999999% system reliability.

        3.2: BDI Architecture

        The Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) architecture is the most influential formal model for deliberative agents, based on a philosophical analysis of practical reasoning.

        3.2.1: Beliefs

        Beliefs represent the agent's current knowledge about itself and the world.

        Formal Belief Model

        Using modal logic, agent $i$'s belief operator $B_i$:

        $$B_i \phi \text{ means "agent } i \text{ believes proposition } \phi \text{"}$$

        Axioms of Belief (KD45 logic):

        • K (Distribution): $B_i(\phi \Rightarrow \psi) \Rightarrow (B_i \phi \Rightarrow B_i \psi)$
        • D (Consistency): $B_i \phi \Rightarrow \neg B_i \neg \phi$ (no contradictory beliefs)
        • 4 (Positive Introspection): $B_i \phi \Rightarrow B_i B_i \phi$ (agent knows what it believes)
        • 5 (Negative Introspection): $\neg B_i \phi \Rightarrow B_i \neg B_i \phi$ (agent knows what it doesn't believe)

        Belief Representation

        Beliefs are stored in a belief base $\mathcal{BB}$:

        $$\mathcal{BB} = \{(\phi_1, conf_1), (\phi_2, conf_2), \ldots\}$$

        Where $\phi_i$ is a proposition and $conf_i \in [0,1]$ is the confidence in that belief.

        Belief Update (Bayesian):

        $$P(\phi | e) = \frac{P(e | \phi) P(\phi)}{P(e)}$$

        After observing evidence $e$, the agent updates its belief in proposition $\phi$.

        3.2.2: Desires

        Desires represent the agent's motivational state, what it wants to achieve or maintain.

        Desire Representation

        $$\mathcal{D} = \{(d_1, priority_1), (d_2, priority_2), \ldots\}$$

        Each desire $d_i$ has a priority $p_i \in \mathbb{R}$. A desire $d$ in state $s$:

        $$d(s) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if state } s \text{ satisfies desire } d \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

        Total Desire Satisfaction:

        $$\text{Satisfaction}(s) = \sum_i p_i \cdot d_i(s)$$

        Desire Inconsistency

        Desires can be mutually inconsistent:

        $$\text{Inconsistent}(d_i, d_j) \iff \neg \exists s : d_i(s) = 1 \wedge d_j(s) = 1$$

        The agent cannot simultaneously satisfy both. This requires desire prioritization or negotiation.

        3.2.3: Intentions

        Intentions are the desires the agent has committed to achieving. Not all desires become intentions, the agent selects achievable desires given its beliefs and resources.

        Intention Selection

        The agent filters desires into intentions:

        $$\mathcal{I} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$$

        Subject to:

        • Achievability: $B_i(\text{achievable}(d)) = \text{True}$ for all $d \in \mathcal{I}$
        • Consistency: Intentions are mutually consistent
        • Resource constraints: Sum of required resources $\leq$ available resources

        Selection function:

        $$\text{filter}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{I}_{current}) \rightarrow \mathcal{I}_{new}$$

        The BDI Reasoning Cycle

        The complete BDI agent execution cycle:

        1. Perceive: $\rho \leftarrow \text{sensor}(\mathcal{E})$
        2. Update beliefs: $\mathcal{B} \leftarrow \text{brf}(\mathcal{B}, \rho)$ (belief revision function)
        3. Generate options: $\mathcal{D} \leftarrow \text{options}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{I})$ (available desires)
        4. Filter options: $\mathcal{I} \leftarrow \text{filter}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{D}, \mathcal{I})$ (select intentions)
        5. Select action: $\pi \leftarrow \text{plan}(\mathcal{B}, \mathcal{I})$, execute $\pi[0]$

          6. The BDI loop:

          $$\mathcal{B}_0, \mathcal{D}_0, \mathcal{I}_0 \xrightarrow{\text{cycle}} \mathcal{B}_1, \mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{I}_1 \xrightarrow{\text{cycle}} \cdots$$
