          Commitment Strategies

          StrategyDescriptionWhen to drop intention
          Blind CommitmentStick with intention until achievedNever (unless achieved)
          Single-Minded CommitmentStick until achieved or impossibleWhen achieved OR when impossible
          Open-Minded CommitmentStick while desiredWhen no longer desired

          Rational Balance: Too much commitment = agent pursues obsolete goals; too little = agent never completes goals. The right balance is:

          $$\text{Reconsider intention} \iff \text{Cost of reconsideration} < \text{Value of reconsidering}$$

          $$\text{Value of reconsidering} = \mathbb{E}[U(\text{new plan})] - \mathbb{E}[U(\text{current plan})]$$

          FAQs: 3.2

          Q1: Why can't an agent just pursue all its desires as intentions?

          A: Three reasons: (1) Inconsistency, desires may conflict, so achieving one prevents achieving another; (2) Resource limits, the agent has finite time, energy, and computational resources; (3) Commitment overhead, maintaining an intention requires ongoing monitoring and plan execution. The filter function selects a consistent, achievable, resource-feasible subset of desires as intentions. If all desires were pursued simultaneously, the agent would either fail on conflicting goals or exhaust its resources.

          Q2: A BDI agent wants to: (1) deliver a package (urgent), (2) recharge battery (important), (3) explore new area (low priority). Battery is at 15%. What should it intend?

          A: The agent should reason: belief = "battery at 15% (critically low)"; desire (1) requires travel; desire (2) is urgent for survival; desire (3) is low priority. A rational BDI agent would filter: $\mathcal{I} = \{d_2 \text{ (recharge)}\}$ immediately, because without recharging, it cannot achieve $d_1$ and would risk complete failure. After recharging, it should reassess and likely intend $d_1$ next. This demonstrates how beliefs affect intention selection.

        3.3: Layered Architectures

        3.3.1: Horizontal Layering

        In a horizontally layered architecture, all layers have access to sensors and actuators. Each layer produces its own recommendation for action.

        Architecture Structure

        $$\mathcal{A}_{horizontal} = \langle L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n, \text{Mediator} \rangle$$

        All layers run in parallel, each receiving sensor input and producing action suggestions:

        $$a_i = L_i(\text{percept}) \text{ for all } i$$

        Mediator selects the final action:

        $$a_{final} = \text{Mediator}(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$$

        The mediator can use priority, voting, or utility maximization.

        Priority-Based Mediation

        $$a_{final} = a_i \text{ where } i = \arg\max_j \{\text{priority}(L_j) : a_j \neq \text{null}\}$$

        Utility-Based Mediation

        Each layer has an associated utility function $u_i(a)$. The mediator selects:

        $$a_{final} = \arg\max_a \sum_i w_i \cdot u_i(a)$$

        Where $w_i$ is the weight of layer $i$ (how much its preferences matter).

        Advantages and Disadvantages

        AspectHorizontalReason
        ParallelismHighAll layers run simultaneously
        ConflictHighLayers can recommend conflicting actions
        Complexity of mediatorHighMediator must reconcile conflicts
        ModularityHighEach layer independently sees the world

        3.3.2: Vertical Layering

        In a vertically layered architecture, layers are arranged hierarchically. Information flows up, control flows down. Only the lowest layer directly controls actuators.

        Single-Pass Vertical Architecture

        $$\text{Percept} \rightarrow L_1 \rightarrow L_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow L_n \rightarrow \text{Action}$$

        Each layer processes the output of the layer below it. The top layer produces the final action decision, which passes down to actuators.

        Two-Pass Vertical Architecture

        Upward pass (data): $L_1 \rightarrow L_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow L_n$

        Downward pass (control): $L_n \rightarrow L_{n-1} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow L_1$

        Data rises from reactive (low level) to strategic (high level). Commands descend from strategic to reactive.

        Data transformation at layer $i$:

        $$D_i = \text{abstract}(D_{i-1}) \quad \text{(upward, increasing abstraction)}$$

        Command refinement at layer $i$:

        $$C_{i-1} = \text{refine}(C_i) \quad \text{(downward, increasing specificity)}$$

        3.3.3: Hybrid Layering

        Hybrid layering combines horizontal and vertical elements:

        $$\mathcal{A}_{hybrid} = \text{Vertical}(L_1^{(reactive)}, L_2^{(deliberative)}) \oplus \text{Horizontal}(L_2, L_3^{(social)})$$

        The lower layers communicate vertically (sequential processing), while higher layers communicate horizontally (peer interactions for coordination).

        FAQs: 3.3

        Q1: Why is the mediator complex in horizontal layering but not needed in vertical layering?

        A: In horizontal layering, all layers see the environment and independently produce action recommendations. Since different layers have different objectives (reactive safety vs. deliberative efficiency), their recommendations often conflict. The mediator must resolve these conflicts, which requires knowledge of all layers and their priorities, a complex design problem. In vertical layering, the hierarchy eliminates the need for mediation: the highest layer makes the final decision, which is then successively refined by lower layers. There is a clear command chain, eliminating ambiguity.

        3.4: Blackboard and Subsumption Architectures

        3.4.1: Blackboard Systems

        The blackboard architecture uses a shared data structure (the blackboard) as the central coordination mechanism.

        Formal Structure

        $$\mathcal{BB_System} = \langle \text{Blackboard}, \{K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_n\}, \text{Control} \rangle$$

        • Blackboard ($\mathcal{B}$): Shared, globally visible data structure
        • Knowledge Sources ($K_i$): Specialist modules that can read/write the blackboard
        • Control: Scheduler that activates appropriate knowledge sources

        Blackboard Operation Protocol

        1. Opportunistic activation: $K_i$ activates when the blackboard contains data it can process
        2. Knowledge source fires: $K_i$ reads input from $\mathcal{B}$, processes it, writes results to $\mathcal{B}$
        3. State advances: New data may trigger additional knowledge sources

          4. Trigger condition for $K_i$:

          $$\text{triggered}(K_i) \iff \text{precondition}(K_i) \subseteq \mathcal{B}$$

          Effect of firing $K_i$:

          $$\mathcal{B}' = \mathcal{B} \cup \text{effects}(K_i) \setminus \text{consumed}(K_i)$$

          Control Strategies

          StrategyMethod
          Focus of AttentionSelect $K_i$ whose trigger best matches current $\mathcal{B}$
          PriorityFixed priority ordering among $K_i$
          UtilitySelect $K_i$ with highest expected contribution

          Utility-based selection:

          $$K^* = \arg\max_{K_i : \text{triggered}(K_i)} U(K_i, \mathcal{B}) = \arg\max_{K_i} \mathbb{E}[\Delta\text{progress} | K_i]$$

          3.4.2: Subsumption Architecture

          The subsumption architecture (Brooks, 1986) is a purely reactive, layered architecture where higher layers subsume (augment and suppress) lower layers.

          Architecture Definition

          Behaviors are organized in priority layers $L_0, L_1, \ldots, L_n$ where $L_0$ is the lowest (most primitive) layer:

          • $L_0$: Avoid obstacles (survival)
          • $L_1$: Wander randomly (exploration)
          • $L_2$: Explore specific areas (goal-seeking)
          • $L_3$: Map building
          • ...

          Subsumption Mechanism

          Higher layers use two mechanisms to override lower layers:

          Suppression ($\nearrow$): Layer $L_i$ suppresses the input of layer $L_j$ (where $j < i$):

          $$\text{If } L_i \text{ sends suppression signal} \Rightarrow \text{Input to } L_j = L_i\text{'s output}$$

          Inhibition ($\searrow$): Layer $L_i$ inhibits the output of layer $L_j$:

          $$\text{If } L_i \text{ sends inhibition signal} \Rightarrow \text{Output of } L_j = 0 \text{ (blocked)}$$

          Formal behavior:

          $$\text{Output}(L_j) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if inhibited by higher layer} \\ L_i\text{'s signal} & \text{if suppressed by higher layer} \\ L_j\text{'s computation} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

          Augmented Finite State Machine (AFSM)

          Each behavior is implemented as an AFSM:

          $$AFSM = \langle Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F \rangle$$

          • $Q$ = states
          • $\Sigma$ = input alphabet (sensor signals + suppression signals)
          • $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ = transition function
          • $q_0$ = initial state
          • $F$ = output function

          3.4.3: Comparative Analysis

          DimensionBlackboardSubsumptionBDI
          Knowledge SharingCentral (blackboard)NoneVia beliefs
          CoordinationExplicit (control)EmergentExplicit (filter)
          ReactivityModerateHighLow-Moderate
          DeliberationHighNoneHigh
          ScalabilityModerateHighLow-Moderate
          TransparencyHighLowHigh
          Best ForComplex problem solvingReal-time roboticsGoal-driven agents
          Example SystemsHEARSAY-II (speech)Mobile robotsJASON, AgentSpeak

          FAQs: 3.4

          Q1: Why does the subsumption architecture achieve real-time performance that BDI struggles with?

          A: Subsumption is purely reactive, each behavior layer computes output in $O(1)$ time based only on current sensor input. There is no planning, search, or reasoning over future states. BDI requires belief update, desire generation, option filtering, and planning, all of which take non-constant time (planning is PSPACE-complete in the worst case). Subsumption achieves real-time performance by trading intelligence for speed: it cannot plan for complex scenarios, but it always responds within a bounded time.

          Q2: In a blackboard system solving a speech recognition problem, what would knowledge sources and the blackboard contain?

          A: This is based on the HEARSAY-II system. The blackboard has levels: Phoneme → Syllable → Word → Word-sequence → Phrase → Sentence. Knowledge sources include: (1) SEG, segments acoustic signal into phoneme hypotheses; (2) POM, identifies phoneme clusters; (3) MOW, locates word hypotheses from phonemes; (4) WORD, extends word hypotheses; (5) PARSE, checks grammatical consistency; (6) PREDICT, predicts likely next words from language model. Each KS reads the blackboard at its level and writes results at the next level up. The control decides which KS to fire based on confidence scores.

        Chapter 4: Environment Modeling

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Formally model agent environments using state-space representations
        • Classify environments along multiple dimensions
        • Apply mathematical tools (MDPs, POMDPs, belief states) to environment modeling
        • Design appropriate agents for specific environment types

        4.1: Agent Environments

        4.1.1: Static Environments

        A static environment does not change while the agent is deliberating.

        Formal Definition

        $$\mathcal{E}_{static}: \frac{\partial s}{\partial t} = 0 \text{ when no agent actions are taken}$$

        More precisely, for any time interval $[t_1, t_2]$ with no agent actions:

        $$s(t_1) = s(t_2)$$

        Markov Decision Process for Static Environments

        In a static environment, the MDP simplifies because the state only changes when the agent acts:

        $$s_{t+1} = T(s_t, a_t) \text{ (deterministic or stochastic)}$$

        The agent has unlimited time to deliberate before each action, the time dimension doesn't pressure the agent.

        Value of Deliberation in Static Environments:

        Since the environment waits, the agent can spend unlimited time planning:

        $$\text{Planning quality} \propto \text{time spent planning}$$

        Optimal planning (e.g., value iteration to convergence) is feasible.

        The "Dirtiness" Score: Static Example

        Consider a vacuum cleaner in a $2 \times 1$ room. State space:

        $$S = \{(\text{Left, Right, Dirt_status}) : \text{Dirt_status} \in \{\text{clean, dirty}\}^2\}$$

        $$|S| = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ states}$$

        Since the environment is static (dirt doesn't spontaneously appear), the agent can plan a complete cleaning sequence in advance.

        4.1.2: Dynamic Environments

        A dynamic environment changes independently of the agent's actions.

        Formal Definition

        $$\mathcal{E}_{dynamic}: \exists t_1 < t_2 \text{ (no agent action between them) s.t. } s(t_1) \neq s(t_2)$$

        The environment has its own dynamics function $D: S \rightarrow S$ that evolves the state:

        $$s(t + \Delta t) = D(s(t)) + \text{noise}$$

        Semi-Markov Decision Process (SMDP)

        When real time matters (dynamic environments), we use SMDPs:

        $$\mathcal{M}_{SM} = \langle S, A, T, R, F \rangle$$

        Where $F(\tau | s, a)$ = distribution over decision-making time $\tau$ while in state $s$ taking action $a$.

        SMDP Value Function:

        $$V^*(s) = \max_{a \in A} \mathbb{E}\left[\int_0^\tau e^{-\beta t} r(s, a, t) dt + e^{-\beta \tau} V^*(s') \bigg| s, a\right]$$

        Where $\beta$ = continuous-time discount rate.

        Real-Time Heuristic Search

        In dynamic environments, the agent must act within deadline $D$:

        $$\text{Best action within } D = \arg\max_{a \in \text{reachable within } D} f(a)$$

        Real-Time A (RTA) limits search depth to what's feasible within the deadline.

        4.1.3: Open and Closed Environments

        Closed Environment

        A closed environment has a fixed, known set of agents and resources:

        $$|\text{Agents}| = n = \text{constant}, |\text{Resources}| = R = \text{constant}$$

        All agents are known in advance. Planning can assume the full agent population.

        Open Environment

        An open environment allows agents to dynamically join and leave:

        $$n(t) = \text{variable}, \quad \frac{dn}{dt} \neq 0$$

        The churn rate $\chi$:

        $$\chi = \frac{\text{agents joining} + \text{agents leaving}}{\Delta t}$$

        High churn makes coordination difficult, the agent population is constantly changing.

        Challenge: Protocols designed for closed environments break in open ones. For example, auctions assume all bidders are present at the start, what if a new bidder arrives mid-auction?

        Open-Failure Modes:

        1. Freeriders: Agents benefit from the system without contributing
        2. Newcomers advantage: Late-joining agents benefit from others' work
        3. Byzantine agents: Malicious agents disrupt protocols

          4. FAQs: 4.1

          Q1: How does a dynamic environment affect the effectiveness of a pre-computed plan?

          A: A pre-computed plan assumes the environment will remain as expected during execution. In a dynamic environment, the state changes independently, assumptions made during planning may be violated by the time the agent executes the plan. For example, an agent planning a route assumes roads are clear; a traffic accident (dynamic change) invalidates the plan. The agent must replan, which requires: (1) Plan monitoring, continuously checking if the plan's assumptions still hold; (2) Replanning, generating a new plan when assumptions are violated; (3) Contingency planning, preparing alternative plans in advance. Dynamic environments favor reactive or anytime planning algorithms over pre-computed complete plans.

        4.2: Environment Properties

        4.2.1: Observable vs. Partially Observable

        This dimension determines how much information the agent can gather about the world state.

        Fully Observable Environment

        The agent's observation $o_t$ uniquely identifies the true state $s_t$:

        $$\exists \text{ bijection } f : O \rightarrow S \text{ s.t. } o_t = f(s_t)$$

        The agent effectively knows the true state: $P(s_t | o_t) \in \{0, 1\}$.

        Partially Observable Environment

        The agent cannot determine the true state from the observation alone:

        $$P(s | o) \notin \{0, 1\} \text{ for some } (s, o) \text{ pairs}$$

        The agent must maintain a belief state $b_t$, a probability distribution over possible states:

        $$b_t : S \rightarrow [0,1], \quad \sum_{s \in S} b_t(s) = 1$$

        Belief Update (Bayes Filter)

        Given belief $b_t$, action $a_t$, and observation $o_{t+1}$:

        Prediction step (action):

        $$b_{t+1}^-(s') = \sum_{s \in S} T(s' | s, a_t) \cdot b_t(s)$$

        Update step (observation):

        $$b_{t+1}(s') = \frac{\Omega(o_{t+1} | s', a_t) \cdot b_{t+1}^-(s')}{P(o_{t+1} | b_t, a_t)}$$

        Where $\Omega(o | s, a)$ = probability of observing $o$ from state $s$ after action $a$.

        Normalization:

        $$P(o_{t+1} | b_t, a_t) = \sum_{s'} \Omega(o_{t+1} | s', a_t) \cdot b_{t+1}^-(s')$$

        POMDP (Partially Observable MDP)

        A POMDP adds observations to the MDP:

        $$\text{POMDP} = \langle S, A, T, R, O, \Omega, \gamma \rangle$$

        The agent maintains belief state $b$ and optimizes the belief-MDP:

        $$V^*(b) = \max_{a} \left[ R(b, a) + \gamma \sum_{o \in O} P(o | b, a) V^*(b') \right]$$

        Where $b'$ = belief state after taking action $a$ and observing $o$.

        Computational complexity: Solving POMDPs exactly is PSPACE-complete (harder than MDPs). Approximate methods: PBVI (Point-Based Value Iteration), SARSOP, POMCP.

        4.2.2: Deterministic vs. Stochastic

        Deterministic Environment

        The next state is completely determined by current state and action:

        $$T(s' | s, a) = \mathbf{1}[s' = f(s, a)]$$

        Planning is a state search problem, no probabilities needed.

        Stochastic Environment

        The next state is probabilistic:

        $$T(s' | s, a) = P(s_{t+1} = s' | s_t = s, a_t = a)$$

        The full probability distribution must be specified:

        $$\sum_{s' \in S} T(s' | s, a) = 1 \quad \forall s \in S, a \in A$$

        Sources of stochasticity:

        1. Actuator noise: Actions don't always have intended effects

          2. $$a_{actual} = a_{intended} + \epsilon, \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_a)$$

          1. Environment noise: External forces affect state

            2. $$s_{t+1} = f(s_t, a_t) + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma_w)$$

            1. Other agents: Unpredictable actions of other agents

              2. Risk-Sensitive Decision Making

              In stochastic environments, agents may be risk-averse:

              $$U_{risk_averse}(x) = -e^{-\lambda x} \text{ (exponential utility)}$$

              Where $\lambda > 0$ = risk aversion coefficient. The certainty equivalent (CE) of a lottery:

              $$CE = \frac{-\ln(-\mathbb{E}[U(X)])}{\lambda} = \mu - \frac{\lambda \sigma^2}{2}$$

              Risk-averse agents prefer lower expected value with lower variance over higher expected value with higher variance.

              4.2.3: Episodic vs. Sequential

              Episodic Environment

              Each episode is an independent perception-action cycle:

              $$\text{Episode}_t = (o_t, a_t, r_t)$$

              Episodes are i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed):

              $$\text{Episode}_i \perp \text{Episode}_j \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$

              Optimal action depends only on current percept: $a^* = f(o_t)$.

              Sequential Environment

              Current actions affect future states:

              $$s_{t+1} = T(s_t, a_t)$$

              $$\text{Return} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_t$$

              The agent must plan over time horizons, a much harder problem.

              Key difference: In episodic environments, the agent can be trained offline (e.g., image classifiers with i.i.d. training examples). In sequential environments, the agent must reason about temporal dependencies and credit assignment.

              FAQs: 4.2

              Q1: What is the computational advantage of knowing an environment is deterministic?

              A: In a deterministic environment, the transition function maps each (state, action) pair to a unique next state, there are no probabilities to handle. This eliminates: (1) the need to maintain probability distributions over states; (2) the need to compute expected values (a sum over all possible next states); (3) the computational overhead of the Bellman equation's summation. The complexity of computing optimal policy drops from $O(|S|^2 |A|)$ per iteration (stochastic) to $O(|S| |A|)$ (deterministic). For large state spaces, this is a significant advantage.

              Q2: Derive the belief state update for a 2-state POMDP.

              A: States $S = \{s_1, s_2\}$. Belief $b_t = (p, 1-p)$ where $p = P(s_1)$. After action $a$ and observation $o$:

              Prediction: $b^-(s_1) = T(s_1|s_1,a) \cdot p + T(s_1|s_2,a) \cdot (1-p)$

              Update: $b_{t+1}(s_1) = \frac{\Omega(o|s_1,a) \cdot b^-(s_1)}{\Omega(o|s_1,a) \cdot b^-(s_1) + \Omega(o|s_2,a) \cdot b^-(s_2)}$

              This is exactly Bayes' theorem applied to a two-state mixture.

        4.3: State Representation

        4.3.1: State Space Models

        A state space model formally represents the environment as a graph of states connected by transitions.

        State Space Graph

        $$G = (S, E)$$

        Where:

        • $S$ = set of states (nodes)
        • $E = \{(s, s', a) : T(s'|s,a) > 0\}$ = set of transitions (edges labeled by actions)

        State space size:

        RepresentationState Space Size
        $n$ binary variables$2^n$
        $n$ integer variables (range $k$)$k^n$
        $n$ real-valued variables$\infty$ (continuous)
        Factored MDPProduct of factor sizes

        Factored State Representation

        Instead of enumerating states explicitly, represent states as assignments to state variables:

        $$s = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in X_1 \times X_2 \times \cdots \times X_n$$

        The transition function factorizes (Dynamic Bayesian Network):

        $$T(s' | s, a) = \prod_{i=1}^n P(x_i' | \text{parents}(x_i), a)$$

        This reduces the representation from $O(|S|^2 |A|)$ to $O(n \cdot |\text{parents}|^{max} \cdot |A|)$, exponential reduction.

        Reward Function Decomposition

        Similarly, rewards can be decomposed:

        $$R(s, a) = \sum_{i=1}^n R_i(x_i, a) \text{ (additive decomposition)}$$

        This allows efficient computation without enumerating all states.

        4.3.2: Environment Mapping

        Environment mapping is the process of building an internal representation of the environment from sensor data.

        Occupancy Grid Mapping

        The environment $\mathcal{E}$ is discretized into a grid:

        $$\mathcal{G} = \{c_{ij} : i \in [1,m], j \in [1,n]\}$$

        Each cell $c_{ij}$ has an occupancy probability:

        $$P(c_{ij} = \text{occupied}) \in [0,1]$$

        Using log-odds representation for numerical stability:

        $$l_{ij} = \log \frac{P(\text{occ})}{1 - P(\text{occ})}$$

        Bayesian update with sensor reading $z$:

        $$l_{ij}^{new} = l_{ij}^{old} + \log \frac{P(z | \text{occ})}{P(z | \text{free})} - \log \frac{P_{prior}(\text{occ})}{P_{prior}(\text{free})}$$

        Converting back to probability:

        $$P(\text{occ}) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{l_{ij}}}$$

        SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping)

        SLAM solves the chicken-and-egg problem:

        • Localization: Find the robot's position given the map
        • Mapping: Build the map given the robot's position

        SLAM posterior:

        $$P(x_{1:t}, m | z_{1:t}, u_{1:t})$$

        Where $x_{1:t}$ = trajectory, $m$ = map, $z_{1:t}$ = observations, $u_{1:t}$ = control inputs.

        EKF-SLAM (Extended Kalman Filter):

        State vector: $\mu_t = (x_t, y_t, \theta_t, l^1_x, l^1_y, \ldots, l^N_x, l^N_y)^T$

        $(x,y,\theta)$ = robot pose, $(l^k_x, l^k_y)$ = landmark positions.

        Uncertainty: $\Sigma_t$ = covariance matrix of size $(3+2N) \times (3+2N)$.

        EKF prediction step:

        $$\mu_t^- = g(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

        $$\Sigma_t^- = G_t \Sigma_{t-1} G_t^T + R_t$$

        EKF update step:

        $$K_t = \Sigma_t^- H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T + Q_t)^{-1}$$

        $$\mu_t = \mu_t^- + K_t(z_t - h(\mu_t^-))$$

        $$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^-$$

        Where $K_t$ = Kalman gain, $H_t$ = Jacobian of observation model, $Q_t$ = observation noise covariance.

        4.3.3: Context Awareness

        Context-aware agents adapt their behavior based on the current context, a high-level description of the situation.

        Context Definition

        Context $C$ is a tuple of relevant situational attributes:

        $$C = (\text{Location}, \text{Time}, \text{User Activity}, \text{Environment State}, \ldots)$$

        Context Recognition

        Given sensor data $z$, infer the current context:

        $$C^* = \arg\max_C P(C | z) = \arg\max_C \frac{P(z | C) P(C)}{P(z)}$$

        This is a classification problem solved by:

        • Naive Bayes: $P(z|C) = \prod_i P(z_i|C)$ (assumes independence)
        • Hidden Markov Models: for temporal context sequences
        • Deep Neural Networks: for raw sensor data

        Context-Adaptive Policy

        The agent's policy is conditioned on context:

        $$\pi(a | s, C) = P(\text{action} = a | \text{state} = s, \text{context} = C)$$

        Different contexts trigger different sub-policies:

        $$\pi_{overall}(a|s) = \sum_C P(C|s) \cdot \pi_C(a|s)$$

        FAQs: 4.3

        Q1: Why is the occupancy grid representation preferred over an exact geometric map?

        A: Occupancy grids have several advantages over exact geometric maps: (1) Handles uncertainty, cells have probabilistic occupancy, naturally representing sensor noise; (2) Works with noisy sensors, multiple uncertain readings are fused via Bayesian update; (3) Computationally efficient, grid operations are $O(m \times n)$, simple and parallelizable; (4) Handles dynamic environments, cells can be updated as the environment changes. Exact geometric maps require perfect measurements and cannot naturally represent uncertainty. The trade-off is resolution, a fine grid requires more memory than a compact geometric description.

        Q2: What is the computational complexity of EKF-SLAM and why does it not scale?

        A: EKF-SLAM maintains a state vector of size $(3 + 2N)$ and a covariance matrix of size $(3+2N)^2$, where $N$ is the number of landmarks. The update step requires inverting the innovation covariance matrix, which is $O((3+2N)^3)$ in the worst case, or $O(N^2)$ with sparse approximations. For large maps ($N = 10000$ landmarks), this is $O(10^8)$ operations per step, far too slow for real-time operation. This motivated the development of scalable alternatives: FastSLAM (particle filter, $O(N \log N)$), graph-based SLAM (batch optimization), and deep learning SLAM.

        Chapter 5: Agent Communication

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Understand why and how agents communicate
        • Formally specify communication models and protocols
        • Describe and apply Agent Communication Languages (ACL, KQML)
        • Analyze interaction protocols mathematically
        • Design correct and efficient communication strategies

        5.1: Communication Fundamentals

        5.1.1: Need for Communication

        Communication in MAS serves multiple critical purposes. Without communication, agents would need to either act independently (missing opportunities for collaboration) or share no information (leading to conflicts and duplication).

        Why Agents Need to Communicate

        1. Information Sharing

        Agent $i$ has information $K_i$ and agent $j$ has $K_j$. After communication:

        $$K_j^{new} = K_j \cup \{\text{shared info from } i\}$$

        Value of Information Sharing:

        $$\Delta U_j = \mathbb{E}[U_j(\text{optimal action given } K_j^{new})] - \mathbb{E}[U_j(\text{optimal action given } K_j)]$$

        Communication is worthwhile iff $\Delta U_j > C_{comm}$ (communication cost).

        2. Coordination

        Without communication, two agents taking the same action can cause conflicts:

        $$P(\text{conflict}) = P(a_i = a_j \wedge \text{actions conflict})$$

        With communication, agents coordinate to avoid conflicts:

        $$P(\text{conflict with comm}) \ll P(\text{conflict without comm})$$

        3. Task Distribution

        Agent $i$ with task $T$ can delegate subtask $T_j$ to agent $j$ if $j$ is better suited:

        $$\text{Delegate} \iff \frac{U_j(T_j)}{C_{comm} + C_{delegation}} > U_i(T_j)$$

        4. Negotiation

        Agents with conflicting goals use communication to reach acceptable agreements.

        Communication Cost Model

        Bandwidth cost: Sending message of size $|m|$ bytes at bandwidth $B$:

        $$t_{transmission} = \frac{|m|}{B}$$

        Latency cost: Network round-trip time $RTT$.

        Computational cost: Processing message $m$:

        $$C_{process}(m) = f(|m|, \text{semantic complexity})$$

        Total communication cost:

        $$C_{total} = \alpha \cdot \frac{|m|}{B} + \beta \cdot RTT + \gamma \cdot C_{process}(m)$$

        FAQs: 5.1.1

        Q1: When should an agent choose NOT to communicate?

        A: An agent should not communicate when: (1) Cost exceeds benefit: $C_{total} > \Delta U$ (the utility gain from communication is less than the cost); (2) Information is irrelevant: The information to be shared does not affect any agent's decisions; (3) Security risk: Sharing information with an untrustworthy agent may be exploited against the sender; (4) Bandwidth constraints: The channel is saturated with higher-priority messages; (5) Privacy: The agent has an obligation not to share certain information. In MAS design, a key challenge is computing $\Delta U$ without actually communicating, which often requires meta-reasoning about what information would be useful.

        5.1.2: Communication Models

        Shannon's Information-Theoretic Model

        Claude Shannon's model of communication:

        $$\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{encode}} \text{Channel} \xrightarrow{\text{decode}} \text{Destination}$$

        Channel capacity (Shannon-Hartley theorem):

        $$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

        Where:

        • $C$ = channel capacity (bits/second)
        • $B$ = bandwidth (Hz)
        • $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio

        Information content of message $m$:

        $$I(m) = -\log_2 P(m) \text{ bits}$$

        Rare messages carry more information. If $P(m) = 0.5$: $I(m) = 1$ bit. If $P(m) = 0.25$: $I(m) = 2$ bits.

        Entropy (average information per message):

        $$H(M) = -\sum_{m \in \mathcal{M}} P(m) \log_2 P(m)$$

        Speech Act Theory (Austin & Searle)

        In agent communication, messages are speech acts, communicative actions that do things, not just convey information.

        Speech Act Components:

        • Locution: The literal content of the utterance
        • Illocution: The communicative intention (what the agent is doing: requesting, informing, promising...)
        • Perlocution: The effect on the listener

        Performative Verbs for Agents:

        PerformativeMeaningEffect
        INFORM"I believe $\phi$"Listener adds $\phi$ to beliefs
        REQUEST"Please do $\alpha$"Listener considers action $\alpha$
        QUERY-IF"Is $\phi$ true?"Listener answers truthfully
        PROPOSE"I suggest $\alpha$ for price $p$"Initiates negotiation
        ACCEPT"I agree to $\alpha$"Commits to agreement
        REJECT"I decline $\alpha$"Declines proposal
        SUBSCRIBE"Notify me when $\phi$"Registers for notification

        Sincerity Conditions (for a speech act to be sincere):

        • INFORM($\phi$): Speaker believes $\phi$
        • REQUEST($\alpha$): Speaker wants $\alpha$ done, doesn't believe it will happen without the request
        • PROMISE($\alpha$): Speaker intends to do $\alpha$

        5.1.3: Message Passing

        Message passing is the fundamental communication primitive in distributed systems.

        Message Structure

        A message $m$ has structure:

        $$m = \langle sender, receiver, performative, content, conv_id, timestamp \rangle$$

        • sender: Identity of sending agent ($A_i$)
        • receiver: Identity of receiving agent(s) ($A_j$ or broadcast)
        • performative: Speech act type (INFORM, REQUEST, etc.)
        • content: The message payload (proposition, action, etc.)
        • conv_id: Conversation identifier (links related messages)
        • timestamp: Time of sending

        Message Passing Patterns

        Point-to-Point:

        $$A_i \xrightarrow{m} A_j$$

        One sender, one receiver. $O(1)$ communication cost.

        Broadcast:

        $$A_i \xrightarrow{m} \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n\}$$

        One sender, all receivers. $O(n)$ cost. Problem: only 1 message needed if using a broadcast medium.

        Multicast:

        $$A_i \xrightarrow{m} G \subseteq \{A_1, \ldots, A_n\}$$

        One sender, subset of receivers. $O(|G|)$ cost.

        Gossip Protocol:

        Each agent forwards messages to $k$ random neighbors:

        $$A_i \xrightarrow{m} \text{random } k \text{ agents}$$

        After $r$ rounds, message reaches all agents with probability:

        $$P(\text{all receive}) \approx 1 - e^{-n(1-(1-k/n)^r)}$$

        Gossip achieves epidemic coverage in $O(\log n)$ rounds.

        Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Communication

        TypeSender blocks?ComplexityUse Case
        SynchronousYes (waits for reply)SimpleRemote procedure calls
        AsynchronousNo (continues)ComplexScalable systems

        Asynchronous message queue model:

        Messages are stored in queue $Q_j$ for agent $j$:

        $$Q_j = \langle m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_k \rangle \text{ (FIFO)}$$

        Agent $j$ processes messages when ready: $m = \text{dequeue}(Q_j)$.

        Message delivery guarantees:

        GuaranteeMeaning
        At-most-onceMessage may be lost, never duplicated
        At-least-onceMessage delivered, may be duplicated
        Exactly-onceMessage delivered exactly once
        OrderedMessages arrive in send order

        FAQs: 5.1.3

        Q1: Why is exactly-once delivery harder to guarantee than at-least-once?

        A: Exactly-once delivery requires: (1) Detection of losses, re-send if no acknowledgment; (2) Detection of duplicates, discard if already processed; (3) Atomic acknowledgment, the receiver must atomically process the message and acknowledge, so that a failure doesn't leave the system uncertain whether the message was processed. At-least-once only requires re-sending until an acknowledgment is received, which is simpler. The challenge with exactly-once is the "two generals problem", it's impossible to guarantee exactly-once delivery in the presence of arbitrary network failures (a fundamental impossibility result in distributed systems).

        Q2: An agent broadcasts to 100 agents, what is the communication overhead in a star vs. gossip network?

        A: Star network: Agent sends to a central hub, which forwards to all 100. Total messages = 1 + 100 = 101. Gossip with $k=5$, $r$ rounds: Round 1: Agent sends to 5 neighbors (5 messages). Round 2: Each of 5 sends to 5 more (25 messages). After $r$ rounds, messages sent ≈ $5^r$. To cover 100 agents: $5^r = 100 \Rightarrow r = \log_5(100) \approx 2.86 \approx 3$ rounds. Total messages ≈ $5 + 25 + 125 = 155$, slightly more than star but with no central bottleneck and higher fault tolerance. In large systems (n = 10,000), gossip's $O(n \log n)$ vs. star's $O(n)$ overhead is offset by gossip's superior fault tolerance.

        5.2: Agent Communication Languages

        5.2.1: ACL (Agent Communication Language)

        FIPA-ACL (Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents, Agent Communication Language) is the standard ACL for multi-agent systems.

        ACL Message Structure

        A FIPA-ACL message is a structured object with parameters:

        (inform
  :sender    (agent-identifier :name agent1)
  :receiver  (agent-identifier :name agent2)
  :content   "temperature(room1, 23.5)"
  :language  "Prolog"
  :ontology  "building-management"
  :protocol  fipa-request
  :conversation-id  conv-001
  :reply-with  reply-001
  :in-reply-to  nil
  :reply-by   20260101T120000
)

        Communicative Acts in FIPA-ACL

        FIPA-ACL defines 22 communicative acts organized by purpose:

        Information Sharing:

        • inform($\phi$): Sender believes $\phi$, wants receiver to believe $\phi$
        • confirm($\phi$): Sender believes $\phi$, expects receiver is uncertain
        • disconfirm($\phi$): Sender believes $\neg\phi$

        Queries:

        • query-if($\phi$): Sender wants to know if $\phi$ is true
        • query-ref($e$): Sender wants to know the value of expression $e$

        Action Requests:

        • request($\alpha$): Sender wants receiver to perform action $\alpha$
        • request-when($\alpha, \phi$): Sender wants $\alpha$ done when $\phi$ becomes true

        Negotiation:

        • propose($\alpha$, $\text{precondition}$): Offer to do $\alpha$ if precondition holds
        • accept-proposal($\alpha$): Accept a proposal
        • reject-proposal($\alpha$, $\text{reason}$): Reject a proposal

        Error Handling:

        • failure($\alpha$, $\text{reason}$): Could not perform $\alpha$
        • not-understood($m$, $\text{reason}$): Could not understand message $m$

        Formal Semantics of FIPA-ACL

        Each communicative act has formal pre- and post-conditions using modal logic.

        For inform($\phi$):

        Feasibility Preconditions (FP):

        $$FP(\text{inform}(i, j, \phi)) \equiv B_i \phi \wedge \neg B_i (B_j \phi \vee B_j \neg \phi)$$

        (Agent $i$ believes $\phi$ AND doesn't think $j$ already knows $\phi$'s truth value)

        Rational Effect (RE):

        $$RE(\text{inform}(i, j, \phi)) \equiv B_j \phi$$

        (The intended effect is that $j$ believes $\phi$)

        For request($\alpha$):

        $$FP(\text{request}(i, j, \alpha)) \equiv I_i(\text{done}(j, \alpha)) \wedge \neg B_i(I_j(\text{done}(\alpha)))$$

        (Agent $i$ intends $j$ to do $\alpha$, and doesn't think $j$ already intends to do it)

        5.2.2: KQML (Knowledge Query and Manipulation Language)

        KQML predates FIPA-ACL and uses a different design philosophy.

        KQML Message Structure

        (ask-one
  :sender       agent1
  :receiver     agent2
  :content      (price IBM ?x)
  :reply-with   q1
  :language     KIF
  :ontology     stock-market
)

        KQML Performatives

        PerformativeMeaning
        tellAssert fact
        ask-oneAsk for one answer
        ask-allAsk for all answers
        achieveRequest agent to make $\phi$ true
        subscribeSubscribe to notifications
        advertiseAdvertise capability
        brokerAsk facilitator to find agents
        forwardForward to appropriate agent

        KQML Facilitators

        KQML uses facilitators (special agents) for:

        • Yellow Pages: Agent capability advertisement and lookup
        • White Pages: Agent identity and location
        • Broker: Matching queries to capable agents

        Yellow Pages Query:

        $$\text{Query}: (\text{broker } : \text{content } (\text{can-answer } ?a \text{ stock-queries}))$$

        $$\text{Response}: \text{list of agent identifiers that handle stock queries}$$

        ACL vs. KQML Comparison

        FeatureFIPA-ACLKQML
        StandardizationFIPA standardInformal
        Formal semanticsYes (modal logic)Partial
        Performatives22 well-definedMany (inconsistent)
        Content languageFlexibleKIF preferred
        TransportFlexibleDirect
        AdoptionIndustry standardResearch use

        5.2.3: Semantic Communication

        Semantic communication focuses on conveying meaning rather than just bits, the receiver understands the intent and context of the message.

        Ontologies for Semantic Interoperability

        Agents from different organizations may use different vocabularies. Ontologies provide shared conceptualizations:

        $$\text{Ontology} = \langle \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{I}, \mathcal{A} \rangle$$

        • $\mathcal{C}$ = set of concepts (classes)
        • $\mathcal{R}$ = set of relations between concepts
        • $\mathcal{I}$ = set of individuals (instances)
        • $\mathcal{A}$ = set of axioms (constraints)

        Semantic Distance:

        $$d_{sem}(c_1, c_2) = \text{path length in ontology hierarchy between } c_1 \text{ and } c_2$$

        Agents with similar ontologies have low semantic distance → easier to communicate.

        Information-Theoretic View of Semantic Communication

        Mutual Information between sender's message $X$ and receiver's understanding $Y$:

        $$I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X | Y) = \sum_{x,y} P(x,y) \log \frac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}$$

        Perfect semantic communication: $I(X;Y) = H(X)$ (receiver perfectly understands sender).

        Semantic Noise: Misunderstanding due to different ontologies:

        $$H(X|Y) = \text{semantic entropy} > 0 \text{ (uncertainty remaining after message received)}$$

        Goal: Design protocols that minimize $H(X|Y)$ while minimizing message length $|m|$.

        FAQs: 5.2

        Q1: Why is formal semantics important for agent communication languages?

        A: Formal semantics (using modal logic) provides a precise, unambiguous specification of what each communicative act means and what effects it should have. Without formal semantics: (1) Agents from different implementations may interpret the same performative differently, causing subtle bugs; (2) It's impossible to verify that an agent's communication behavior is correct; (3) Interoperability between agents from different vendors cannot be guaranteed. With formal semantics, implementors have a contract to implement against, and formal verification tools can check compliance.

        Q2: How can two agents with different ontologies communicate semantically?

        A: Several approaches: (1) Ontology alignment, find mappings between concepts in both ontologies: $align: C_1 \rightarrow C_2$; (2) Upper ontology, both agents share a common upper ontology (like SUMO) and specialize it differently; (3) Translation agent, a mediator agent translates between ontologies; (4) Negotiation, agents exchange grounding signals to establish shared meaning for terms used in a conversation; (5) Semantic web standards, using OWL/RDF allows automated reasoning to find concept correspondences.

        5.3: Interaction Protocols

        Interaction protocols define the rules governing the sequence of messages in a multi-agent conversation.

        5.3.1: Request-Response Protocol

        The simplest protocol: one agent requests, another responds.

        Protocol Specification (Finite State Machine)

        States: $\{\text{Init}, \text{Waiting}, \text{Done}, \text{Failed}\}$

        Transitions:

        $$\text{Init} \xrightarrow{\text{send REQUEST}} \text{Waiting}$$

        $$\text{Waiting} \xrightarrow{\text{receive INFORM}} \text{Done}$$

        $$\text{Waiting} \xrightarrow{\text{receive FAILURE}} \text{Failed}$$

        $$\text{Waiting} \xrightarrow{\text{timeout}} \text{Failed}$$

        Timeout Analysis

        Let $\tau$ = network latency (random variable), $D$ = deadline.

        $$P(\text{success}) = P(\tau \leq D)$$

        For exponential latency $\tau \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$:

        $$P(\text{success}) = 1 - e^{-\mu D}$$

        Setting $P(\text{success}) = 0.99$:

        $$D = \frac{\ln(100)}{\mu} = \frac{4.605}{\mu}$$

        The timeout should be set to $D \approx 4.6 / \mu$ for 99% success rate.

        Protocol Compliance

        Agent $j$ is compliant with the request protocol if it always responds within deadline $D$:

        $$\forall \text{ REQUEST received by } j : \exists \text{ response within time } D$$

        Protocol safety: No incorrect messages are ever sent.

        Protocol liveness: The protocol eventually terminates.

        $$\text{Safety}: \forall \text{ execution} \sigma : \sigma \text{ conforms to valid state transitions}$$

        $$\text{Liveness}: \forall \text{ execution starting from Init}: \text{eventually reaches Done or Failed}$$

        5.3.2: Contract Net Protocol

        The Contract Net Protocol (CNP) (Smith, 1980) is a foundational MAS protocol for task allocation.

        Protocol Overview

        Roles:

        • Manager: Has a task, needs an agent to execute it
        • Contractor(s): Capable agents that bid for the task

        CNP Steps

        Step 1 Announcement:

        Manager broadcasts task announcement:

        $$m_{announce} = \langle \text{CALL-FOR-PROPOSALS}, \text{task_spec}, \text{deadline} \rangle$$

        Step 2 Bidding:

        Each contractor $i$ that can handle the task submits a bid:

        $$m_{bid_i} = \langle \text{PROPOSE}, \text{bid_price}_i, \text{quality_estimate}_i \rangle$$

        Contractor $i$ bids iff its cost $c_i$ allows a profitable bid:

        $$\text{Bid} \iff c_i < \max_{\text{feasible bid}} \text{revenue}_i$$

        Step 3 Evaluation:

        Manager selects the winning contractor:

        $$j^* = \arg\min_{j \in \text{bidders}} \text{bid_price}_j \cdot w_p + (1 - \text{quality}_j) \cdot w_q$$

        Where $w_p$ and $w_q$ are weights for price and quality.

        Step 4 Award:

        $$m_{award} = \langle \text{ACCEPT-PROPOSAL}, j^* \rangle$$

        $$m_{reject_j} = \langle \text{REJECT-PROPOSAL}, j \rangle \quad \forall j \neq j^*$$

        Step 5 Execution and Report:

        $$m_{result} = \langle \text{INFORM}, \text{task_result} \rangle$$

        CNP Complexity

        • Messages per task: $O(n)$ where $n$ = number of contractors
        • Manager evaluation: $O(n \log n)$ (sort bids)
        • Total time: $O(n + \text{task_execution_time})$

        Optimal Bidding Strategy

        A strategic contractor bids optimally by solving:

        $$\text{bid}^* = \arg\max_{b > c_i} (b - c_i) \cdot P(\text{win} | \text{bid} = b)$$

        Under symmetric auction theory, with $n-1$ competitors having costs distributed as $F(c)$:

        $$\text{bid}^*(c_i) = c_i + \frac{\int_{c_i}^{\bar{c}} F(c)^{n-1} dc}{F(c_i)^{n-1}}$$

        This is the symmetric Bayes-Nash equilibrium bid.

        5.3.3: Negotiation Protocols

        Negotiation is the process by which agents reach mutually acceptable agreements through a sequence of proposals and counter-proposals.

        Negotiation Space

        The negotiation space (or contract space) $\mathcal{N}$ is the set of all possible agreements:

        $$\mathcal{N} = \mathcal{X}_1 \times \mathcal{X}_2 \times \cdots \times \mathcal{X}_k$$

        Where $\mathcal{X}_j$ = domain of issue $j$ (e.g., price, quantity, delivery date).

        Individual Rationality and Pareto Optimality

        An agreement $x \in \mathcal{N}$ is:

        Individually Rational (IR): Better for each agent than no deal:

        $$x \text{ is IR} \iff U_i(x) \geq U_i(\text{no deal}) \quad \forall i$$

        Pareto Optimal (PO): No other agreement makes all agents at least as well off:

        $$x \text{ is PO} \iff \nexists x' \in \mathcal{N} : U_i(x') \geq U_i(x) \forall i \text{ with strict inequality for some } i$$

        Bilateral Negotiation

        Alternating Offers Protocol (Rubinstein, 1982):

        At time $t$, agent $i$ makes offer $x^t$:

        • If opponent accepts: done
        • If opponent rejects: opponent makes counter-offer $x^{t+1}$

        Discount factor: Agents discount future payoffs by $\delta_i \in [0,1)$ per period.

        Unique Subgame Perfect Equilibrium:

        $$x^* = \frac{1 - \delta_j}{1 - \delta_i \delta_j}$$

        (buyer's share of a $[0,1]$ surplus)

        For equal discount factors $\delta_i = \delta_j = \delta$:

        $$x^* = \frac{1}{1+\delta}$$

        As $\delta \to 1$ (patient agents): $x^* \to \frac{1}{2}$ (equal split, Nash bargaining solution).

        Nash Bargaining Solution

        The Nash Bargaining Solution maximizes the product of utility gains:

        $$x^* = \arg\max_{x \in \mathcal{N}: U_i(x) \geq d_i} \prod_{i=1}^n (U_i(x) - d_i)$$

        Where $d_i = U_i(\text{no deal})$ is agent $i$'s disagreement point.

        This satisfies four axioms:

        1. Pareto Optimality: No other feasible solution is better for all
        2. Symmetry: If agents are symmetric, they get equal payoffs
        3. Invariant to affine transformations of utility
        4. Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives

          5. FAQs: 5.3

          Q1: In CNP, why might a contractor truthfully reveal its true cost?

          A: In a first-price sealed-bid auction (similar to CNP), bidding your true cost $c_i$ is not optimal, you would get zero profit if you win. The optimal bid shades above cost: $b^* = c_i + \text{markup}$. However, in a second-price (Vickrey) auction, bidding your true cost IS the dominant strategy, because the winner pays the second-highest bid, not their own. In CNP's first-price setting, contractors shade bids upward based on competition: more competitors → lower markup, less competitors → higher markup.

          Q2: Prove that the Nash Bargaining Solution is Pareto Optimal.

          A: Suppose $x^* = \arg\max \prod_i (U_i(x) - d_i)$ is not Pareto optimal. Then there exists $x'$ such that $U_i(x') \geq U_i(x^*)$ for all $i$, with strict inequality for some $j$. Then $(U_j(x') - d_j) > (U_j(x^*) - d_j)$ and $(U_i(x') - d_i) \geq (U_i(x^*) - d_i) > 0$ for all $i$ (using individual rationality). Therefore $\prod_i (U_i(x') - d_i) > \prod_i (U_i(x^*) - d_i)$, contradicting $x^*$ being the maximizer. Contradiction, so $x^*$ must be Pareto optimal. $\square$

        Chapter 6: Coordination and Cooperation

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Formally define coordination and cooperation in MAS
        • Analyze centralized and distributed coordination mechanisms
        • Study team and coalition formation algorithms
        • Apply distributed constraint optimization to multi-agent problems
        • Understand task decomposition, allocation, and consensus

        6.1: Coordination Mechanisms

        Coordination is the process of managing the interactions and dependencies between agents so they avoid conflicts and accomplish joint goals.

        6.1.1: Centralized Coordination

        In centralized coordination, a single coordinator agent manages all interactions.

        Coordinator Architecture

        $$\text{Coordinator}: \mathcal{C} = \langle S^G, A^G, f^G \rangle$$

        • $S^G$ = global state (information from all agents)
        • $A^G$ = global action space (commands to all agents)
        • $f^G$ = global policy

        The coordinator receives state from agents, computes optimal joint action:

        $$\mathbf{a}^* = (a_1^*, a_2^*, \ldots, a_n^*) = \arg\max_{\mathbf{a}} U^G(S^G, \mathbf{a})$$

        Where $U^G$ = global utility function.

        Optimal Centralized Allocation

        Given $n$ tasks $\{T_1, \ldots, T_n\}$ and $m$ agents $\{A_1, \ldots, A_m\}$, the coordinator solves:

        $$\max_{x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}} \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^m u_{ij} x_{ij}$$

        Subject to:

        $$\sum_{j=1}^m x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \text{ (each task assigned to exactly one agent)}$$

        $$\sum_{i=1}^n x_{ij} \leq c_j \quad \forall j \text{ (agent } j \text{ has capacity } c_j\text{)}$$

        This is the Assignment Problem, solvable optimally by the Hungarian Algorithm in $O(n^3)$ time.

        Hungarian Algorithm (Brief Overview)

        For an $n \times n$ cost matrix $C$:

        1. Subtract row minimum from each row
        2. Subtract column minimum from each column
        3. Cover all zeros with minimum number of lines $k$
        4. If $k = n$: optimal assignment found
        5. Else: adjust matrix and repeat

          6. Complexity: $O(n^3)$, efficient for moderate $n$ but impractical for very large MAS.

          Centralized Coordination Bottleneck

          The coordinator must process $n$ agent states and broadcast $n$ commands:

          $$T_{coord} = T_{collect} + T_{compute} + T_{distribute}$$

          $$= O(n) + O(n^3) + O(n) = O(n^3)$$

          For $n > 1000$, this becomes prohibitive, motivating distributed approaches.

          6.1.2: Distributed Coordination

          In distributed coordination, no single agent has global information. Agents coordinate locally.

          Distributed Constraint Satisfaction Problem (DCSP)

          A DCSP is a constraint satisfaction problem distributed across agents:

          $$\text{DCSP} = \langle X, D, C, \mathcal{A} \rangle$$

          • $X = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ = variables (one or more per agent)
          • $D = \{D_1, \ldots, D_n\}$ = variable domains
          • $C = \{c_1, \ldots, c_k\}$ = constraints between variables
          • $\mathcal{A} = \{A_1, \ldots, A_m\}$ = agents (each controls subset of $X$)

          Goal: Find assignment $\{x_i = v_i : v_i \in D_i\}$ satisfying all constraints in $C$.

          Agent Knows Only:

          • Its own variables' domains
          • Constraints involving its own variables

          Asynchronous Backtracking (ABT)

          ABT is a complete DCSP algorithm where agents operate asynchronously:

          Agent $i$ maintains:

          • Current value $d_i \in D_i$
          • Agent view $AV_i$: last known values of all lower-priority variables
          • No-good list $nogoods_i$: known inconsistent partial assignments

          ABT Algorithm:

          1. Check: Is current $d_i$ consistent with $AV_i$?
          2. If consistent: Send $\langle OK?, i, d_i \rangle$ to higher-priority agents
          3. If inconsistent: Try new value from $D_i$
          4. If no value works: Generate no-good, send to lowest-priority agent in no-good, backtrack

            5. Termination and Correctness:

            ABT is sound (terminates only with a solution or infeasibility proof) and complete (always terminates for finite domains).

            Distributed Constraint Optimization (DCOP)

            DCOP extends DCSP with optimization: minimize total constraint violation cost.

            $$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \sum_{c \in C} f_c(\mathbf{x}_c)$$

            Where $f_c(\mathbf{x}_c)$ = cost of constraint $c$ given values $\mathbf{x}_c$ of variables in $c$.

            DPOP (Dynamic Programming Optimization Protocol):

            Organizes agents into a spanning tree. Messages pass up (cost aggregation) and down (optimal value selection):

            UTIL propagation (leaves to root):

            $$UTIL_i(x_i) = \sum_{k \in children(i)} \min_{x_k} [f_{ik}(x_i, x_k) + UTIL_k(x_k)]$$

            VALUE propagation (root to leaves):

            $$x_i^* = \arg\min_{x_i} [UTIL_i(x_i) + \sum_{j \in ancestors(i)} f_{ij}(x_j^*, x_i)]$$

            Complexity: DPOP is complete and optimal, with exponential message size in tree width, practical for sparse constraint graphs.

            6.1.3: Synchronization

            Synchronization ensures agents coordinate in time, critical for parallel tasks with dependencies.

            Task Dependency Graph

            Tasks and their dependencies form a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG):

            $$G_{dep} = (T, E)$$

            Where edge $(T_i, T_j) \in E$ means $T_j$ cannot start until $T_i$ completes.

            Critical Path Method (CPM)

            The critical path determines the minimum project duration.

            Earliest Start Time (EST):

            $$EST(T_i) = \max_{j : (T_j, T_i) \in E} [EST(T_j) + d_j]$$

            Where $d_j$ = duration of task $T_j$. (For tasks with no predecessors: $EST(T_i) = 0$.)

            Latest Start Time (LST):

            $$LST(T_i) = EST(\text{end}) - \max_{j : (T_i, T_j) \in E} [LST(T_j) - d_i] - d_i$$

            Float (slack): $F(T_i) = LST(T_i) - EST(T_i)$

            Critical tasks: $F(T_i) = 0$, delaying these delays the whole project.

            Project Duration:

            $$D_{project} = \max_{\text{path } p \text{ in } G_{dep}} \sum_{T_i \in p} d_i$$

            This equals the length of the longest path (critical path) in $G_{dep}$.

            Barrier Synchronization

            All agents must reach a barrier before any can proceed:

            $$\text{Barrier: all } n \text{ agents call barrier.wait()}$$

            $$\text{All released simultaneously when all have called wait}$$

            Expected waiting time at barrier:

            If agent $i$'s time to reach the barrier is $t_i \sim F_i$, the waiting time for agent $i$ is:

            $$W_i = \max_j(t_j) - t_i$$

            Expected barrier release time:

            $$\mathbb{E}[\max_j t_j] \approx \mu + \sigma \sqrt{2 \ln n} \text{ (for normal } t_j\text{)}$$

            Barrier synchronization introduces overhead proportional to the variance of completion times.

            FAQs: 6.1

            Q1: For a project with tasks $A(3h), B(2h), C(4h), D(1h)$ where $C$ depends on $A$ and $B$, and $D$ depends on $C$: find the critical path and project duration.

            A: Tasks: $A=3h, B=2h, C=4h, D=1h$. Dependencies: $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$ and $B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$.

            EST(A) = 0, EST(B) = 0

            EST(C) = max(EST(A) + d(A), EST(B) + d(B)) = max(0+3, 0+2) = 3

            EST(D) = EST(C) + d(C) = 3 + 4 = 7

            EST(end) = EST(D) + d(D) = 7 + 1 = 8

            Critical path: A → C → D (length 3+4+1 = 8 hours)

            Project duration: 8 hours. Task B has float = 1 hour (can start up to 1h late without delaying project).

        6.2: Cooperation Strategies

        6.2.1: Team Formation

        A team is a group of agents that cooperate to achieve a shared goal.

        Formal Team Definition

        A team is a tuple:

        $$\mathcal{T} = \langle \mathcal{A}_T, G_T, \Pi_T \rangle$$

        • $\mathcal{A}_T \subseteq \mathcal{A}$: set of team members
        • $G_T$: shared team goal
        • $\Pi_T$: joint team plan (role assignments + action sequences)

        Team Goal Achievement

        Team goal $G_T$ is decomposed into subgoals:

        $$G_T = G_1 \oplus G_2 \oplus \cdots \oplus G_k$$

        Where $\oplus$ is a composition operator (sequential, parallel, or conditional).

        Each subgoal $G_j$ is assigned to agent $\mathcal{A}_{r(j)}$ based on capability:

        $$r(j) = \arg\max_{i \in \mathcal{A}_T} \text{capability}(\mathcal{A}_i, G_j)$$

        Capability function:

        $$\text{capability}(\mathcal{A}_i, G_j) \in [0,1]$$

        Measures how well agent $i$ can achieve subgoal $j$.

        Optimal Team Composition

        Given a goal $G_T$ and available agents $\mathcal{A}$, find the best team:

        $$\mathcal{T}^* = \arg\max_{\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{A}} \left[\sum_{j} \text{capability}(r(j), G_j) - \text{coordination_cost}(|\mathcal{T}|)\right]$$

        Where $\text{coordination_cost}(n) = O(n^2)$ captures the overhead of coordinating a team of size $n$.

        Optimal team size trade-off:

        $$n^* = \arg\max_n \left[n \cdot \bar{c} - \alpha n^2\right] = \frac{\bar{c}}{2\alpha}$$

        Where $\bar{c}$ = average marginal contribution and $\alpha$ = coordination cost coefficient.

        Joint Intentions

        A team has joint intentions, not just individual intentions, but shared beliefs about what the team is doing:

        Mutual Belief (MB): All team members believe $\phi$:

        $$MB_{\mathcal{T}}(\phi) \iff \forall i \in \mathcal{T}: B_i \phi \wedge B_i(MB_{\mathcal{T}}(\phi))$$

        Shared Plan: All members have the same plan $\Pi_T$:

        $$\text{SharedPlan}(\mathcal{T}, \Pi_T) \iff MB_{\mathcal{T}}(\Pi_T) \wedge \forall i \in \mathcal{T}: I_i(\text{execute role}(i, \Pi_T))$$

        6.2.2: Coalition Formation

        A coalition is a temporary group of agents that form, cooperate, and dissolve when the task is complete.

        Cooperative Game Theory

        A cooperative game is $(N, v)$:

        • $N = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ = set of all agents
        • $v : 2^N \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ = characteristic function ($v(S)$ = value coalition $S$ can achieve)

        Properties of $v$:

        • Superadditivity: $v(S \cup T) \geq v(S) + v(T)$ for $S \cap T = \emptyset$

        Superadditivity means larger coalitions achieve at least as much as their parts separately, the incentive to form coalitions.

        The Shapley Value

        The Shapley value $\phi_i(v)$ gives agent $i$'s fair share of the coalition's value:

        $$\phi_i(v) = \sum_{S \subseteq N \setminus \{i\}} \frac{|S|! \cdot (n - |S| - 1)!}{n!} [v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)]$$

        Interpretation: Average marginal contribution of agent $i$ across all possible orderings of agents joining the coalition.

        Shapley Value Properties:

        1. Efficiency: $\sum_i \phi_i(v) = v(N)$ (entire value is distributed)
        2. Symmetry: Symmetric agents get equal shares
        3. Null Player: Agent contributing nothing gets $\phi_i = 0$
        4. Linearity: Shapley value is linear in $v$

          5. Example: 3 agents, $v(\{1\}) = 1, v(\{2\}) = 1, v(\{3\}) = 0, v(\{1,2\}) = 4, v(\{1,3\}) = 2, v(\{2,3\}) = 2, v(\{1,2,3\}) = 6$

          $$\phi_1 = \frac{1}{3!}[v(1)-v(\emptyset) + v(12)-v(2) + v(13)-v(3) + \ldots] = \frac{1+3+2+4}{6} \cdot \text{adjustment} = 2.67$$

          The Core

          The core of a cooperative game is the set of payoff distributions where no coalition has incentive to deviate:

          $$\text{Core}(v) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^n : \sum_{i \in N} x_i = v(N) \wedge \sum_{i \in S} x_i \geq v(S) \forall S \subseteq N\}$$

          If $x \in \text{Core}(v)$, then no subgroup of agents can do better by forming their own coalition.

          Core non-emptiness: The core may be empty (unstable games). Checking non-emptiness is solvable by linear programming.

          Coalition Formation Algorithm

          Greedy Merging:

          1. Start: each agent is its own coalition
          2. Repeatedly merge two coalitions $S, T$ if $v(S \cup T) > v(S) + v(T)$
          3. Stop when no beneficial merges remain

            4. This is an $O(n^2)$ algorithm that finds a locally optimal partition.

            Optimal coalition structure (finding the best partition of $N$ into coalitions) is NP-hard, requires exploring all $B_n$ (Bell number) partitions.

            $$B_n = \sum_{k=0}^n S(n,k) = 1, 1, 2, 5, 15, 52, 203, 877, 4140, \ldots$$

            For $n = 20$: $B_{20} \approx 5.17 \times 10^{13}$, exhaustive search is infeasible.

            6.2.3: Resource Sharing

            Agents must often share scarce resources. Resource sharing protocols ensure efficiency and fairness.

            Resource Allocation Problem

            $m$ resources $\{R_1, \ldots, R_m\}$, $n$ agents $\{A_1, \ldots, A_n\}$. Agent $i$ has utility $u_i(\mathbf{x}_i)$ for resource bundle $\mathbf{x}_i$.

            Social Welfare Maximization:

            $$\max_{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n} \sum_{i=1}^n u_i(\mathbf{x}_i) \quad \text{s.t.} \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{x}_i \leq \mathbf{R}$$

            Utilitarian Social Welfare: $SW_U = \sum_i u_i$

            Egalitarian Social Welfare: $SW_E = \min_i u_i$ (maximize minimum welfare)

            Nash Social Welfare: $SW_N = \prod_i u_i$ (balance efficiency and fairness)

            Max-Min Fairness

            The max-min fair allocation maximizes the minimum agent utility:

            $$\max_{\mathbf{x}} \min_i u_i(\mathbf{x}_i) \quad \text{s.t.} \sum_i \mathbf{x}_i \leq \mathbf{R}$$

            For bandwidth allocation, max-min fair gives each agent:

            $$x_i = \min(d_i, \text{fair_share})$$

            Where $d_i$ = demand of agent $i$, fair share = $R / n$.

            Envy-Free Allocation

            An allocation is envy-free if no agent prefers another's bundle:

            $$u_i(\mathbf{x}_i) \geq u_i(\mathbf{x}_j) \quad \forall i, j$$

            For divisible resources, envy-free allocations always exist. For indivisible resources, envy-freeness may be impossible (e.g., one item, two agents).

            FAQs: 6.2

            Q1: Compute the Shapley value for a 2-agent game: $v(\{1\}) = 3, v(\{2\}) = 5, v(\{1,2\}) = 10$.

            A: For agent 1: Order (1,2): contribution = $v(\{1\}) - v(\emptyset) = 3$. Order (2,1): contribution = $v(\{1,2\}) - v(\{2\}) = 10-5 = 5$. Shapley value: $\phi_1 = \frac{1}{2}(3 + 5) = 4$.

            For agent 2: Order (1,2): contribution = $v(\{1,2\}) - v(\{1\}) = 7$. Order (2,1): contribution = $v(\{2\}) - v(\emptyset) = 5$. $\phi_2 = \frac{1}{2}(7+5) = 6$.

            Check: $\phi_1 + \phi_2 = 4 + 6 = 10 = v(\{1,2\})$ ✓ (efficiency property satisfied).

            Q2: Why might the core be empty, and what are the implications for coalition formation?

            A: The core can be empty in games with cyclic dominance, e.g., a 3-agent majority voting game where any 2 agents can "win" by forming a coalition. No distribution satisfies all coalition constraints simultaneously because each potential distribution can be improved by some coalition. Implications: (1) No stable coalition structure exists; (2) Coalitions will continually form and break apart, the system is inherently unstable; (3) Designers must use approximate stability concepts (like the $\epsilon$-core, where defecting coalitions must gain at least $\epsilon$ to justify leaving) or use binding contracts.

        6.3: Distributed Problem Solving

        6.3.1: Task Decomposition

        Task decomposition breaks a complex task into subtasks that can be solved by individual agents or smaller groups.

        Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) Decomposition

        A task $T$ is decomposed using HTN methods:

        $$T \xrightarrow{\text{method}} \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k, \text{ordering constraints}\}$$

        HTN Planning Problem:

        $$\Pi_{HTN} = \langle D, s_0, \omega \rangle$$

        Where:

        • $D$ = domain (primitive tasks + decomposition methods)
        • $s_0$ = initial state
        • $\omega$ = network of tasks to accomplish

        Decomposition tree: A task $T$ decomposes into a tree where leaves are primitive (executable) tasks:

        $$T \rightarrow T_1 \rightarrow \{T_{11}, T_{12}\} \quad T_2 \rightarrow \{T_{21}\}$$

        Goal: Find a linearization of leaf tasks that is executable and achieves the top-level goal.

        Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)

        In practice, decomposition follows a WBS:

        $$WBS(T, d) = \begin{cases} T & \text{if } T \text{ is primitive or depth } d = 0 \\ \{WBS(T_i, d-1) : T_i \in \text{decompose}(T)\} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

        The WBS creates a hierarchy of tasks. Each level has:

        • Level 0: Project goal
        • Level 1: Major deliverables
        • Level 2: Subtasks
        • Level $k$: Work packages (assigned to individual agents)

        Decomposition Criteria

        CriterionFormulaGoal
        Minimize dependencies$|\{(T_i, T_j) : T_i \rightarrow T_j\}|$Enable parallel execution
        Balance load$\min_i \max_j |d_{ij}|$Equalize agent workloads
        Match capabilities$\max_i \text{cap}(A_{r(i)}, T_i)$Best agent for each task
        Minimize communication$\sum_{(T_i, T_j) \in E} C_{ij}$Reduce coordination overhead

        6.3.2: Task Allocation

        Task allocation assigns subtasks to agents to maximize efficiency.

        Market-Based Task Allocation

        Tasks are "sold" at auction, agents "buy" based on their capabilities:

        1. Task posting: Manager posts task $T_i$ with deadline and requirements
        2. Bidding: Agent $j$ bids $b_{ij}$ (based on its estimated cost)
        3. Allocation: Task allocated to lowest bidder: $j^* = \arg\min_j b_{ij}$

          4. Social efficiency: Market-based allocation is efficient (social welfare maximizing) when agents bid truthfully (incentivized by Vickrey auctions).

          Combinatorial Task Allocation

          Agents may have synergies, some tasks are cheaper to do together:

          $$c(T_i, T_j | \text{same agent}) < c(T_i) + c(T_j)$$

          The Combinatorial Assignment Problem (CAP):

          $$\min_{x_{ij}} \sum_j \sum_{S \subseteq T} c_j(S) \cdot y_{jS}$$

          Where $y_{jS} = 1$ if agent $j$ is assigned task set $S$. This is NP-hard but solvable with branch-and-bound or approximation algorithms.

          Load Balancing

          For load-balanced allocation, each agent should have equal expected workload:

          $$\text{Load}(A_j) = \sum_{i : r(i) = j} d_i \approx \frac{\sum_i d_i}{m} \quad \text{for all } j$$

          Imbalance metric:

          $$\text{Imbalance} = \frac{\max_j \text{Load}(A_j) - \min_j \text{Load}(A_j)}{\text{avg Load}}$$

          Target: $\text{Imbalance} \to 0$ for perfect load balance.

          6.3.3: Consensus Building

          Consensus algorithms allow agents to agree on a common value despite having different initial values and possible failures.

          Byzantine Generals Problem

          $n$ generals must agree on attack/retreat. Up to $f$ generals are traitors (Byzantine faults, send arbitrary messages).

          Theorem (Lamport, Shostak, Pease 1982):

          $$\text{Consensus requires } n \geq 3f + 1 \text{ generals}$$

          With fewer than $3f+1$ loyal generals, no algorithm can guarantee consensus.

          Oral Message (OM) Algorithm:

          For $m$ rounds of messages and $\leq m$ traitors:

          • OM(0): Each loyal general announces its value
          • OM(m): Each general runs OM(m-1) for each other general's values, takes majority

          Round complexity: $O(n^f)$, exponential in number of traitors.

          Paxos Consensus Algorithm

          Paxos achieves consensus among $n$ agents with up to $f < n/2$ crash failures.

          Roles: Proposers, Acceptors, Learners

          Phase 1 Prepare:

          • Proposer sends PREPARE($n$) to majority of acceptors
          • Acceptor promises not to accept proposals numbered $< n$, returns highest accepted value

          Phase 2 Accept:

          • Proposer sends ACCEPT($n$, $v$) where $v$ = highest valued seen in Phase 1
          • Acceptor accepts if hasn't promised a higher number

          Safety: At most one value is decided. Liveness: Eventually a value is decided (with bounded network delays).

          Message complexity: $O(n)$ per round, $O(1)$ rounds in happy path.

          Average Consensus

          For continuous values, agents run:

          $$x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + \epsilon \sum_{j \in N_i} (x_j(t) - x_i(t))$$

          Where $N_i$ = neighbors of agent $i$, $\epsilon > 0$ = step size.

          Convergence theorem: If the graph is connected and $\epsilon < 1/d_{max}$ (where $d_{max}$ = maximum degree):

          $$x_i(t) \to \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_i x_i(0) \quad \forall i$$

          All agents converge to the average of initial values.

          Convergence rate: $\|x(t) - \bar{x}\mathbf{1}\| \leq \lambda_2^t \|x(0) - \bar{x}\mathbf{1}\|$

          Where $\lambda_2$ = second largest eigenvalue of weight matrix $W$. Faster convergence when $\lambda_2$ is small (well-connected graph).

          FAQs: 6.3

          Q1: Why does Byzantine consensus require $n \geq 3f+1$ rather than $n \geq 2f+1$?

          A: With crash failures ($n \geq 2f+1$ suffices): failed agents simply stop, they don't send messages. A majority of $f+1$ correct agents can always outvote $f$ silent ones. With Byzantine failures: traitors send different messages to different agents, half the other agents might receive "attack," half receive "retreat." Even a majority vote can be corrupted: with $n = 3f$, the $f$ traitors can fool $f$ correct agents into voting with them, splitting the remaining $f$ correct agents. This allows traitors to manipulate the majority. With $n = 3f+1$, the $2f+1$ correct agents can always form a true majority against $f$ traitors.

          Q2: In average consensus, how many iterations are needed to reduce error by factor 100 if $\lambda_2 = 0.9$?

          A: We need $\lambda_2^t \leq 0.01$, so $0.9^t \leq 0.01$. Taking logarithm: $t \geq \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.9)} = \frac{-4.605}{-0.1054} \approx 43.7$. So approximately 44 iterations are needed. Note: a poorly connected graph (larger $\lambda_2$, say 0.99) would require $\frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.99)} \approx 458$ iterations, 10× more. This shows how graph connectivity dramatically affects consensus convergence speed.

        Chapter 7: Negotiation and Conflict Resolution

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Formally model cooperative and competitive negotiation
        • Design and analyze auction mechanisms
        • Apply game theory to conflict resolution
        • Understand trust and reputation systems mathematically

        7.1: Negotiation Models

        Negotiation is a process in which two or more agents reach a mutually acceptable agreement on matters of shared concern.

        7.1.1: Cooperative Negotiation

        In cooperative negotiation, agents act together to maximize joint welfare.

        Utility Possibility Frontier

        The set of all Pareto optimal outcomes traces the Utility Possibility Frontier (UPF):

        $$UPF = \{(U_1(x), U_2(x)) : x \in \mathcal{N}, \text{Pareto optimal}\}$$

        Illustration (2 agents):

        The UPF is the "northwest" boundary of the feasible utility region. Points inside are inefficient; points outside are infeasible.

        Kalai-Smorodinsky Bargaining Solution

        An alternative to Nash's solution: proportionally equalize utility gains:

        $$KS = \text{point on UPF where } \frac{U_1(x) - d_1}{U_2(x) - d_2} = \frac{U_1^{max} - d_1}{U_2^{max} - d_2}$$

        Where $U_i^{max}$ = maximum utility agent $i$ could achieve (if the other agent got their disagreement payoff).

        ZOPA (Zone of Possible Agreement)

        In bilateral negotiation, ZOPA = range of agreements acceptable to both parties:

        $$ZOPA = [\max(d_1, \text{seller_min}), \min(d_2, \text{buyer_max})]$$

        ZOPA is non-empty iff buyer's max > seller's min:

        $$\text{buyer_max} > \text{seller_min} \iff \text{deal is possible}$$

        Surplus to distribute:

        $$\text{Surplus} = \text{buyer_max} - \text{seller_min}$$

        Cooperative negotiation seeks to maximize total surplus (reach the UPF) and then split it fairly.

        Mediated Negotiation

        A mediator agent $M$ helps agents reach a deal:

        1. Agents report their utility functions to $M$ (truthfully or strategically)
        2. $M$ computes the optimal agreement $x^*$ (e.g., Nash solution)
        3. $M$ proposes $x^*$ to both agents

          4. Mechanism Design: Design rules so that truthful reporting is incentive-compatible:

          $$U_i(x^*_{truthful}) \geq U_i(x^*_{reported \neq truthful}) \quad \forall i$$

          If this holds, agents have no incentive to misreport (dominant strategy truthfulness).

          7.1.2: Competitive Negotiation

          In competitive negotiation, agents act strategically to maximize their own utility, possibly at the expense of others.

          Game Theory Foundations

          A strategic game is:

          $$G = \langle N, \{A_i\}_{i \in N}, \{u_i\}_{i \in N} \rangle$$

          • $N$ = set of players
          • $A_i$ = action set of player $i$
          • $u_i : \prod_j A_j \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ = utility function of player $i$

          Nash Equilibrium

          A Nash Equilibrium (NE) is a strategy profile $(a_1^*, \ldots, a_n^*)$ where no agent can improve by unilaterally deviating:

          $$u_i(a_i^*, a_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(a_i, a_{-i}^*) \quad \forall a_i \in A_i, \forall i \in N$$

          Where $a_{-i}^*$ = strategies of all agents except $i$.

          Nash Existence Theorem: Every finite game has at least one Nash equilibrium (possibly in mixed strategies).

          Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium: Player $i$ randomizes over actions with probabilities $\sigma_i \in \Delta(A_i)$:

          $$u_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}^*) \quad \forall \sigma_i$$

          Prisoner's Dilemma

          The classic example of why rational agents fail to cooperate:

          CooperateDefect
          Cooperate(3,3)(0,5)
          Defect(5,0)(1,1)

          Analysis:

          • If the opponent cooperates: Defect gives 5 > 3 (Cooperate) → Defect is better
          • If the opponent defects: Defect gives 1 > 0 (Cooperate) → Defect is better
          • Dominant strategy: Defect regardless of opponent's action
          • Nash Equilibrium: (Defect, Defect) = (1,1)
          • Social Optimum: (Cooperate, Cooperate) = (3,3)
          • Price of Anarchy: $PoA = 3/1 = 3$

          Resolution in MAS:

          • Repeated games: If agents interact repeatedly, cooperation can be sustained by threat of future punishment
          • Folk Theorem: In infinite repeated games, any outcome between individual rationality and Pareto optimality can be a Nash Equilibrium

          Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma

          In the IPD with discount factor $\delta$:

          Grim Trigger Strategy: Cooperate until opponent defects, then defect forever.

          Cooperation is sustainable iff:

          $$\frac{3}{1-\delta} \geq 5 + \frac{\delta}{1-\delta}$$

          $$3 \geq 5(1-\delta) + \delta = 5 - 4\delta$$

          $$\delta \geq \frac{1}{2}$$

          If agents are patient enough ($\delta \geq 0.5$), mutual cooperation is a Nash equilibrium.

          7.1.3: Multi-Issue Negotiation

          Real negotiations involve multiple issues (price, quantity, delivery time, warranty, etc.).

          Issue Space

          The negotiation space is the product of individual issue ranges:

          $$\mathcal{X} = \prod_{j=1}^k [l_j, u_j]$$

          Where $[l_j, u_j]$ = range of issue $j$.

          Agent utility: Additive utility across issues:

          $$U_i(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^k w_{ij} \cdot v_{ij}(x_j)$$

          Where:

          • $w_{ij}$ = weight of issue $j$ for agent $i$ ($\sum_j w_{ij} = 1$)
          • $v_{ij}(x_j)$ = value of outcome $x_j$ on issue $j$ for agent $i$

          Logrolling

          Logrolling (issue trading): Agent $i$ concedes on issues it cares less about in exchange for concessions from agent $j$ on issues $j$ cares less about.

          If $w_{1,price} \gg w_{1,delivery}$ and $w_{2,delivery} \gg w_{2,price}$:

          • Agent 1 accepts lower price importance for better delivery
          • Agent 2 accepts worse delivery for better price

          Both can improve: Pareto improvement via logrolling.

          Formal logrolling condition:

          $$\frac{w_{1,j}}{w_{1,k}} \neq \frac{w_{2,j}}{w_{2,k}} \quad \text{for some issues } j, k$$

          When weights differ, logrolling is possible and beneficial.

          Time Pressure in Negotiation
