          Deadline negotiation: Agent $i$ must reach agreement by deadline $D_i$.

          Concession strategy under time pressure:

          $$x_i(t) = d_i + (x_i(0) - d_i) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t}{D_i}\right)^\beta$$

          Where:

          • $x_i(0)$ = initial offer (aggressive)
          • $d_i$ = reservation value (walk-away point)
          • $\beta > 0$ = concession rate ($\beta > 1$: slow then fast, $\beta < 1$: fast then slow)

          As $t \to D_i$: $x_i(t) \to d_i$ (must make final offer near reservation value).

          FAQs: 7.1

          Q1: In multi-issue negotiation, why are multiple issues better than one issue?

          A: With a single issue (e.g., price only), negotiation is purely zero-sum, every concession from one agent is exactly a gain for the other. This makes agreement hard and leaves no surplus-creating opportunities. With multiple issues and different agent weightings, agents can trade concessions (logrolling), conceding on less-important issues and gaining on more-important ones. This creates Pareto improvements: both agents end up better off than any single-issue solution. Multiple issues transform a zero-sum negotiation into a positive-sum one, increasing the probability of agreement and the joint welfare of the outcome.

        7.2: Auction Mechanisms

        Auctions are structured mechanisms for allocating resources through competitive bidding.

        7.2.1: English Auctions

        The English (ascending) auction is the most familiar auction format.

        Protocol

        1. Auctioneer announces reserve price $r$
        2. Bidders submit increasing bids
        3. Bidding ends when no bidder wishes to raise
        4. Winner: highest bidder, pays their bid

          5. Formal Model

          Let $v_i \sim F$ = agent $i$'s private valuation. The dominant strategy:

          $$\text{Bid up to } v_i \text{ (your true valuation)}$$

          Stop bidding when current price $p \geq v_i$.

          Winner's identity: Bidder with highest valuation $v_{(1)} = \max_i v_i$.

          Winner's payment: Slightly above second-highest valuation $v_{(2)}$ (in the limit).

          $$p^* \approx v_{(2)}$$

          Revenue Analysis

          Expected revenue (symmetric IPV model, $v_i \sim U[0,1]$, $n$ bidders):

          $$\mathbb{E}[\text{Revenue}] = \mathbb{E}[v_{(2)}] = \frac{n-1}{n+1}$$

          For $n=2$: $\mathbb{E}[R] = 1/3$. For $n=10$: $\mathbb{E}[R] = 9/11 \approx 0.818$.

          More bidders → higher expected revenue (more competition).

          7.2.2: Dutch Auctions

          The Dutch (descending) auction starts high and decreases.

          Protocol

          1. Price starts at high value $P_0$ and decreases
          2. First bidder to call "Stop!" wins at current price

            3. Strategic Analysis

            Unlike English auctions, waiting is risky, another bidder may call first.

            Optimal stopping strategy (symmetric case):

            $$\text{Bid (call stop) at price } b(v_i) = v_i - \frac{\int_{\underline{v}}^{v_i} F(x)^{n-1} dx}{F(v_i)^{n-1}}$$

            Where $F$ = CDF of valuations, $n$ = number of bidders.

            Equivalence Theorem: Dutch auction is strategically equivalent to first-price sealed-bid auction.

            Revenue Equivalence Theorem (Vickrey, 1961):

            Under symmetric independent private values, all "standard" auction formats (English, Dutch, first-price, second-price) yield the same expected revenue:

            $$\mathbb{E}[R_{English}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{Dutch}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{first-price}] = \mathbb{E}[R_{second-price}]$$

            7.2.3: Sealed Bid Auctions

            First-Price Sealed-Bid Auction

            All bidders submit a single sealed bid simultaneously. Highest bidder wins and pays their bid.

            Optimal bidding strategy (uniform distribution, $n$ bidders):

            $$b^*(v_i) = \frac{n-1}{n} v_i$$

            Bidders "shade" their bids below their true value. As $n \to \infty$: $b^* \to v_i$ (competitive market).

            Proof: Setting up the symmetric BNE:

            Agent $i$ bids $b(v_i) = \alpha v_i$. Expected payoff:

            $$\pi_i(b, v_i) = (v_i - b) \cdot P(\text{win}) = (v_i - b) \cdot \left(\frac{b}{\alpha}\right)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{1^{n-1}}$$

            Maximizing over $b$: FOC gives $b^* = \frac{n-1}{n} v_i$. ✓

            Second-Price (Vickrey) Sealed-Bid Auction

            Highest bidder wins but pays the second-highest bid.

            Dominant strategy: Bid your true valuation $b_i^* = v_i$.

            Proof: Bidding $v_i$ is a dominant strategy. Suppose bidder $i$ bids $b_i \neq v_i$.

            Case 1: $b_i > v_i$, if this changes the outcome (wins when wouldn't otherwise), pays some $p \leq b_{(1)} \leq b_i$ which may exceed $v_i$, causing loss. No gain from overbidding.

            Case 2: $b_i < v_i$, may miss winning when the second-highest bid $b_j \in [b_i, v_i]$, missing profitable trade ($v_i - b_j > 0$). No gain from underbidding. ✓

            Efficiency: Vickrey auction always allocates to the highest-value agent (efficient).

            Strategy-proofness: Truthful bidding is a dominant strategy (regardless of other bidders' strategies).

            Combinatorial Auction

            Multiple items auctioned simultaneously; bidders can bid on bundles.

            Winner Determination Problem (WDP):

            $$\max_{x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}} \sum_{i \in \text{bidders}} \sum_{S \subseteq \text{items}} b_{iS} \cdot x_{iS}$$

            Subject to: each item in at most one winning bundle.

            WDP is NP-hard, the most computationally challenging aspect of combinatorial auctions.

            Revenue Equivalence in Combinatorial Auctions: Does NOT hold, format matters significantly when there are complementarities and substitutes.

            FAQs: 7.2

            Q1: A seller chooses between English and Dutch auctions. Under the Revenue Equivalence Theorem, which should they use? What factors break the equivalence?

            A: Under the RET's assumptions (symmetric, risk-neutral, independent private values), neither format is better, both yield identical expected revenue. However, the RET breaks when: (1) Risk aversion, risk-averse bidders shade less in first-price/Dutch auctions, increasing revenue over second-price/English; (2) Correlated values, if bids are correlated, English auctions (where bids reveal information) yield higher revenue; (3) Asymmetric bidders, different distributions of valuations break symmetry; (4) Resale, if the winner can resell, strategies change. In practice, English auctions are common for unique/art items (where information release matters), while Dutch auctions are used for perishables (speed is critical).

            Q2: Why is truthful bidding in Vickrey auctions a DOMINANT strategy, not just a Nash Equilibrium strategy?

            A: A Nash Equilibrium strategy is optimal only given others' strategies. A dominant strategy is optimal regardless of what others do. In the Vickrey auction, bidding $v_i$ is weakly better than any other bid $b_i \neq v_i$ for every possible combination of other bidders' bids, not just against their equilibrium strategies. This is a stronger property: even if you knew the other bids in advance, you would still bid $v_i$. This makes Vickrey auctions "strategy-proof" and resistant to strategic manipulation.

        7.3: Conflict Resolution

        7.3.1: Mediation Techniques

        Mediation involves a neutral third-party agent (the mediator) helping conflicting agents reach an agreement.

        Types of Conflict

        TypeDefinitionFormula
        Resource ConflictTwo agents want the same resource$A_i, A_j$ both want $R$; $|R| = 1$
        Goal ConflictGoals are mutually exclusive$G_i \wedge G_j \equiv \text{False}$
        Belief ConflictAgents hold contradictory beliefs$B_i \phi \wedge B_j \neg\phi$
        Preference ConflictAgents prefer different outcomes$\text{rank}_i(x) \neq \text{rank}_j(x)$

        Mediator Strategies

        1. Compromise:

        Find agreement $x^*$ equidistant from both agents' ideal points:

        $$x^* = \frac{x_i^{ideal} + x_j^{ideal}}{2}$$

        Minimizes maximum dissatisfaction: $\min_{x} \max_i d(x, x_i^{ideal})$

        2. Trade-off Identification:

        Mediator identifies issues where logrolling is possible:

        $$\text{Logroll}(i \rightarrow j \text{ on issue } k) \iff \frac{\Delta U_j}{|\Delta x_k|} > \frac{\Delta U_i}{|\Delta x_k|}$$

        Agent $j$ gains more than agent $i$ loses per unit of concession on issue $k$.

        3. Package Deal:

        Mediator proposes a complete agreement package that makes both agents better off than their threat points:

        $$x^{package} : U_i(x^{package}) > d_i \text{ and } U_j(x^{package}) > d_j$$

        4. Single Negotiating Text (SNT):

        Mediator creates a draft agreement and iteratively refines it based on agents' objections.

        Formal Mediation Protocol

        1. Agents submit preferences (utility functions) to mediator
        2. Mediator computes feasible agreement set:

          3. $$\mathcal{F} = \{x \in \mathcal{N} : U_i(x) \geq d_i \forall i\}$$

          1. Mediator selects optimal agreement:

            2. $$x^* = \arg\max_{x \in \mathcal{F}} SW(x) \text{ (social welfare criterion)}$$

            1. Agents accept or reject $x^*$

              2. 7.3.2: Arbitration

              Arbitration is binding conflict resolution by a third party, unlike mediation (which is advisory), the arbitrator's decision is final.

              Arbitration Schemes

              1. Final Offer Arbitration (FOA):

              Each agent submits a final offer. The arbitrator picks the offer closest to "fair":

              $$x^{arb} = \begin{cases} x_i^{final} & \text{if } |x_i^{final} - x^{fair}| < |x_j^{final} - x^{fair}| \\ x_j^{final} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

              Strategic effect: Agents are incentivized to make reasonable offers (not extreme), since extreme offers lose to the opponent's offer.

              Convergence to truth: If $x^{fair} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ (uncertain to agents):

              • Agent $i$ with value $v_i$ sets $x_i^{final}$ to maximize probability of winning:

              $$x_i^{final} = v_i + \sigma \cdot \Phi^{-1}\left(P(v_i \text{ is fair})\right)$$

              Where $\Phi^{-1}$ = inverse normal CDF.

              2. Conventional Arbitration:

              Arbitrator chooses any outcome, not limited to agents' offers. The arbitrator maximizes social welfare within fairness constraints.

              3. Super-Final-Offer Arbitration (SFOA):

              Agents submit offers, arbitrator picks one, but can also impose penalties for unreasonable offers, incentivizes truthfulness.

              7.3.3: Trust and Reputation

              Trust is the subjective belief that an agent will behave as expected. Reputation is the public perception of an agent's trustworthiness.

              Beta Reputation System

              Model trust as a Beta distribution, the natural conjugate prior for Bernoulli outcomes:

              $$\text{Trust}(A_j \rightarrow A_i) \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_{ij}, \beta_{ij})$$

              Where:

              • $\alpha_{ij}$ = number of positive interactions between $A_i$ and $A_j$ (past cooperations)
              • $\beta_{ij}$ = number of negative interactions (past defections)

              Expected trustworthiness:

              $$\mathbb{E}[\text{Trust}_{ij}] = \frac{\alpha_{ij}}{\alpha_{ij} + \beta_{ij}}$$

              Uncertainty about trust:

              $$\text{Var}[\text{Trust}_{ij}] = \frac{\alpha_{ij} \beta_{ij}}{(\alpha_{ij} + \beta_{ij})^2 (\alpha_{ij} + \beta_{ij} + 1)}$$

              Update rule after a new interaction:

              • Positive outcome: $\alpha_{ij} \leftarrow \alpha_{ij} + 1$
              • Negative outcome: $\beta_{ij} \leftarrow \beta_{ij} + 1$

              Reputation Propagation

              Agent $A_k$ can compute trust in $A_j$ using recommendations from $A_i$:

              $$\text{Trust}_{kj} = \text{Trust}_{ki} \cdot \text{Trust}_{ij} \text{ (discounted recommendation)}$$

              Full Bayesian reputation:

              $$\text{Trust}_{kj} = \int_0^1 t \cdot P(t | \text{observations}_{kj}, \text{recommendations}) dt$$

              Sybil Attack and Trust

              In open MAS, malicious agents create multiple fake identities (Sybil attack) to manipulate reputation:

              $$\text{Sybil advantage} = \frac{k \cdot \text{positive ratings from fake IDs}}{\text{total ratings}}$$

              Defense: Proof of work, social network-based identity verification, centralized PKI.

              EigenTrust Algorithm

              Used in peer-to-peer systems. Local trust $c_{ij}$ (agent $i$'s direct trust in $j$):

              $$c_{ij} = \frac{\max(s_{ij}, 0)}{\sum_j \max(s_{ij}, 0)}$$

              Where $s_{ij}$ = satisfaction score from direct interactions.

              Global trust $t_j$ = EigenTrust vector (stationary distribution of trust propagation):

              $$\mathbf{t} = C^T \mathbf{t} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad t_j = \sum_i c_{ij} t_i$$

              This is the left eigenvector of the trust matrix $C$ corresponding to eigenvalue 1, analogous to PageRank.

              Iterative computation:

              $$\mathbf{t}^{(k+1)} = C^T \mathbf{t}^{(k)}$$

              Converges to the principal eigenvector.

              FAQs: 7.3

              Q1: An agent has interacted with peer $j$ 20 times: 15 positive, 5 negative. Compute expected trust and its 95% confidence interval.

              A: $\alpha = 15, \beta = 5$ (adding prior of 1 each: $\alpha = 16, \beta = 6$ for a non-informative prior).

              $\mathbb{E}[Trust] = \frac{16}{16+6} = \frac{16}{22} \approx 0.727$

              $\text{Var} = \frac{16 \times 6}{22^2 \times 23} = \frac{96}{11132} \approx 0.00862$, $\text{SD} \approx 0.0929$

              95% CI using Beta distribution quantiles: approximately $(0.54, 0.88)$.

              Interpretation: We're 95% confident the agent's true cooperation probability lies between 54% and 88%. After more interactions, this interval will narrow.

              Q2: Why is EigenTrust more robust against collusion than simple average reputation?

              A: Simple average reputation: any agent can boost another's reputation by repeatedly rating them positively. A group of $k$ colluding agents can give each other $k$ positive ratings, artificially inflating reputations proportional to group size. EigenTrust weights recommendations by the recommender's own global reputation, a high-trust agent's recommendation counts more. Colluding agents have low global trust (they only trust each other), so their mutual recommendations carry little weight in the global trust computation. The eigenvector structure naturally suppresses the influence of isolated trust clusters.

        Chapter 8: Knowledge Representation

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Understand and apply formal knowledge representation methods
        • Model agent knowledge using semantic networks, frames, and ontologies
        • Work with semantic web technologies (RDF, OWL, SPARQL)
        • Design effective knowledge sharing architectures for MAS

        8.1: Knowledge Representation Methods

        Knowledge Representation (KR) is the field of AI that studies how to formally represent information about the world so that computer agents can use it for reasoning.

        8.1.1: Rule-Based Systems

        Already introduced in Chapter 2, we now study rule-based KR from a representational perspective.

        Horn Clauses

        Horn clauses are the foundation of logic programming (Prolog):

        $$A_1 \wedge A_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge A_k \Rightarrow B$$

        Where $B$ is the conclusion and $A_1, \ldots, A_k$ are conditions (all are atoms, no negation in head).

        Three types:

        1. Fact: $\top \Rightarrow B$ (unconditionally true, written just as $B$)
        2. Rule: $A_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge A_k \Rightarrow B$ ($k \geq 1$)
        3. Goal: $A_1 \wedge \cdots \wedge A_k \Rightarrow \bot$ (query, written as $\leftarrow A_1, \ldots, A_k$)

          4. Resolution: Given rule $A \Rightarrow B$ and fact $A$, derive $B$. This is Modus Ponens:

          $$\frac{A \Rightarrow B \quad A}{B}$$

          Unification: Two atoms $p(X, b)$ and $p(a, Y)$ unify with substitution $\theta = \{X/a, Y/b\}$:

          $$p(X, b)[\theta] = p(a, b) = p(a, Y)[\theta]$$

          SLD Resolution (Prolog): Backward chaining using unification:

          1. Goal: $\leftarrow g(X)$
          2. Find rule: $b(X) \Rightarrow g(X)$
          3. New goal: $\leftarrow b(X)$
          4. Repeat until empty goal (success) or no rules apply (failure)

            5. Expressiveness vs. Tractability

            LogicExpressivenessDecidabilityComplexity
            PropositionalLowDecidableNP-complete (SAT)
            Horn clausesModerateDecidableP (linear)
            First-orderHighSemi-decidableUndecidable
            Description LogicsHighDecidableEXPTIME

            Horn clauses offer a sweet spot: reasonably expressive AND efficiently computable.

            8.1.2: Semantic Networks

            Semantic networks represent knowledge as a graph of concepts and relationships.

            Formal Definition

            A semantic network is a directed labeled graph:

            $$SN = \langle C, R, E \rangle$$

            Where:

            • $C$ = set of concept nodes (entities, classes, properties)
            • $R$ = set of relation types (IS-A, PART-OF, HAS-PROPERTY, etc.)
            • $E \subseteq C \times R \times C$ = set of edges (labeled arcs)

            Each triple $(c_1, r, c_2)$ means: "concept $c_1$ has relation $r$ to concept $c_2$"

            Example:

            • (Dog, IS-A, Animal): Dog is a type of Animal
            • (Dog, HAS-PROPERTY, Fur): Dogs have fur
            • (Spot, INSTANCE-OF, Dog): Spot is a dog

            Inheritance Reasoning

            In a semantic network with IS-A hierarchy, properties inherit up/down:

            $$\frac{(c, \text{IS-A}, c') \quad (c', \text{HAS-PROPERTY}, p)}{(c, \text{HAS-PROPERTY}, p)}$$

            Transitivity of IS-A:

            $$\frac{(c_1, \text{IS-A}, c_2) \quad (c_2, \text{IS-A}, c_3)}{(c_1, \text{IS-A}, c_3)}$$

            Spreading Activation:

            In semantic networks, reasoning is often done by spreading activation:

            • Activate source node with strength $S_0 = 1$
            • Activation spreads to neighbors, decaying:

            $$S(c, t+1) = \sum_{c' : (c', r, c) \in E} \frac{S(c', t)}{\text{out-degree}(c')} \cdot w_r$$

            Where $w_r$ = weight of relation type $r$.

            Nodes with high activation after spreading are conceptually related to the source.

            Path-Based Similarity

            Wu-Palmer similarity between concepts $c_1$ and $c_2$ in an IS-A hierarchy:

            $$\text{sim}(c_1, c_2) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{depth}(LCS)}{\text{depth}(c_1) + \text{depth}(c_2)}$$

            Where LCS = Lowest Common Subsumer (deepest common ancestor in IS-A hierarchy).

            8.1.3: Frames and Ontologies

            Frames

            Frames (Minsky, 1975) represent knowledge as structured objects (like Python dictionaries with defaults):

            $$\text{Frame}(\text{Dog}) = \begin{cases} \text{type}: & \text{Mammal} \\ \text{legs}: & 4 \text{ (default)} \\ \text{sound}: & \text{bark (default)} \\ \text{color}: & \text{?} \text{ (unknown)} \\ \text{owner}: & \text{?} \end{cases}$$

            Slots: Named attributes of the frame (type, legs, sound, ...)

            Fillers: Values of slots (Mammal, 4, bark, ...)

            Default reasoning: If a slot value is unknown, use the default:

            $$\text{value}(c, s) = \begin{cases} \text{explicit filler of } s \text{ in } c & \text{if exists} \\ \text{inherited value from parent class} & \text{else if exists} \\ \text{default value of } s & \text{else} \\ \text{unknown} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

            Ontologies

            An ontology is a formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization.

            $$\text{Ontology} = \langle \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{I}, \mathcal{A} \rangle$$

            • $\mathcal{C}$: Classes (concepts) e.g., Person, Organization, Product
            • $\mathcal{P}$: Properties (relations between classes) e.g., worksFor, hasColor
            • $\mathcal{I}$: Individuals (instances of classes) e.g., "Alice", "Google"
            • $\mathcal{A}$: Axioms (logical constraints) e.g., every Person has exactly one birthdate

            Description Logic (DL)

            Ontologies are formally specified in Description Logic (DL):

            Concept constructors (ALC logic):

            • Atomic concept: $A$ (e.g., Person)
            • Intersection: $A \sqcap B$ (Person AND Employee)
            • Union: $A \sqcup B$ (Doctor OR Lawyer)
            • Negation: $\neg A$ (not a Child)
            • Existential restriction: $\exists R.C$ (has some child who is a Doctor)
            • Universal restriction: $\forall R.C$ (all children are Doctors)

            Terminological Box (TBox) class definitions:

            $$\text{Parent} \equiv \text{Person} \sqcap \exists \text{hasChild.Person}$$

            (A Parent is a Person who has at least one child who is also a Person)

            Assertional Box (ABox) individual assertions:

            $$\text{Person}(\text{Alice}), \text{hasChild}(\text{Alice}, \text{Bob})$$

            Reasoning tasks:

            • Satisfiability: Is concept $C$ non-empty?
            • Subsumption: Is $C \sqsubseteq D$ (is every $C$ also a $D$)?
            • Instance checking: Is $a$ an instance of $C$?
            • Retrieval: Find all instances of $C$

            Complexity (ALC): All tasks are EXPTIME-complete.

            FAQs: 8.1

            Q1: What is the difference between a semantic network and an ontology?

            A: A semantic network is an informal knowledge representation, a graph of concepts and relations, typically without formal semantics or reasoning guarantees. An ontology is a formal, logically precise specification with: (1) Formal semantics (model-theoretic meaning); (2) Reasoning support (entailment, consistency checking); (3) Expressiveness declarations (what kinds of knowledge can/cannot be expressed); (4) Interoperability (standard languages like OWL enable sharing). A semantic network might say "(Dog, IS-A, Animal)" informally; an ontology would say this in OWL with axioms that entail formal logical consequences. Ontologies can be checked for consistency and used for automated reasoning; semantic networks are typically used for human understanding or simple inference.

            Q2: Compute Wu-Palmer similarity between "Dog" and "Cat" given: Animal(depth 1) → Mammal(2) → Dog(3) and Mammal(2) → Cat(3).

            A: LCS of Dog and Cat = Mammal (their deepest common ancestor). $\text{depth}(LCS) = \text{depth(Mammal)} = 2$. $\text{depth}(Dog) = 3$, $\text{depth}(Cat) = 3$. $\text{sim}(Dog, Cat) = \frac{2 \times 2}{3 + 3} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$. For comparison, $\text{sim}(Dog, Animal) = \frac{2 \times 1}{3 + 1} = 0.5$, Dog is less similar to Animal (its grandparent) than to Cat (its sibling).

        8.2: Semantic Web Technologies

        8.2.1: RDF (Resource Description Framework)

        RDF is the foundational data model of the Semantic Web, a way to represent knowledge as triples.

        RDF Triple Model

        Every RDF statement is a triple:

        $$\text{Triple} = (\text{Subject}, \text{Predicate}, \text{Object})$$

        $$\langle s, p, o \rangle \in \text{URI} \times \text{URI} \times (\text{URI} \cup \text{Literal})$$

        Example triples:

        • ⟨Alice, rdf:type, foaf:Person⟩ : Alice is a Person
        • ⟨Alice, foaf:name, "Alice Smith"⟩ : Alice's name is "Alice Smith"
        • ⟨Alice, foaf:knows, Bob⟩ : Alice knows Bob

        An RDF graph is a set of triples:

        $$G = \{(s_1, p_1, o_1), (s_2, p_2, o_2), \ldots\}$$

        RDF Graph Semantics

        Interpretation $\mathcal{I}$ assigns meaning:

        • $\text{dom}(\mathcal{I})$ = domain of discourse
        • $\mathcal{I}^{ext}: \text{URI} \rightarrow \text{dom}(\mathcal{I})$ = extension mapping

        A triple $(s, p, o)$ is true in $\mathcal{I}$ iff:

        $$(\mathcal{I}^{ext}(s), \mathcal{I}^{ext}(o)) \in \mathcal{I}^{ext}(p)$$

        RDF Entailment

        $G_1$ entails $G_2$ ($G_1 \models G_2$) iff every interpretation satisfying $G_1$ also satisfies $G_2$.

        RDF entailment rules (subset):

        RuleAntecedentConsequent
        rdf1$\langle s, p, o \rangle$$\langle p, \text{rdf:type}, \text{rdf:Property} \rangle$
        rdf2$\langle s, p, o \rangle$ if $o$ is a literal$\langle o, \text{rdf:type}, \text{rdf:XMLLiteral} \rangle$
        rdfs1$\langle D, \text{rdfs:subClassOf}, C \rangle \langle a, \text{rdf:type}, D \rangle$$\langle a, \text{rdf:type}, C \rangle$
        rdfs2$\langle C, \text{rdfs:subClassOf}, D \rangle \langle D, \text{rdfs:subClassOf}, E \rangle$$\langle C, \text{rdfs:subClassOf}, E \rangle$

        Named Graphs and Dataset

        An RDF Dataset contains multiple named graphs:

        $$\text{Dataset} = \{(n_1, G_1), (n_2, G_2), \ldots\} \cup G_{default}$$

        Enables separate tracking of provenance and contexts.

        8.2.2: OWL (Web Ontology Language)

        OWL extends RDF with richer expressiveness using Description Logics.

        OWL Profiles

        ProfileLogicComplexityUse Case
        OWL Lite$\mathcal{SHIF}(D)$EXPTIMESimple hierarchies
        OWL DL$\mathcal{SHOIN}(D)$NEXPTIMEFull ontologies
        OWL FullBeyond DLUndecidableMaximum expressivity
        OWL 2 EL$\mathcal{EL}^{++}$PLarge biomedical ontologies
        OWL 2 QL$DL$-LiteNLogSpaceDatabase integration
        OWL 2 RL$\mathcal{RL}$PRule-based reasoning

        Key OWL Constructs

        Class axioms:

        $$A \sqsubseteq B \text{ (rdfs:subClassOf)} \quad \text{every A is a B}$$

        $$A \equiv B \text{ (owl:equivalentClass)} \quad \text{A and B have same members}$$

        $$A \sqcap B \sqsubseteq \bot \text{ (owl:disjointWith)} \quad \text{A and B share no members}$$

        Property axioms:

        $$R \sqsubseteq S \text{ (rdfs:subPropertyOf)}$$

        $$R^{-} \text{ (owl:inverseOf)}$$

        $$\text{Functional}(R): \forall x,y,z : R(x,y) \wedge R(x,z) \Rightarrow y=z$$

        $$\text{Transitive}(R): R(x,y) \wedge R(y,z) \Rightarrow R(x,z)$$

        Complex class expressions (OWL 2):

        $$\exists R.C \text{ (owl:someValuesFrom)}$$

        $$\forall R.C \text{ (owl:allValuesFrom)}$$

        $$\geq n R.C \text{ (owl:minCardinality)}$$

        $$\leq n R.C \text{ (owl:maxCardinality)}$$

        Example OWL axiom in DL:

        $$\text{Professor} \equiv \text{Person} \sqcap \exists \text{worksAt.University} \sqcap \forall \text{teaches.Course}$$

        (A Professor is a Person who works at some University and teaches only Courses)

        OWL Reasoning

        Standard OWL reasoning tasks and their complexity for OWL DL:

        TaskComplexity
        ConsistencyNEXPTIME
        SubsumptionNEXPTIME
        Instance retrievalNEXPTIME
        RealizationNEXPTIME

        In practice, highly optimized reasoners (Pellet, HermiT, FaCT++) handle most real-world ontologies efficiently.

        8.2.3: SPARQL

        SPARQL is the query language for RDF knowledge graphs.

        SPARQL Query Structure

        SELECT ?x ?y
WHERE {
  ?x rdf:type foaf:Person .
  ?x foaf:knows ?y .
  ?y foaf:name "Bob" .
}

        This query finds all persons $x$ who know someone named Bob.

        SPARQL Formal Semantics

        A SPARQL graph pattern $P$ is evaluated against an RDF graph $G$:

        $$\llbracket P \rrbracket_G = \{\mu : \text{var}(P) \rightarrow \text{RDF Terms}\}$$

        A solution mapping $\mu$ maps variables to RDF terms such that $\mu(P) \subseteq G$.

        Triple pattern: $(s, p, o)$ where some of $s, p, o$ may be variables:

        $$\llbracket (s, p, o) \rrbracket_G = \{\mu : \mu(s,p,o) \in G\}$$

        Join (AND):

        $$\llbracket P_1 \text{ AND } P_2 \rrbracket_G = \llbracket P_1 \rrbracket_G \bowtie \llbracket P_2 \rrbracket_G$$

        Where $\bowtie$ is the compatible join (maps that agree on shared variables).

        Optional (LEFT JOIN):

        $$\llbracket P_1 \text{ OPT } P_2 \rrbracket_G = \llbracket P_1 \rrbracket_G \leftarrow \llbracket P_2 \rrbracket_G$$

        Union (OR):

        $$\llbracket P_1 \text{ UNION } P_2 \rrbracket_G = \llbracket P_1 \rrbracket_G \cup \llbracket P_2 \rrbracket_G$$

        Filter:

        $$\llbracket \text{FILTER}(C, P) \rrbracket_G = \{\mu \in \llbracket P \rrbracket_G : \mu \text{ satisfies condition } C\}$$

        SPARQL Query Types

        Query FormDescriptionExample Use
        SELECTReturn variable bindingsGet all agents and their capabilities
        CONSTRUCTReturn new RDF graphTransform/merge knowledge graphs
        ASKReturn booleanCheck if fact exists
        DESCRIBEReturn descriptionGet all info about a resource

        SPARQL Update (SPARQL/Update)

        Modify RDF graphs:

        INSERT DATA {
  <Agent_1> <hasCapability> <NLP_Processing> .
}
DELETE DATA {
  <Agent_1> <status> "offline" .
}

        FAQs: 8.2

        Q1: Why does OWL 2 EL restrict expressiveness, and what is gained?

        A: OWL 2 EL restricts to the $\mathcal{EL}^{++}$ description logic, which allows: existential restrictions ($\exists R.C$), conjunction ($C \sqcap D$), top and bottom ($\top, \bot$), role chains ($R_1 \circ R_2 \sqsubseteq R_3$), and concrete domains. It prohibits: disjunction ($\sqcup$), negation ($\neg$), universal restrictions ($\forall$), and number restrictions. Gain: Reasoning (subsumption, classification) becomes polynomial in the size of the ontology, vs. NEXPTIME for full OWL DL. This enables classification of ontologies with millions of classes (like SNOMED CT with 350,000+ concepts) in seconds. The trade-off is reduced expressiveness, some axioms cannot be stated, but for many biomedical and scientific ontologies, EL expressiveness is sufficient.

        Q2: Write a SPARQL query to find all agents in a MAS knowledge graph that have capability "Planning" and are currently "active".

        A:

        ```sparql

        PREFIX mas:

        SELECT ?agent ?capability

        WHERE {

        ?agent rdf:type mas:Agent .

        ?agent mas:hasCapability ?capability .

        ?capability rdf:type mas:PlanningCapability .

        ?agent mas:status "active" .

        }

        ORDER BY ?agent

        ```

        This triple pattern joins four constraints: the agent must be of type Agent, have a capability that is a Planning capability, and have status "active". SPARQL evaluates this as a compatible join of four triple patterns.

        8.3: Knowledge Sharing

        8.3.1: Shared Knowledge Bases

        A shared knowledge base (KB) is a common repository accessible by all agents in a MAS.

        Architecture

        $$KB_{shared} = \langle \mathcal{F}, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{Q} \rangle$$

        • $\mathcal{F}$ = set of facts (asserted knowledge)
        • $\mathcal{R}$ = set of rules (derived knowledge)
        • $\mathcal{Q}$ = query interface (how agents access knowledge)

        Access protocol:

        $$\text{Agent}_i \xrightarrow{\text{assert}(\phi)} KB_{shared}$$

        $$\text{Agent}_i \xrightarrow{\text{query}(q)} KB_{shared} \xrightarrow{\text{answer}(\{\phi : q(\phi)\})} \text{Agent}_i$$

        Consistency Management

        Multiple agents asserting facts may create inconsistencies:

        $$\exists \phi_1, \phi_2 \in \mathcal{F} : \phi_1 \wedge \phi_2 \equiv \bot \text{ (inconsistency)}$$

        Revision strategies (AGM belief revision):

        Three operations: Expansion ($+$), Contraction ($-$), Revision ($*$):

        Levi Identity: Revision = Contraction + Expansion:

        $$\mathcal{F} * \phi = (\mathcal{F} - \neg\phi) + \phi$$

        AGM Postulates for belief revision:

        1. $(\mathcal{F} * \phi)$ is a consistent belief set
        2. $\phi \in (\mathcal{F} * \phi)$ (the new fact is included)
        3. $(\mathcal{F} * \phi) \subseteq (\mathcal{F} + \phi)$ (revision is at most expansion)
        4. If $\neg\phi \notin \mathcal{F}$: $(\mathcal{F} * \phi) = (\mathcal{F} + \phi)$ (if consistent, just add)
        5. $\mathcal{F} * \phi = \mathcal{F} * \psi$ if $\phi \equiv \psi$ (equivalent inputs, equivalent results)

          6. Knowledge Base Partitioning

          For scalability, the KB is partitioned:

          $$KB_{shared} = KB_1 \cup KB_2 \cup \cdots \cup KB_k$$

          Agent $i$ has access rights to partition $KB_j$ based on role:

          $$\text{Access}(A_i,

        Chapter 9: Machine Learning in Multi-Agent Systems

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

        • Formally derive and apply Q-Learning and its convergence properties
        • Design reward functions that align agent behavior with system goals
        • Balance exploration and exploitation using principled strategies
        • Extend single-agent RL to cooperative and competitive multi-agent settings
        • Understand and apply swarm intelligence algorithms with full mathematical detail

        9.1: Reinforcement Learning

        Reinforcement Learning (RL) is the study of how an agent learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment to maximize a cumulative reward signal.

        The central challenge is that the agent receives no direct instructions about what to do. It must discover good behavior through trial, error, and feedback.

        9.1.1: Q-Learning

        The RL Framework Revisited

        Formally, the agent-environment interaction follows a Markov Decision Process:

        $$\mathcal{M} = \langle S, A, T, R, \gamma \rangle$$

        At each time step $t$:

        1. Agent observes state $s_t \in S$
        2. Agent selects action $a_t \in A$
        3. Environment transitions to $s_{t+1} \sim T(\cdot | s_t, a_t)$
        4. Agent receives reward $r_t = R(s_t, a_t, s_{t+1})$

          5. The agent's goal: find policy $\pi^* : S \to A$ that maximizes:

          $$J(\pi) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_t \right]$$

          The Q-Function

          The action-value function (Q-function) under policy $\pi$ is:

          $$Q^{\pi}(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k r_{t+k} \,\Big|\, s_t = s,\, a_t = a \right]$$

          The optimal Q-function satisfies the Bellman optimality equation:

          $$Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'} \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma \max_{a' \in A} Q^*(s', a') \right]$$

          This is a system of $|S| \times |A|$ equations. The optimal policy is recovered as:

          $$\pi^*(s) = \arg\max_{a \in A} Q^*(s, a)$$

          Temporal Difference Learning

          Temporal Difference (TD) error is the discrepancy between current Q-estimate and a better estimate:

          $$\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)$$

          The TD error decomposes into three parts:

          • $r_{t+1}$ = actual reward just received
          • $\gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a')$ = bootstrapped estimate of future value
          • $Q(s_t, a_t)$ = current estimate being corrected

          Q-Learning Update Rule

          $$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \, \delta_t$$

          $$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

          Where $\alpha \in (0, 1]$ is the learning rate.

          Convergence Conditions (Watkins and Dayan, 1992)

          Q-Learning converges to $Q^*$ with probability 1 if and only if:

          Condition 1 (Exploration): Every state-action pair is visited infinitely often:

          $$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \mathbf{1}[s_t = s, a_t = a] = \infty \quad \forall (s, a) \in S \times A$$

          Condition 2 (Learning rate decay):

          $$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a) = \infty \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \alpha_t(s, a)^2 < \infty$$

          A schedule satisfying this: $\alpha_t = \frac{1}{t^{0.6}}$ or $\alpha_t = \frac{c}{c + N_t(s,a)}$ where $N_t(s,a)$ is the visit count.

          Convergence Rate Analysis

          After $T$ iterations with a fixed learning rate $\alpha$:

          $$\|Q_T - Q^*\|_{\infty} \leq \left(1 - \alpha(1 - \gamma)\right)^T \|Q_0 - Q^*\|_{\infty} + \frac{\alpha R_{max}}{1 - \gamma}$$

          This shows a bias-variance tradeoff:

          • Small $\alpha$: slow convergence, small asymptotic bias
          • Large $\alpha$: fast initial progress, large asymptotic noise

          Tabular Q-Learning: Full Algorithm

          Initialize $Q(s, a) = 0$ for all $(s, a)$. Set episode counter $e = 1$.

          For each episode:

          1. Observe initial state $s_0$
          2. For each step $t$:

            3. - Select $a_t$ using exploration policy (see 9.1.3)

            - Execute $a_t$, observe $r_{t+1}$ and $s_{t+1}$

            - Update: $Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha [r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s_{t+1}, a') - Q(s_t, a_t)]$

            - $s_t \leftarrow s_{t+1}$

            1. Repeat until convergence

              2. SARSA: On-Policy Alternative

              SARSA updates using the action actually taken, not the greedy action:

              $$Q(s_t, a_t) \leftarrow Q(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma Q(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) - Q(s_t, a_t) \right]$$

              The name comes from the quintuple: $(S_t, A_t, R_{t+1}, S_{t+1}, A_{t+1})$.

              Q-Learning vs SARSA:

              PropertyQ-LearningSARSA
              PolicyOff-policyOn-policy
              Convergence target$Q^*$ (optimal)$Q^{\pi}$ (current policy)
              SafetyCan be unsafe (explores freely)Safer (considers actual behavior)
              Cliff-walkingTakes risky shortcutAvoids cliff edge

              Deep Q-Network (DQN)

              For large or continuous state spaces, approximate $Q$ with a neural network:

              $$Q(s, a; \theta) \approx Q^*(s, a)$$

              DQN loss function:

              $$L(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a,r,s') \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ \left( r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a'; \theta^-) - Q(s, a; \theta) \right)^2 \right]$$

              Key innovations of DQN:

              • Experience Replay: Store transitions $(s, a, r, s')$ in replay buffer $\mathcal{D}$, sample mini-batches randomly
              • Target Network: Use separate parameters $\theta^-$ (updated slowly) for bootstrap targets

              Replay buffer benefit: Breaks temporal correlations, converts correlated sequential data into approximately i.i.d. training samples.

              Q and A 9.1.1

              Q1: Why does Q-Learning require off-policy data? Can it learn from a fixed dataset?

              A: Q-Learning is off-policy because the update uses $\max_{a'} Q(s', a')$, which is the greedy action, regardless of what action the behavior policy actually chose. This means Q-Learning can learn from any data, even data generated by a completely different policy. In principle, yes, it can learn from a fixed offline dataset. However, without the exploration condition (visiting all state-action pairs), it cannot guarantee convergence to $Q^*$. The quality of offline Q-Learning depends on how well the fixed dataset covers the state-action space.

              Q2: Prove that the TD error is zero in expectation when $Q = Q^*$.

              A: By definition, $Q^*$ satisfies the Bellman equation: $Q^*(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{s'}[R(s,a,s') + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')]$. The expected TD error is $\mathbb{E}[\delta_t | s_t = s, a_t = a] = \mathbb{E}[r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s_{t+1}, a') - Q^*(s_t, a_t)] = Q^*(s,a) - Q^*(s,a) = 0$. Therefore $Q^*$ is a fixed point of the expected TD update, meaning learning stops exactly at the optimal Q-function.

              Q3: What is the computational cost of maintaining a full Q-table for a problem with 1000 states and 50 actions?

              A: The Q-table requires $|S| \times |A| = 1000 \times 50 = 50{,}000$ entries. Each entry is a floating-point number (8 bytes), so total memory is $50{,}000 \times 8 = 400{,}000$ bytes or approximately 400 KB. For each step, one lookup and one update are required: $O(1)$ time. The real challenge is not memory but coverage: to visit all 50,000 entries even once requires at least 50,000 steps, and convergence typically requires many visits per entry.

        9.1.2: Reward Functions

        The reward function is the most critical design choice in RL. It encodes what the agent should optimize.

        Formal Properties of Reward Functions

        A reward function $R : S \times A \times S \to \mathbb{R}$ should satisfy:

        Completeness: Every desirable behavior is incentivized.

        Consistency: The optimal policy under $R$ achieves the designer's true goal $G$:

        $$\pi^*(R) \text{ achieves } G$$

        Shaping compatibility: $R$ should not introduce spurious optima that distract the agent.

        Reward Shaping

        Potential-Based Reward Shaping (Ng, Harada, Russell 1999):

        Add a shaping term $F(s, a, s')$ to the original reward $R$:

        $$R'(s, a, s') = R(s, a, s') + F(s, a, s')$$

        The shaping term must be potential-based:

        $$F(s, a, s') = \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$$

        Where $\Phi : S \to \mathbb{R}$ is a potential function.

        Policy Invariance Theorem: If $F$ is potential-based, then:

        $$\pi^*(R') = \pi^*(R)$$

        The shaped reward has exactly the same optimal policy as the original reward. Shaping only affects the learning speed, not the final solution.

        Example: If $\Phi(s)$ = distance from goal (negative), then the shaping term rewards moving toward the goal.

        Reward Function Pathologies

        Reward Hacking: The agent finds unexpected ways to maximize $R$ that do not achieve the true goal $G$:

        $$\pi^*(R) \not\to G \quad \text{even though} \quad R \text{ was designed to capture } G$$

        Example: A cleaning robot is rewarded for reporting no mess. Optimal policy: disable its own sensors.

        Sparse Rewards: $R(s, a, s') = 0$ for most transitions, $R = 1$ only at goal. The agent receives almost no feedback:

        $$P(r_t > 0) = \frac{|\text{goal states}|}{|S|} \ll 1$$

        Solution: reward shaping, curiosity-driven exploration, or hierarchical RL.

        Dense but Misaligned Rewards: Frequent rewards that do not align with the true objective:

        $$\mathbb{E}[\sum_t r_t \text{ (dense)}] \neq \text{True Objective}$$

        Example: A robot rewarded per step taken, leading it to spin in circles rather than reach the goal.

        Intrinsic Motivation

        Add an intrinsic reward $r^i_t$ to the extrinsic reward $r^e_t$:

        $$r_t = r^e_t + \beta \cdot r^i_t$$

        Where $\beta > 0$ controls the relative importance.

        Count-based Exploration Bonus:

        $$r^i_t = \frac{\hat{\sigma}(s_t)}{\sqrt{N(s_t)}}$$

        Where $N(s_t)$ = visit count and $\hat{\sigma}$ = estimated novelty of the state.

        Curiosity-Driven Exploration (ICM):

        $$r^i_t = \|\hat{s}_{t+1} - s_{t+1}\|^2$$

        Where $\hat{s}_{t+1}$ = state predicted by a learned forward dynamics model. States that are hard to predict (novel or complex) yield high intrinsic reward.

        Inverse Reward Learning (IRL)

        When the reward function is unknown, infer it from expert demonstrations $\mathcal{D} = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots\}$:

        $$R^* = \arg\max_R \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \mathcal{D}}\left[\sum_t \log \pi_{R}(a_t | s_t)\right]$$

        Maximum Entropy IRL (Ziebart et al., 2008):

        $$P(\tau | R) = \frac{e^{R(\tau)}}{Z(R)}$$

        Where $Z(R) = \int e^{R(\tau)} d\tau$ is the partition function (normalizer).

        The reward is recovered by maximizing the likelihood of demonstrations under the maximum entropy model.

        Q and A 9.1.2

        Q1: A cleaning robot receives $+10$ for being at the charging station. Why is this a bad reward?

        A: The robot learns that being at the charging station is highly rewarding, and it will simply stay there permanently. This is reward hacking: the agent found a trivial strategy to maximize $R$ that does not achieve the designer's goal (a clean environment). The reward was intended to encourage charging when low on battery, but without the cost of not cleaning, the agent ignores the cleaning task entirely. The fix: add a penalty for uncleaned areas, or use a reward that explicitly encodes the cleaning goal.

        Q2: Show that potential-based shaping does not change the optimal policy.

        A: Consider the shaped MDP with $R' = R + \gamma \Phi(s') - \Phi(s)$. The Q-function under the shaped reward: $Q'^{\pi}(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_t \gamma^t r'_t | s_0=s, a_0=a] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_t \gamma^t r_t + \sum_t \gamma^t(\gamma\Phi(s_{t+1}) - \Phi(s_t)) | s_0=s, a_0=a]$. The shaping sum telescopes: $\sum_t \gamma^t(\gamma\Phi(s_{t+1}) - \Phi(s_t)) = -\Phi(s_0)$ (under appropriate conditions). Therefore $Q'^{\pi}(s,a) = Q^{\pi}(s,a) - \Phi(s)$. Since $\Phi(s)$ does not depend on $a$: $\arg\max_a Q'^{\pi}(s,a) = \arg\max_a Q^{\pi}(s,a)$. The optimal policy is unchanged.

        9.1.3: Exploration vs. Exploitation

        The exploration-exploitation dilemma is the fundamental tension in RL: should the agent use its current knowledge (exploit) or try new actions to potentially discover better strategies (explore)?

        The Multi-Armed Bandit Formalization

        The pure exploration-exploitation problem is the $k$-armed bandit:

        • $k$ arms (actions), each with unknown mean reward $\mu_i$
        • At each step, pull one arm and observe reward $r \sim P_i(\mu_i, \sigma_i)$
        • Goal: maximize total reward over $T$ steps

        Regret: The difference between total reward of always pulling the best arm vs. the actual policy:

        $$\text{Regret}(T) = T \cdot \mu^* - \sum_{t=1}^T \mathbb{E}[r_t]$$

        Where $\mu^* = \max_i \mu_i$.

        Decomposition of regret:

        $$\text{Regret}(T) = \sum_{i=1}^k \Delta_i \cdot \mathbb{E}[N_i(T)]$$

        Where $\Delta_i = \mu^* - \mu_i$ (suboptimality gap) and $N_i(T)$ = number of pulls of arm $i$.

        Exploration Strategies

        1. Epsilon-Greedy

        $$a_t = \begin{cases} \arg\max_a Q(s_t, a) & \text{with probability } 1 - \epsilon \\ \text{uniform random action} & \text{with probability } \epsilon \end{cases}$$

        Regret: $\text{Regret}(T) = O(\epsilon T + \frac{k}{\epsilon})$ for fixed $\epsilon$.

        Optimal $\epsilon^* = \sqrt{k/T}$ gives $\text{Regret}(T) = O(\sqrt{kT})$.

        Decaying epsilon: $\epsilon_t = \min(1, \frac{ck}{d^2 t})$ where $c, d$ are constants.

        2. Upper Confidence Bound (UCB1)

        $$a_t = \arg\max_a \left[ \hat{Q}_t(a) + c \sqrt{\frac{\ln t}{N_t(a)}} \right]$$

        Where:

        • $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ = estimated Q-value of action $a$ at time $t$
        • $N_t(a)$ = number of times action $a$ has been selected
        • $c$ = exploration constant (typically $c = \sqrt{2}$)

        Intuition: The UCB term $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$ is large for rarely-tried actions (high uncertainty) and shrinks as an action is explored more (lower uncertainty).

        UCB1 Regret Bound (Auer et al., 2002):

        $$\text{Regret}(T) \leq \sum_{i : \mu_i < \mu^*} \frac{8 \ln T}{\Delta_i} + \left(1 + \frac{\pi^2}{3}\right) \sum_i \Delta_i$$

        This is $O(\sqrt{kT \ln T})$, which is nearly optimal.

        3. Thompson Sampling (Bayesian Exploration)

        Maintain a posterior distribution over Q-values. At each step, sample from the posterior and act greedily:

        $$\tilde{Q}(a) \sim P(Q^* | \text{observations so far})$$

        $$a_t = \arg\max_a \tilde{Q}(a)$$

        For Bernoulli rewards with Beta prior:

        $$\tilde{\mu}_a \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha_a, \beta_a)$$

        Where $\alpha_a$ = successes, $\beta_a$ = failures observed for arm $a$.

        Thompson Sampling Regret: $\text{Regret}(T) = O\left(\sqrt{kT \ln k}\right)$ -- same order as UCB but often better in practice.

        4. Boltzmann (Softmax) Exploration

        Actions are selected proportionally to their estimated value:

        $$P(a_t = a | s_t) = \frac{\exp(Q(s_t, a) / \tau)}{\sum_{a'} \exp(Q(s_t, a') / \tau)}$$

        Where $\tau > 0$ is the temperature parameter.

        • $\tau \to 0$: greedy (pure exploitation)
        • $\tau \to \infty$: uniform random (pure exploration)

        Optimal temperature schedule: $\tau_t = \frac{c}{\ln(t+1)}$ for some constant $c$.

        Exploration-Exploitation in Full RL

        In the full RL setting (not just bandits), exploration must balance:

        • Temporal credit assignment: Rewards may be delayed, making it hard to know which actions were responsible
        • State coverage: Agent must visit all relevant states, not just all actions in one state
        • Directed exploration: Exploration should be directed toward informative states

        Posterior Sampling for RL (PSRL):

        At the start of each episode, sample a model $\tilde{M} \sim P(M | \mathcal{D})$ from the posterior and act optimally under $\tilde{M}$:

        $$\tilde{M} \sim P(M | \mathcal{D}), \quad \pi_t = \pi^*(\tilde{M})$$

        PSRL Regret Bound:

        $$\text{Regret}(T) = O\left(|S| \sqrt{|A| T \ln T}\right)$$

        This is near-optimal for episodic MDPs.

        Q and A 9.1.3

        Q1: UCB adds a bonus $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)}$. What happens to this bonus as $N_t(a) \to \infty$?

        A: As $N_t(a) \to \infty$, the bonus $c\sqrt{\ln t / N_t(a)} \to 0$. This means that as an arm is pulled more and more, the exploration bonus decreases toward zero. Eventually, only the estimated value $\hat{Q}_t(a)$ drives selection, and UCB becomes increasingly greedy. This is correct behavior: as we gather more data about an arm, our uncertainty decreases, and exploration of that arm becomes less valuable. UCB achieves the optimal balance: arms with high estimated value are pulled frequently, and arms with high uncertainty are also pulled, but neither excessively.

        Q2: An agent uses $\epsilon = 0.1$ in a 10-armed bandit for $T = 10{,}000$ steps. Estimate the expected regret.

        A: With $\epsilon = 0.1$, the agent takes random actions $10\%$ of the time. In those random steps ($0.1 \times 10{,}000 = 1000$ steps), it gets average reward $\bar{\mu}$ instead of $\mu^*$. Assuming the best arm has $\mu^* = 1$ and average arm has $\bar{\mu} = 0.5$, the regret from exploration steps is approximately $1000 \times (1 - 0.5) = 500$. During exploitation steps (9000), the agent uses its estimates. With $9000$ exploitation steps split among arms, each arm gets roughly $1000/10 = 100$ pulls, giving reasonable estimates. Total expected regret $\approx 500 + \text{initial learning regret} \approx 600$--$700$ for a simple problem.

        9.2: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning

        Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) extends RL to settings with multiple learning agents that simultaneously interact with the environment and with each other.

        9.2.1: Cooperative Learning

        In cooperative MARL, all agents share a common reward signal and work together to maximize joint performance.

        Cooperative MARL Framework: Dec-POMDP

        A Decentralized Partially Observable MDP (Dec-POMDP) is the standard model for cooperative MARL:

        $$\text{Dec-POMDP} = \langle n, S, \{A^i\}_{i=1}^n, T, R, \{O^i\}_{i=1}^n, \Omega, \gamma \rangle$$

        • $n$ = number of agents
        • $S$ = joint state space
        • $A^i$ = action space of agent $i$; joint action: $\mathbf{a} = (a^1, \ldots, a^n) \in \mathcal{A} = \prod_i A^i$
        • $T : S \times \mathcal{A} \to \Delta(S)$ = joint transition function
        • $R : S \times \mathcal{A} \to \mathbb{R}$ = shared reward function (same for all agents)
        • $O^i$ = observation space of agent $i$
        • $\Omega : S \times \mathcal{A} \to \prod_i \Delta(O^i)$ = joint observation function

        Each agent $i$ has only its local observation $o^i_t$ and history $\tau^i_t = (o^i_1, a^i_1, \ldots, o^i_t)$.

        Optimal policy: A joint policy $\pi^* = (\pi^{*,1}, \ldots, \pi^{*,n})$ that maximizes:

        $$V^{\pi^*} = \max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{\pi} \left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t R(s_t, \mathbf{a}_t) \right]$$

        Value Decomposition

        QMIX decomposes the joint Q-function into individual Q-functions while maintaining monotonicity:

        $$Q_{tot}(\mathbf{s}, \mathbf{a}) = f_{mix}\left( Q^1(\tau^1, a^1), \ldots, Q^n(\tau^n, a^n) \right)$$

        Where $f_{mix}$ is a monotone mixing network:

        $$\frac{\partial Q_{tot}}{\partial Q^i} \geq 0 \quad \forall i$$

        This ensures:

        $$\arg\max_{\mathbf{a}} Q_{tot} = \left( \arg\max_{a^1} Q^1, \ldots, \arg\max_{a^n} Q^n \right)$$

        Joint greedy action selection decomposes into individual greedy selections. Each agent can act independently while still coordinating implicitly.

        VDN (Value Decomposition Networks): The simplest case, additive decomposition:

        $$Q_{tot}(\mathbf{s}, \mathbf{a}) = \sum_{i=1}^n Q^i(\tau^i, a^i)$$

        Centralized Training, Decentralized Execution (CTDE)

        The standard paradigm in cooperative MARL:

        • Training: Central access to joint state $s$, all agents' observations, actions, and rewards
        • Execution: Each agent acts using only its local policy $\pi^i(\tau^i)$

        This allows powerful centralized training algorithms while maintaining decentralized (scalable, communication-free) execution.

        Counterfactual Baseline (COMA):

        To assign credit to individual agents, COMA computes each agent's counterfactual advantage:

        $$A^i(s, \mathbf{a}) = Q(s, \mathbf{a}) - \sum_{a'^i} \pi^i(a'^i | \tau^i) Q(s, (a'^i, \mathbf{a}^{-i}))$$

        Where $\mathbf{a}^{-i}$ = actions of all agents except $i$.

        The counterfactual baseline is the expected Q-value if agent $i$ were to randomize over its actions (holding others fixed). A positive advantage means action $a^i$ was better than average for agent $i$.

        Q and A 9.2.1

        Q1: Why is cooperative MARL harder than single-agent RL, even with a shared reward?

        A: Several unique challenges arise: (1) Non-stationarity -- from each agent's perspective, the environment includes other learning agents whose policies change over time, violating the Markov property. The state transition distribution seen by agent $i$ is $T'(s' | s, a^i) = \sum_{a^{-i}} \pi^{-i}(a^{-i}|s) T(s' | s, a^i, a^{-i})$, which shifts as $\pi^{-i}$ changes. (2) Exponential joint action space -- the joint action space $|\mathcal{A}| = \prod_i |A^i|$ grows exponentially with $n$. (3) Credit assignment -- with a shared reward, it is unclear how much each agent contributed. (4) Decentralized execution -- agents must act on partial information at execution time. These challenges make Dec-POMDP optimization NEXP-complete.

        9.2.2: Competitive Learning

        In competitive MARL, agents have opposing objectives. Agent $i$'s gain is (partially or fully) agent $j$'s loss.

        Zero-Sum Markov Games

        A two-player zero-sum Markov game:

        $$\Gamma = \langle S, A^1, A^2, T, R^1, R^2, \gamma \rangle$$

        Where $R^1(s, a^1, a^2) = -R^2(s, a^1, a^2)$ for all $(s, a^1, a^2)$.

        Nash Q-function for zero-sum games:

        $$Q^{*,1}(s, a^1, a^2) = R^1(s, a^1, a^2) + \gamma \sum_{s'} T(s'|s,a^1,a^2) \cdot V^{*,1}(s')$$

        Where:

        $$V^{*,1}(s) = \max_{\sigma^1 \in \Delta(A^1)} \min_{\sigma^2 \in \Delta(A^2)} \sum_{a^1, a^2} \sigma^1(a^1) \sigma^2(a^2) Q^{*,1}(s, a^1, a^2)$$

        This is the minimax value, computed by solving a linear program at each state.

        Minimax-Q Learning (Littman, 1994):

        $$Q(s, a^1, a^2) \leftarrow Q(s, a^1, a^2) + \alpha \left[ R^1 + \gamma V(s') - Q(s, a^1, a^2) \right]$$

        $$V(s') = \max_{\sigma^1} \min_{\sigma^2} \sum_{a^1, a^2} \sigma^1(a^1) \sigma^2(a^2) Q(s', a^1, a^2)$$

        The minimax step requires solving a linear program at each update:

        $$\max_{\sigma^1 \in \Delta(A^1)} \min_{\sigma^2 \in \Delta(A^2)} \mathbf{\sigma}^{1\top} \mathbf{Q}(s') \mathbf{\sigma}^2$$

        This is solvable in polynomial time (LP duality).

        Nash Equilibrium in Markov Games

        A Nash Equilibrium in a Markov game is a joint policy $(\pi^{*,1}, \ldots, \pi^{*,n})$ where:

        $$V^{i,\pi^{*,i}, \pi^{*,-i}}(s) \geq V^{i,\pi^i, \pi^{*,-i}}(s) \quad \forall i, \pi^i, s$$

        No agent can improve by unilaterally deviating, at every state.

        Nash Q-Learning (Hu and Wellman, 2003):

        $$Q^i(s, \mathbf{a}) \leftarrow (1-\alpha) Q^i(s, \mathbf{a}) + \alpha \left[ R^i(s, \mathbf{a}) + \gamma \text{NashVal}^i(s') \right]$$

        Where $\text{NashVal}^i(s')$ = agent $i$'s value at Nash Equilibrium of the stage game at $s'$.

        9.2.3: Self-Play Methods

        Self-play is a technique where an agent learns by playing against itself or past versions of itself.

        Naive Self-Play

        The simplest form: always train against the current version of yourself.

        Problem: Can converge to cyclic strategies rather than Nash Equilibrium.

        Example in rock-paper-scissors: if current policy plays Rock 100%, opponent learns Scissors 100%. Next iteration plays Paper 100%. Next iteration, Scissors again. This cycles indefinitely.

        Fictitious Play

        Each agent best-responds to the historical average strategy of all other agents:

        $$\bar{\sigma}^{-i}_t = \frac{1}{t} \sum_{k=1}^t \sigma^{-i}_k \text{ (average past strategies)}$$

        $$\sigma^i_{t+1} = \text{BR}(\bar{\sigma}^{-i}_t) = \arg\max_{\sigma^i} U_i(\sigma^i, \bar{\sigma}^{-i}_t)$$

        Convergence of Fictitious Play: Converges to Nash Equilibrium in:

        • Zero-sum games (Robinson, 1951)
        • Potential games (Monderer and Shapley, 1996)
        • Does NOT always converge in general-sum games

        Speed of convergence: The average strategy converges at rate $O(1/\sqrt{t})$.

        Policy Space Response Oracles (PSRO)

        PSRO is a game-theoretic generalization of self-play:

        1. Maintain a policy portfolio $\Pi^i = \{\pi^i_1, \ldots, \pi^i_k\}$ for each agent
        2. Compute meta-game payoff matrix $M_{ij}$ for each policy pair
        3. Solve meta-game for Nash Equilibrium meta-strategy $\sigma^i$ over the portfolio
        4. Add new best response policy to portfolio: $\pi^i_{k+1} = \text{BR}(\sigma^{-i})$
        5. Repeat

          6. PSRO converges to Nash Equilibrium as the portfolio grows.

          PSRO regret bound:

          $$\text{Regret at iteration } k \leq \frac{C}{\sqrt{k}}$$

          Where $C$ depends on game complexity.

          AlphaGo/AlphaZero Self-Play

          The most famous application of self-play:

          1. Neural network parameterizes both policy $\pi_\theta(a|s)$ and value $V_\theta(s)$
          2. Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) generates improved policies via lookahead:

            3. $$\pi_{MCTS}(a|s) \propto N(s,a)^{1/\tau}$$

            Where $N(s,a)$ = MCTS visit count for action $a$ at state $s$.

            1. Training target: minimize loss

              2. $$L(\theta) = (V_\theta(s) - z)^2 - \pi_{MCTS}^\top \log \pi_\theta + c\|\theta\|^2$$

              Where $z$ = game outcome ($+1$ win, $-1$ loss).

              1. Self-play generates new training data: agent plays against itself

                2. This achieves superhuman performance in Go, Chess, and Shogi.

                Q and A 9.2.3

                Q1: Why is self-play prone to strategy cycling, and how does PSRO address this?

                A: Naive self-play always trains against the current policy, which creates a moving target. The current policy shifts in response to training, which shifts the target again, creating a feedback loop. In Rock-Paper-Scissors, this produces a cyclic orbit around the Nash Equilibrium rather than converging to it. PSRO addresses this by maintaining a portfolio of all past policies and computing a meta-game Nash Equilibrium over the full portfolio. The best response is against this mixture, not just the current policy. Because the portfolio only grows, past strategies are never forgotten, preventing cycling.

        9.3: Swarm Intelligence

        Swarm intelligence studies collective behavior emerging from simple local interactions among large numbers of agents, inspired by biological systems.

        9.3.1: Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)

        ACO is a metaheuristic inspired by ant foraging behavior, used for combinatorial optimization problems.

        Biological Inspiration

        Real ants find shortest paths by depositing pheromones as they walk:

        • More ants on a path = more pheromone = more ants attracted = positive feedback
        • Pheromones evaporate over time = prevents premature convergence

        ACO for the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)

        Problem: Find the shortest tour visiting all $n$ cities exactly once.

        Representation: Pheromone trail $\tau_{ij} > 0$ on edge $(i,j)$ and heuristic information $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ (inverse distance).

        Transition Probability: Ant $k$ at city $i$ chooses next city $j$ with probability:

        $$P_{ij}^k = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_i^k} [\tau_{il}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$$

        Where:

        • $\mathcal{N}_i^k$ = set of cities not yet visited by ant $k$ (feasibility constraint)
        • $\alpha \geq 0$ = importance of pheromone (exploitation)
        • $\beta \geq 0$ = importance of heuristic (greedy guidance)

        Pheromone Update:

        All pheromone trails evaporate at rate $\rho \in (0,1)$:

        $$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^k$$

        Where the deposit by ant $k$:

        $$\Delta \tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} \frac{Q}{L^k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ used edge } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

        $Q$ is a constant and $L^k$ is the tour length of ant $k$.

        Interpretation: Shorter tours ($L^k$ small) deposit more pheromone per edge, reinforcing shorter paths.

        Ant System (AS) Fixed Point Analysis

        At steady state, the pheromone on edge $(i,j)$ stabilizes to:

        $$\tau_{ij}^* = \frac{Q \cdot \mathbb{E}[\text{fraction of ants using } (i,j)]}{\rho \cdot L^*}$$

        Where $L^*$ is the average tour length. Shorter edges attract more ants and accumulate more pheromone, creating a self-reinforcing cycle toward the optimal tour.

        ACO Variants

        VariantPheromone UpdateKey Difference
        AS (Ant System)All ants contributeSimple, less focused
        ACS (Ant Colony System)Only best ant updatesStronger exploitation
        MMAS (Max-Min AS)Bounds $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$Prevents premature convergence
        Elitist ASBest-so-far ant contributes extraStronger best-solution reinforcement

        MMAS Bound:

        $$\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}(1 - \rho^{1/n})}{(n/2 - 1)\rho^{1/n}} \cdot \frac{1}{n}$$

        ACO Convergence

        Theorem (Gutjahr, 2000): For any combinatorial optimization problem where the optimal solution can be constructed incrementally, the MMAS variant of ACO converges to the globally optimal solution with probability approaching 1 as the number of iterations approaches infinity.

        Convergence time: $O(n^4 \cdot \exp(O(n \ln n)))$ in the worst case, but empirically much faster.

        Q and A 9.3.1

        Q1: What is the role of the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in ACO? What happens when $\alpha = 0$ or $\beta = 0$?

        A: $\alpha$ controls how much ants follow pheromone trails (learned experience), and $\beta$ controls how much ants follow heuristic information (greedy distance preference). When $\alpha = 0$: ants ignore pheromone entirely and make purely greedy choices based on $\eta_{ij}$ alone -- the algorithm becomes a probabilistic greedy heuristic with no learning component. When $\beta = 0$: ants follow pheromone only, with no distance preference -- the algorithm relies entirely on accumulated experience. Without heuristic guidance, convergence is very slow. Typically, $\alpha = 1$ and $\beta = 2$--$5$ gives a good balance. Higher $\alpha/\beta$ ratio favors exploitation of learned trails; lower ratio favors exploration via greedy heuristics.

        9.3.2: Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)

        PSO is a population-based metaheuristic inspired by bird flocking and fish schooling, designed for continuous optimization.

        PSO Dynamics

        A swarm of $n$ particles searches a $d$-dimensional space. Each particle $i$ has:

        • Position: $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$
        • Velocity: $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$
        • Personal best: $\mathbf{p}_i$ (best position found by particle $i$)
        • Global best: $\mathbf{g}$ (best position found by any particle)

        Velocity Update:

        $$\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = w \cdot \mathbf{v}_i(t) + c_1 r_1 \odot (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i(t)) + c_2 r_2 \odot (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i(t))$$

        Position Update:

        $$\mathbf{x}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{x}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t+1)$$

        Where:

        • $w \in [0,1]$ = inertia weight (controls momentum)
        • $c_1 > 0$ = cognitive coefficient (attraction to personal best)
        • $c_2 > 0$ = social coefficient (attraction to global best)
        • $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)^d$ = random vectors (element-wise)
        • $\odot$ = element-wise multiplication

        The three terms in velocity update:

        1. $w \mathbf{v}_i(t)$: inertia (particle continues in current direction)
        2. $c_1 r_1 (\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i)$: cognitive force (attracted to own best)
        3. $c_2 r_2 (\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$: social force (attracted to swarm's best)

          4. Convergence Analysis

          A single particle's trajectory (ignoring stochasticity, fixing $\mathbf{p}_i = \mathbf{g} = \mathbf{p}$):

          $$\mathbf{x}(t+1) = (1 + w - \phi) \mathbf{x}(t) - w \mathbf{x}(t-1) + \phi \mathbf{p}$$

          Where $\phi = c_1 r_1 + c_2 r_2$.

          This is a second-order linear recurrence. The particle converges if and only if the characteristic roots lie inside the unit circle.

          Stability condition (Clerc and Kennedy, 2002):

          $$w < 1, \quad |\phi| < 2(1 + w)$$

          A standard choice ensuring convergence: $w = 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 1.4962$.

          PSO Convergence to Global Optimum

          PSO does not guarantee finding the global optimum in finite time for non-convex problems. However:

          Theorem: For any $\epsilon > 0$ and any target $f^*$, PSO with appropriate parameters finds a solution with $f(\mathbf{x}) \geq f^* - \epsilon$ with probability approaching 1 as iteration count approaches infinity (stochastic convergence).

          This is weaker than exact convergence but sufficient for most applications.

          Personal and Global Best Updates

          $$\mathbf{p}_i(t+1) = \begin{cases} \mathbf{x}_i(t+1) & \text{if } f(\mathbf{x}_i(t+1)) > f(\mathbf{p}_i(t)) \\ \mathbf{p}_i(t) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

          $$\mathbf{g}(t+1) = \arg\max_{i} f(\mathbf{p}_i(t+1))$$

          PSO Topologies

          Global Best (gbest): All particles share one global best. Fast convergence but prone to premature convergence.

          Local Best (lbest): Each particle shares best with $k$ nearest neighbors. Slower but better exploration:

          $$\mathbf{g}_i = \arg\max_{j \in N_k(i)} f(\mathbf{p}_j)$$

          Ring Topology: $k=2$ neighbors. Slowest convergence but most robust exploration.

        9.3.3: Collective Intelligence

        Collective intelligence is the broader study of how groups of agents achieve cognitive capabilities beyond individual capacity.

        Formal Model: Collective Decision Making

        A group of $n$ agents each has an estimate $\hat{v}_i$ of the true value $v^*$:

        $$\hat{v}_i = v^* + \epsilon_i, \quad \epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

        Collective estimate (simple average):

        $$\bar{v} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \hat{v}_i$$

        Accuracy of collective estimate:

        $$\text{Var}(\bar{v}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \to 0 \text{ as } n \to \infty$$

        The collective is more accurate than any individual by a factor of $\sqrt{n}$ (Law of Large Numbers).

        Wisdom of Crowds Condition (Surowiecki):

        The collective outperforms individuals when:

        1. Diversity: Agents have independent, diverse information
        2. Independence: Agents do not copy each other
        3. Decentralization: No single agent dominates
        4. Aggregation: An effective mechanism exists to combine estimates

          5. Formally, if $\text{Cov}(\epsilon_i, \epsilon_j) = \rho \sigma^2$ for $i \neq j$:

          $$\text{Var}(\bar{v}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} + \frac{n-1}{n} \rho \sigma^2 \to \rho \sigma^2 \text{ as } n \to \infty$$

          When agents are correlated ($\rho > 0$), adding more agents eventually has no benefit. Independence (low $\rho$) is essential for collective intelligence.

          Stigmergy: Indirect Communication via Environment

          Agents communicate indirectly by modifying shared environment:

          $$s_{t+1} = s_t + \sum_i \text{mark}(a_t^i) - \text{decay}(s_t)$$

          Ants (pheromones), termites (building traces), and digital agents (shared data structures) all use stigmergy.

          Benefits: No direct communication needed, naturally asynchronous, scales to massive populations.

          Q and A 9.3.3

          Q1: How does PSO differ from genetic algorithms (GAs) as an optimization approach?

          A: Both are population-based metaheuristics, but they differ fundamentally in how they use population information. PSO uses the population to define attractive points (personal best, global best) that guide each particle's continuous trajectory through the search space. There is no genetic recombination or selection pressure -- particles update positions continuously. GAs use selection, crossover, and mutation to generate new candidate solutions from combinations of existing ones -- they are discrete and population-level. PSO is generally faster on continuous problems because it uses gradient-like velocity information; GAs are more flexible for discrete, combinatorial, and mixed-variable problems. PSO is simpler to implement (fewer parameters), while GAs have stronger theoretical convergence guarantees via schema theorems.

          Q2: In collective intelligence, why does agent correlation destroy the benefit of having more agents?

          A: When agents are correlated ($\rho > 0$), their estimates are not independent -- they share common errors (e.g., all agents read the same biased news source). Adding more agents adds more of the same error, not more independent information. The variance floor $\rho\sigma^2$ is irreducible -- no matter how many correlated agents you aggregate, the collective never beats the systematic shared error. This is why diversity of information sources is more important than sheer numbers for collective intelligence. One independent agent with $\rho=0$ can, in principle, achieve lower collective variance than 1000 highly correlated agents.

        Chapter 10: Multi-Agent Planning

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Formally describe centralized, decentralized, and hierarchical planning for MAS
        • Apply scheduling algorithms and resource allocation methods
        • Model temporal planning with time windows and deadlines
        • Design collaborative planning protocols for shared goal achievement

        10.1: Planning Fundamentals

        Multi-agent planning addresses how a group of agents should coordinate their individual plans to achieve individual or joint goals without conflicts.

        10.1.1: Centralized Planning

        In centralized planning, a single planner has complete information and generates a joint plan for all agents.

        Joint Planning Problem

        $$\Pi_{joint} = \langle \{\mathcal{A}_i\}_{i=1}^n, S, A_{joint}, T, G_{joint}, s_0 \rangle$$

        • $A_{joint} = A^1 \times A^2 \times \cdots \times A^n$ = joint action space
        • $G_{joint}$ = set of joint goal states (may be conjunction of individual goals)

        A joint plan is a sequence of joint actions:

        $$\Pi = ((a^1_1, a^2_1, \ldots, a^n_1), (a^1_2, \ldots, a^n_2), \ldots)$$

        Complexity of Centralized Multi-Agent Planning

        The state space for $n$ agents, each with $|S_i|$ local states:

        $$|S_{joint}| = \prod_{i=1}^n |S_i|$$

        For $n = 10$ agents each with $|S_i| = 100$ local states: $|S_{joint}| = 100^{10} = 10^{20}$ -- astronomically large.

        Optimal centralized multi-agent planning is PSPACE-complete (each agent's individual plan is PSPACE-complete, and coupling makes it no harder in the worst case). With temporal reasoning, it becomes EXPSPACE.

        MA-STRIPS Planning

        Multi-Agent STRIPS extends classical planning for multiple agents:

        Each agent $i$ has:

        • Private preconditions: facts known only to agent $i$
        • Public preconditions: facts observable by other agents
        • Private effects: changes to private state
        • Public effects: changes to shared state

        Interaction: Two agents' actions interact if one's effects conflict with the other's preconditions:

        $$\text{conflict}(a^i, a^j) \iff \text{PRE}(a^i) \cap \text{DEL}(a^j) \neq \emptyset \text{ or } \text{PRE}(a^j) \cap \text{DEL}(a^i) \neq \emptyset$$

        Non-interacting actions can be parallelized.

        Plan Parallelism

        A parallel plan assigns actions to time steps, maximizing parallelism:

        $$\text{Makespan} = \text{number of time steps in parallel plan}$$

        The minimum makespan is bounded below by the critical path length:

        $$\text{Makespan}^* \geq \text{CriticalPath}$$

        Partial Order Planning generates plans with minimal ordering constraints, maximizing opportunities for parallel execution.

        10.1.2: Decentralized Planning

        In decentralized planning, agents plan locally with partial information and coordinate through communication.

        Agent-by-Agent Planning (AA Planning)

        Agents plan sequentially: agent 1 plans first, then agent 2 plans given agent 1's plan, etc.

        $$\pi^1 = \text{plan}(s_0, G^1)$$

        $$\pi^2 = \text{plan}(s_0, G^2 | \pi^1)$$

        $$\vdots$$

        $$\pi^n = \text{plan}(s_0, G^n | \pi^1, \ldots, \pi^{n-1})$$

        Problem: Order-dependent -- earlier agents impose constraints on later ones, potentially making later agents' problems infeasible even when a valid joint plan exists.

        Coordination through Plan Commitment

        Agents exchange commitment sets $C^i$ -- promises about their future actions:

        $$C^i = \{(s, a, t) : \text{agent } i \text{ will execute } a \text{ in situation } s \text{ at time } t\}$$

        Other agents plan assuming commitments are fulfilled. This reduces uncertainty but makes planning brittle to commitment violations.

        Commitment cost: If commitment $c \in C^i$ cannot be met, the cost is:

        $$\text{cost}(\text{violate } c) = \sum_{j : c \in \text{depends}(\pi^j)} \text{replan_cost}(\pi^j)$$

        Distributed Constraint-Based Planning

        Agents model interactions as constraints and use distributed constraint solving:

        Variable: Each agent $i$ assigns value $\pi^i$ (plan) from domain $\Pi^i$

        Constraints: $c(\pi^i, \pi^j)$ encodes compatibility:

        $$c(\pi^i, \pi^j) = \mathbf{1}[\text{plans } \pi^i \text{ and } \pi^j \text{ are conflict-free}]$$

        DCOP formulation:

        $$\max_{(\pi^1, \ldots, \pi^n)} \sum_{i} U^i(\pi^i) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad c(\pi^i, \pi^j) = 1 \, \forall i,j$$

        Solved by distributed algorithms such as DPOP, Max-Sum, or ADOPT.

        10.1.3: Hierarchical Planning

        Hierarchical planning decomposes planning into multiple levels of abstraction, reducing search complexity.

        Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) Revisited

        An HTN domain $\mathcal{D}$ contains:

        Primitive tasks: Directly executable actions with preconditions and effects.

        Compound tasks: Abstract tasks decomposable into networks of simpler tasks.

        Methods: $\text{method}(T, (T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k, C))$ where:

        • $T$ = compound task being decomposed
        • $T_1, \ldots, T_k$ = subtasks
        • $C$ = ordering and constraint set

        HTN Planning Algorithm:

        1. Start with the top-level task $T_{top}$
        2. Choose a method $m$ for $T_{top}$
        3. Decompose into subtasks
        4. Recurse: decompose each compound subtask
        5. Primitive tasks form the executable plan

          6. Complexity: HTN planning with acyclic task hierarchies is decidable. HTN with cyclic hierarchies is undecidable (equivalent to post correspondence problem).

          Abstraction Hierarchy

          Multiple levels of abstraction:

          $$L_k \xrightarrow{\text{refine}} L_{k-1} \xrightarrow{\text{refine}} \cdots \xrightarrow{\text{refine}} L_0 \text{ (executable)}$$

          ABSTRIPS (Sacerdoti, 1974): Plan at level $k$ first, then refine to level $k-1$:

          $$\Pi_k \to \text{refine} \to \Pi_{k-1} \to \cdots \to \Pi_0$$

          Downward refinement property: An abstract plan can always be refined to a concrete plan -- ensures that planning at higher levels does not commit to infeasible abstract solutions.

          $$\Pi_k \text{ is valid} \Rightarrow \exists \Pi_0 \text{ valid refinement of } \Pi_k$$

          Complexity savings: Instead of searching $|S|^d$ (depth $d$ in full state space), hierarchical planning searches $\sum_{l=0}^k |S_l|^{d_l}$ where $|S_l| \ll |S|$.

          Q and A 10.1

          Q1: Why does agent-by-agent planning sometimes fail to find a valid joint plan even when one exists?

          A: In AA planning, agent 1 plans optimally for its own goals, possibly consuming resources or occupying positions that would be critical for agent 2. When agent 2 plans given agent 1's committed plan, the residual problem may be infeasible even though a different plan for agent 1 would have left agent 2's problem solvable. Example: two robots must pass each other in a narrow corridor. Agent 1 plans to occupy the corridor center; agent 2 cannot pass. But if agent 1 had planned a slightly different path, both could pass. AA planning is incomplete because it commits to locally optimal plans without global coordination. Multi-agent planning requires backtracking at the agent level, not just within each agent's individual search.

        10.2: Scheduling and Task Management

        Scheduling assigns tasks to agents over time to optimize performance criteria such as makespan, throughput, or tardiness.

        10.2.1: Task Scheduling
