        Scheduling Problem Formulation

        $$\text{Schedule} = \{(T_i, A_{j(i)}, t_i^{start}, t_i^{end}) : i = 1, \ldots, m\}$$

        Where:

        • $T_i$ = task $i$
        • $A_{j(i)}$ = agent assigned to $T_i$
        • $t_i^{start}$ = start time of $T_i$
        • $t_i^{end} = t_i^{start} + p_i$ where $p_i$ = processing time

        Common objectives:

        Makespan (total completion time):

        $$C_{max} = \max_i t_i^{end}$$

        Total weighted completion time:

        $$\sum_i w_i C_i \quad \text{where } C_i = t_i^{end}$$

        Total tardiness:

        $$\sum_i \max(0, C_i - d_i) \quad \text{where } d_i = \text{due date of task } i$$

        Graham's Scheduling Notation

        Scheduling problems are denoted $\alpha | \beta | \gamma$:

        • $\alpha$ = machine environment (1 = single machine, $P$ = parallel identical, $R$ = unrelated machines)
        • $\beta$ = constraints ($r_j$ = release times, $d_j$ = deadlines, $p_j=1$ = unit processing times)
        • $\gamma$ = objective ($C_{max}$, $\sum C_j$, $\sum T_j$)

        Example: $P | | C_{max}$ (parallel machines, minimize makespan):

        List scheduling heuristic (Graham's LPT):

        1. Sort tasks by decreasing processing time: $p_{(1)} \geq p_{(2)} \geq \cdots$
        2. Assign next task to the currently least-loaded agent

          3. LPT Approximation Ratio:

          $$\frac{C_{max}^{LPT}}{C_{max}^*} \leq \frac{4}{3} - \frac{1}{3m}$$

          Where $m$ = number of agents. LPT is at most $4/3$ times worse than optimal.

          FIFO vs Priority Scheduling

          FIFO (First In First Out):

          $$\text{serve tasks in order of arrival: } r_1 \leq r_2 \leq \cdots$$

          SPT (Shortest Processing Time):

          $$\text{serve shortest task first} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \min \sum_i C_i$$

          SPT minimizes average completion time (optimal for $1 | | \sum C_i$):

          $$\sum_i C_i^{SPT} \leq \sum_i C_i^{\text{any policy}}$$

          Proof: Consider swapping adjacent tasks $i, j$ with $p_i > p_j$ (out of SPT order). Before swap: $C_i = p_i + p_j$ (if $j$ is after $i$). After swap: $C_j = p_j$, $C_i = p_j + p_i$. Reduction: $(p_i + p_j) + (p_j + p_i) - (p_j) - (p_j + p_i) = p_i - p_j > 0$. Any swap into SPT order reduces total completion time.

        10.2.2: Resource Allocation

        Resource-Constrained Project Scheduling (RCPS)

        Given $m$ tasks, $k$ resource types with capacities $R^l$:

        Variables: Start time $t_i^s$ for each task $i$

        Constraints:

        • Precedence: $t_j^s \geq t_i^e$ for all $(i,j) \in E$ (precedence edges)
        • Resource: $\sum_{i : t_i^s \leq t \leq t_i^e} r_i^l \leq R^l$ for all $t, l$ (resource capacity)

        Objective: Minimize makespan $C_{max} = \max_i t_i^e$

        RCPS is NP-hard. Solved by branch-and-bound, constraint programming, or metaheuristics.

        Auction-Based Resource Allocation

        Resources are allocated through ascending price auctions:

        1. Auctioneer announces initial prices $p^l = 0$ for each resource $l$
        2. Agent $i$ demands bundle $x_i^l$ that maximizes its utility at current prices:

          3. $$x_i = \arg\max_{x} U_i(x) - \sum_l p^l x^l$$

          1. If $\sum_i x_i^l > R^l$ (excess demand): raise price $p^l \leftarrow p^l + \delta$
          2. If $\sum_i x_i^l < R^l$ (excess supply): lower price
          3. Repeat until $\sum_i x_i^l = R^l$ for all $l$ (market clearing)

            4. Walrasian Equilibrium Theorem: If utility functions are concave (agents have decreasing marginal utility), this process converges to an efficient allocation.

        10.2.3: Temporal Planning

        Temporal planning adds time to classical planning, handling durative actions, deadlines, and time windows.

        Temporal Planning Model

        $$\Pi_{temporal} = \langle S, A, T, G, s_0, \mathcal{T} \rangle$$

        Where $\mathcal{T}$ adds temporal structure:

        • Each action $a$ has duration $d(a) > 0$
        • Actions have time-dependent preconditions (conditions at start, during, or at end)
        • Time windows $[r_i, d_i]$ constrain when tasks must start/end

        Simple Temporal Networks (STN)

        An STN represents temporal constraints between timepoints:

        $$STN = \langle X, E \rangle$$

        • $X = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ = timepoints
        • $E = \{(x_i, x_j, [l_{ij}, u_{ij}]) : l_{ij} \leq x_j - x_i \leq u_{ij}\}$ = difference constraints

        Consistency: An STN is consistent iff there exists a valid assignment of times to all timepoints.

        Checking consistency: Shortest path algorithm on constraint graph (Bellman-Ford):

        $$d_{ij} = \text{shortest path from } x_i \text{ to } x_j \text{ in constraint graph}$$

        The STN is consistent iff there are no negative cycles: $d_{ii} \geq 0$ for all $i$.

        Complexity: $O(n^3)$ by Floyd-Warshall.

        Disjunctive Temporal Networks (DTN)

        DTNs allow disjunctive constraints:

        $$(x_j - x_i \in [l_1, u_1]) \vee (x_j - x_i \in [l_2, u_2])$$

        DTN consistency is NP-complete (requires choosing which disjunct to satisfy).

        Q and A 10.2

        Q1: An agent must schedule 5 tasks with processing times $p = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]$ on a single machine. Compute the total completion time under FIFO and SPT.

        A: FIFO order (as given): $[3, 1, 4, 1, 5]$. Completion times: $C_1 = 3, C_2 = 4, C_3 = 8, C_4 = 9, C_5 = 14$. $\sum C_i = 3+4+8+9+14 = 38$.

        SPT order (shortest first): $[1, 1, 3, 4, 5]$. Completion times: $C_1=1, C_2=2, C_3=5, C_4=9, C_5=14$. $\sum C_i = 1+2+5+9+14 = 31$. SPT reduces total completion time by $38 - 31 = 7$ (18.4% improvement). SPT is optimal for minimizing total completion time on a single machine.

        10.3: Shared Goal Planning

        10.3.1: Collaborative Planning

        Collaborative planning is the process by which multiple agents jointly develop a plan for achieving shared goals.

        Joint Planning Protocol

        1. Goal announcement: Each agent $i$ broadcasts its goals $G^i$
        2. Conflict detection: Identify goal conflicts $G^i \cap \neg G^j \neq \emptyset$
        3. Negotiation: Resolve conflicts through negotiation (priority, compromise)
        4. Plan construction: Each agent constructs its sub-plan
        5. Consistency checking: Verify sub-plans are jointly consistent
        6. Commitment: Agents commit to joint plan $\Pi^*$

          7. Shared Plan Formalism (SharedPlan, Grosz and Kraus)

          A group $G$ has a SharedPlan for goal $g$ iff:

          1. Each agent $i \in G$ intends to perform their part: $I_i(\text{do}(\text{part}^i))$
          2. Each agent believes the team is committed: $B_i(\text{TeamCommitment}(G, g))$
          3. Agents have mutual belief about the plan: $MB_G(\Pi)$

            4. Team Commitment: All agents remain committed to the shared goal until:

            • The goal is achieved
            • The goal becomes impossible
            • The group collectively decides to abandon the goal

            $$\text{TeamCommit}(G, g) \equiv \forall i \in G: I_i(g) \vee \exists \text{collective abandonment decision}$$

        10.3.2: Distributed Goal Achievement

        Goal Distribution Problem

        Given joint goal $G_{joint} = G^1 \wedge G^2 \wedge \cdots \wedge G^n$, distribute goals to agents:

        $$\text{Assign}: G_{joint} \to \{(G^i_{assigned}, \mathcal{A}_i)\}_{i=1}^n$$

        Objective: Minimize total cost + overlap + conflict:

        $$\min \sum_i \text{cost}(\mathcal{A}_i, G^i_{assigned}) + \lambda \cdot \text{conflict}(\{G^i_{assigned}\})$$

        Decomposability Condition:

        $G_{joint}$ is decomposable iff:

        $$\exists G^1, \ldots, G^n : G^1 \wedge \cdots \wedge G^n \equiv G_{joint} \wedge \text{GoalConflict}(G^1, \ldots, G^n) = 0$$

        If goals are decomposable, each agent can plan independently.

        Goal-Plan Libraries

        Agents precompute plan libraries:

        $$\mathcal{L}^i = \{(g, \pi) : \pi \text{ achieves } g \text{ for agent } i\}$$

        At runtime, agents look up applicable plans:

        $$\Pi^i = \{\pi \in \mathcal{L}^i : g_\pi \in G^i_{assigned}\}$$

        This reduces planning to retrieval, making runtime coordination fast.

        10.3.3: Coordination Constraints

        Coordination constraints specify requirements on how agents' plans must interact.

        Formal Coordination Constraints

        A coordination constraint $\kappa$ over plans $(\Pi^1, \ldots, \Pi^n)$:

        $$\kappa(\Pi^1, \ldots, \Pi^n) \in \{\text{satisfied, violated}\}$$

        Types of coordination constraints:

        TypeFormal Definition
        Non-interference$\text{effects}(\Pi^i) \cap \text{precond}(\Pi^j) = \emptyset$
        Synchronization$\exists t : a^i_t \text{ completes before } a^j_{t'}$ starts
        Resource sharing$\sum_i \text{resource}^l(\Pi^i, t) \leq R^l$ for all $t, l$
        Information dependency$\Pi^i$ uses output of $\Pi^j$ as input

        Least Commitment Planning

        Delay committing to specific actions or orderings as long as possible:

        $$\Pi_{LC} = \{a : a \text{ is necessarily in any valid completion}\} \cup \{(a_i \prec a_j) : \text{ordering is necessary}\}$$

        Rationale: Uncommitted choices preserve flexibility for multi-agent coordination. Only add constraints when forced by causal reasoning.

        Q and A 10.3

        Q1: What is the practical advantage of using plan libraries in multi-agent systems?

        A: Plan libraries shift work from runtime planning to offline compilation. During online operation, agents need only retrieve and adapt pre-compiled plans, which takes $O(1)$ or $O(\log |\mathcal{L}|)$ time (lookup), compared to $O(b^d)$ (planning search). This enables: (1) Hard real-time guarantees -- response within bounded time; (2) Better plans -- offline compilation allows exhaustive search and optimization; (3) Reusability -- the same plan works in similar situations; (4) Communication -- agents can share plan handles (names) rather than full plans. The cost is reduced flexibility -- pre-compiled plans may not handle truly novel situations. Hybrid approaches combine library plans with online planning for novel sub-problems.

        Chapter 11: Decision Making and Game Theory

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Formally define and apply utility theory for rational decision making
        • Analyze normal-form and extensive-form games
        • Compute Nash Equilibria in pure and mixed strategies
        • Design voting mechanisms and consensus protocols
        • Understand Byzantine fault tolerance and its implications

        11.1: Utility Theory

        11.1.1: Utility Functions

        Utility theory provides the mathematical foundation for rational decision making. A utility function maps outcomes to real numbers representing desirability.

        Von Neumann-Morgenstern (VNM) Utility Theory

        A rational agent's preferences over lotteries (probability distributions over outcomes) can be represented by an expected utility function iff preferences satisfy four axioms:

        Axiom 1 -- Completeness:

        $$\forall A, B : A \succeq B \text{ or } B \succeq A$$

        (Agent can always compare any two lotteries)

        Axiom 2 -- Transitivity:

        $$A \succeq B \wedge B \succeq C \Rightarrow A \succeq C$$

        Axiom 3 -- Continuity:

        $$A \succ B \succ C \Rightarrow \exists p \in [0,1] : B \sim pA + (1-p)C$$

        (There exists a probability making the agent indifferent between $B$ and a lottery over $A, C$)

        Axiom 4 -- Independence:

        $$A \succ B \Rightarrow pA + (1-p)C \succ pB + (1-p)C \quad \forall p \in (0,1], C$$

        VNM Theorem: If an agent satisfies all four axioms, there exists a utility function $U: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that:

        $$A \succeq B \iff \mathbb{E}[U(A)] \geq \mathbb{E}[U(B)]$$

        That is: $A \succeq B \iff \sum_x P_A(x) U(x) \geq \sum_x P_B(x) U(x)$.

        The utility function $U$ is unique up to positive affine transformations: if $U$ represents preferences, so does $aU + b$ for any $a > 0$.

        Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium

        The certainty equivalent (CE) of a lottery $L$ is the certain payoff that the agent values equally:

        $$U(CE(L)) = \mathbb{E}[U(L)]$$

        For a risk-averse agent with concave $U$:

        $$CE(L) < \mathbb{E}[L]$$

        The risk premium is:

        $$RP(L) = \mathbb{E}[L] - CE(L) > 0$$

        The agent pays $RP$ to avoid risk.

        Arrow-Pratt Absolute Risk Aversion:

        $$A(x) = -\frac{U''(x)}{U'(x)}$$

        This measures the curvature of the utility function:

        • $A(x) > 0$: risk averse (concave $U$)
        • $A(x) = 0$: risk neutral (linear $U$)
        • $A(x) < 0$: risk seeking (convex $U$)

        Common utility functions:

        TypeFunctionRisk Aversion $A(x)$
        Linear$U(x) = x$0 (risk neutral)
        Log$U(x) = \ln x$$1/x$ (decreasing)
        Exponential$U(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$$\lambda$ (constant, CARA)
        Power$U(x) = x^{1-\rho}/(1-\rho)$$\rho/x$ (decreasing, CRRA)

        11.1.2: Preference Modeling

        Preferences over outcomes can be complex: non-linear, multi-attribute, and context-dependent.

        Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT)

        When outcomes have multiple attributes $X = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k)$:

        Additive utility (if attributes are preference-independent):

        $$U(x_1, \ldots, x_k) = \sum_{j=1}^k w_j u_j(x_j), \quad \sum_j w_j = 1$$

        Where $u_j : X_j \to [0,1]$ is the marginal utility function for attribute $j$.

        Multiplicative utility (when additive form does not hold):

        $$(1 + cU(x)) = \prod_{j=1}^k (1 + c w_j u_j(x_j))$$

        Where $c$ satisfies $1 + c = \prod_j (1 + c w_j)$.

        Attribute independence test: Attributes $X_i$ and $X_j$ are preferentially independent if the preference between lotteries over $(X_i, X_j)$ does not depend on the values of other attributes. If all pairs are independent, the additive form holds.

        Ordinal vs. Cardinal Utility

        Ordinal utility: Only the ranking of outcomes matters, not magnitudes:

        $$U \text{ and } g(U) \text{ for any strictly increasing } g \text{ represent the same preferences}$$

        Ordinal utility is sufficient for deterministic choice.

        Cardinal utility (VNM): Magnitudes matter because they determine probabilities in lotteries:

        $$U \text{ and } aU + b \text{ for } a > 0 \text{ represent the same preferences, but } g(U) \text{ for nonlinear } g \text{ generally does not}$$

        Cardinal utility is required for decision making under uncertainty.

        11.1.3: Risk Analysis

        Expected Utility Maximization under Risk

        Given a decision problem with uncertain outcomes:

        $$a^* = \arg\max_{a \in A} \mathbb{E}[U(a)] = \arg\max_{a \in A} \sum_{\omega \in \Omega} P(\omega) U(\text{outcome}(a, \omega))$$

        Decision matrix:

        ActionState $\omega_1$ ($p_1$)State $\omega_2$ ($p_2$)EU
        $a_1$$x_{11}$$x_{12}$$p_1 U(x_{11}) + p_2 U(x_{12})$
        $a_2$$x_{21}$$x_{22}$$p_1 U(x_{21}) + p_2 U(x_{22})$

        Choose $a^* = \arg\max_a EU(a)$.

        Value at Risk (VaR) and Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR)

        For agents that care about worst-case outcomes:

        Value at Risk at level $\alpha$:

        $$VaR_{\alpha}(X) = \inf\{x : P(X \leq x) \geq \alpha\}$$

        This is the $\alpha$-quantile of the loss distribution. $VaR_{0.95}$ is the 95th percentile loss.

        Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) / Expected Shortfall:

        $$CVaR_{\alpha}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X | X \geq VaR_{\alpha}(X)]$$

        CVaR is the expected loss given that loss exceeds the VaR threshold. CVaR is a coherent risk measure (subadditive, convex), making it suitable for optimization.

        CVaR Optimization:

        $$\min_{a \in A} CVaR_{\alpha}(L(a)) = \min_{a \in A, \, z \in \mathbb{R}} \left[ z + \frac{1}{1-\alpha} \mathbb{E}[(L(a) - z)^+] \right]$$

        This can be solved as a linear program when $L(a)$ is linear in $a$.

        Q and A 11.1

        Q1: An agent faces a lottery: win $100 with probability 0.5, win 0 with probability 0.5. Its utility is $U(x) = \sqrt{x}$. Compute the certainty equivalent and risk premium.

        A: $\mathbb{E}[U] = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{100} + 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{0} = 0.5 \cdot 10 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 5$. Certainty equivalent: $U(CE) = 5 \Rightarrow \sqrt{CE} = 5 \Rightarrow CE = 25$. Expected value: $\mathbb{E}[L] = 0.5 \cdot 100 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 50$. Risk premium: $RP = 50 - 25 = 25$. The agent would accept a certain $25 over a 50-50 gamble with expected value $50, demonstrating significant risk aversion (the agent gives up half the expected value to avoid risk).

        11.2: Game Theory

        Game theory studies strategic interactions where each agent's outcome depends on the actions of all agents.

        11.2.1: Nash Equilibrium

        Normal Form Game

        A normal form (strategic form) game:

        $$G = \langle N, \{A^i\}_{i \in N}, \{u^i\}_{i \in N} \rangle$$

        • $N = \{1, \ldots, n\}$ = set of players
        • $A^i$ = action set of player $i$; joint action space $A = \prod_i A^i$
        • $u^i : A \to \mathbb{R}$ = utility function of player $i$

        Nash Equilibrium (NE):

        A joint strategy $(\sigma^{1*}, \ldots, \sigma^{n*})$ is a Nash Equilibrium iff for every player $i$:

        $$u^i(\sigma^{i*}, \sigma^{-i*}) \geq u^i(\sigma^i, \sigma^{-i*}) \quad \forall \sigma^i \in \Delta(A^i)$$

        No player can benefit by unilaterally deviating from their NE strategy.

        Computing Nash Equilibrium

        For two-player zero-sum games (minimax):

        $$\max_{\sigma^1 \in \Delta(A^1)} \min_{\sigma^2 \in \Delta(A^2)} \sigma^{1\top} M \sigma^2$$

        This is a linear program (LP), solvable in polynomial time.

        LP formulation:

        $$\max_v \quad v$$

        $$\text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{a^1} \sigma^1_{a^1} M_{a^1, a^2} \geq v \quad \forall a^2 \in A^2$$

        $$\sum_{a^1} \sigma^1_{a^1} = 1, \quad \sigma^1_{a^1} \geq 0$$

        For general-sum games: Support enumeration, Lemke-Howson algorithm (exponential in worst case), or Gambit software.

        Mixed Strategy Equilibrium Computation

        For a $2 \times 2$ game with payoff matrix for player 1:

        $$M = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$$

        Player 2 mixes to make player 1 indifferent between strategies:

        $$a \cdot q + b(1-q) = c \cdot q + d(1-q)$$

        $$q^* = \frac{d - b}{(a - b) - (c - d)}$$

        Similarly, player 1 mixes at:

        $$p^* = \frac{d - c}{(a - c) - (b - d)}$$

        The mixed NE is $(p^*, 1-p^*)$ for player 1 and $(q^*, 1-q^*)$ for player 2.

        Nash's Existence Theorem

        Theorem (Nash, 1950): Every finite game (finite players, finite action sets) has at least one Nash Equilibrium, possibly in mixed strategies.

        Proof sketch (Brouwer fixed-point): Define the best-response function $BR: \Delta \to \Delta$ mapping each joint strategy profile to the set of best responses. By Kakutani's theorem, this correspondence has a fixed point $\sigma^* = BR(\sigma^*)$, which is a Nash Equilibrium.

        11.2.2: Cooperative Games

        Already introduced in Chapter 6. We extend with additional mathematical content.

        TU Games and Imputations

        A transferable utility (TU) game $(N, v)$. An imputation is a payoff vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ satisfying:

        • Efficiency: $\sum_{i \in N} x_i = v(N)$
        • Individual Rationality: $x_i \geq v(\{i\})$ for all $i$

        Nucleolus

        The nucleolus is a solution concept that lexicographically minimizes the maximum excess:

        $$e(S, x) = v(S) - \sum_{i \in S} x_i$$

        The excess measures how much coalition $S$ is dissatisfied with the imputation $x$.

        The nucleolus $x^{nuc}$ lexicographically minimizes the sorted vector of excesses.

        Computation: Solve a sequence of linear programs, each minimizing the maximum remaining excess.

        Uniqueness: The nucleolus is always unique and lies in the core if the core is non-empty.

        Balanced Collections and Core Non-emptiness

        A collection $\mathcal{B}$ of coalitions is balanced if there exist weights $\lambda_S > 0$ such that:

        $$\sum_{S \in \mathcal{B}: i \in S} \lambda_S = 1 \quad \forall i \in N$$

        Bondareva-Shapley Theorem: The core of $(N, v)$ is non-empty iff for every balanced collection $\mathcal{B}$:

        $$\sum_{S \in \mathcal{B}} \lambda_S v(S) \leq v(N)$$

        This provides an efficient linear programming test for core non-emptiness.

        11.2.3: Non-Cooperative Games

        Extensive Form Games

        Extensive form games represent sequential decision making with a game tree:

        $$\Gamma = \langle N \cup \{c\}, V, E, \text{turn}, Z, u, H, \rho \rangle$$

        • $V$ = nodes (decision points); $Z \subseteq V$ = terminal nodes
        • $E$ = edges (actions)
        • $\text{turn}: V \setminus Z \to N \cup \{c\}$ = who acts at each node ($c$ = chance node)
        • $u^i: Z \to \mathbb{R}$ = payoff at terminal nodes
        • $H$ = information sets (sets of nodes a player cannot distinguish)
        • $\rho: H_c \to \Delta(A)$ = chance probabilities

        Backward Induction computes subgame perfect equilibrium:

        At each terminal node, utility is fixed. Working backward:

        $$V^i(v) = \max_{a \in A(v)} V^i(\text{successor}(v, a)) \text{ if turn}(v) = i$$

        $$V^i(v) = \sum_{a} \rho(v, a) V^i(\text{successor}(v, a)) \text{ if turn}(v) = c$$

        Subgame Perfect Equilibrium (SPE): A Nash Equilibrium that is also a Nash Equilibrium in every subgame. Backward induction finds SPE in finite perfect-information games.

        Repeated Games and Folk Theorem

        In a repeated game $G^T$ (game $G$ played $T$ times):

        Folk Theorem: For any feasible, individually rational payoff vector $v^*$ (satisfying $v^*_i \geq \underline{v}_i$ where $\underline{v}_i$ = minmax payoff):

        $$\exists \delta^* < 1 : \forall \delta \geq \delta^*, v^* \text{ is achievable as a subgame perfect NE of the infinitely repeated game}$$

        This means cooperation (which requires trust and long-term relationships) is sustainable through punishment threats when agents are sufficiently patient ($\delta$ close to 1).

        Q and A 11.2

        Q1: Find the Nash Equilibrium of Rock-Paper-Scissors in mixed strategies.

        A: The payoff matrix (for player 1, standard zero-sum) assigns $+1$ for win, $-1$ for loss, $0$ for draw. By symmetry, the unique Nash Equilibrium is the uniform mixed strategy: each player plays each action with probability $1/3$. Verification: if player 2 plays $(1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$, player 1's expected payoff is $(1/3)(0) + (1/3)(+1) + (1/3)(-1) = 0$ for Rock; similarly 0 for Paper and Scissors. Player 1 is indifferent among all pure strategies, so any mixture (including $1/3$ each) is a best response. By symmetry of the zero-sum game, the unique NE is the uniform mixture for both players, with expected value 0 for both.

        11.3: Distributed Decision Systems

        11.3.1: Voting Mechanisms

        Voting aggregates individual preferences into a collective decision.

        Social Choice Functions

        A social choice function $f: L^n \to X$ maps $n$ agents' preference orderings $L$ over alternatives $X$ to a single collective choice.

        Arrow's Impossibility Theorem (1951):

        No social choice function satisfies all four conditions simultaneously when $|X| \geq 3$:

        1. Unrestricted domain: Works for any preference profile
        2. Pareto efficiency: If all prefer $x$ over $y$, society prefers $x$
        3. Independence of irrelevant alternatives (IIA): Society's ranking of $x, y$ depends only on individuals' rankings of $x, y$
        4. Non-dictatorship: No single agent's preference always determines the outcome

          5. Implication for MAS: No voting rule perfectly aggregates preferences. Designers must choose which desirable property to sacrifice.

          Common Voting Rules

          Plurality Voting:

          $$f(\text{profile}) = \arg\max_{x \in X} |\{i : x \text{ is most preferred by agent } i\}|$$

          Satisfies Pareto but not IIA. Simple but ignores preference intensity.

          Borda Count:

          $$\text{score}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n \text{rank}^i(x) \text{ (position in agent } i\text{'s preference order)}$$

          $$f = \arg\max_x \text{score}(x)$$

          More sensitive to full preference ordering, but vulnerable to strategic manipulation.

          Condorcet Winner:

          Alternative $x$ is a Condorcet winner if it beats every other alternative in pairwise majority voting:

          $$x \succ_{CW} y \iff |\{i : x \succ^i y\}| > n/2 \quad \forall y \neq x$$

          A Condorcet winner may not exist (Condorcet paradox: $A \succ B \succ C \succ A$ -- majority cycle).

          Approval Voting:

          Each agent submits a subset $S^i \subseteq X$ of approved alternatives:

          $$f = \arg\max_x |\{i : x \in S^i\}|$$

          Allows voters to express support for multiple alternatives, reduces strategic voting.

          Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem

          For any voting rule with $|X| \geq 3$ that is non-dictatorial and onto (all alternatives can win), there exists a preference profile where some agent can benefit from misreporting their true preferences.

          Implication: No non-trivial voting rule is strategy-proof for more than 2 alternatives. Agents will sometimes lie in their votes.

        11.3.2: Consensus Algorithms

        Paxos (Detailed)

        Paxos achieves consensus among $n = 2f+1$ nodes with up to $f$ crash failures.

        Roles:

        • Proposers: Propose values
        • Acceptors: Vote on proposals
        • Learners: Learn the decided value

        Phase 1a (Prepare): Proposer with ID $n$ sends $\text{PREPARE}(n)$ to all acceptors.

        Phase 1b (Promise): Acceptor replies with $\text{PROMISE}(n, v_{prev}, n_{prev})$ where:

        • $v_{prev}$ = previously accepted value (if any)
        • $n_{prev}$ = proposal number of previously accepted value
        • Acceptor promises not to accept proposals with number $< n$

        Phase 2a (Accept): Proposer sends $\text{ACCEPT}(n, v)$ where:

        • $v = v_{prev}$ (highest-numbered previously accepted value from Phase 1b responses)
        • OR $v$ = any value if no previous values were seen

        Phase 2b (Accepted): Acceptor accepts if it has not promised a higher number.

        Safety property: At most one value is decided in any execution.

        Formal safety proof: Suppose values $v$ and $v'$ are both decided. They must each have been accepted by a majority ($f+1$ of $2f+1$). Any two majorities overlap in at least 1 node. This node accepted both values, which contradicts the promise mechanism. Hence $v = v'$.

        Liveness: Paxos may loop (two proposers keep preempting each other). Multi-Paxos elects a stable leader to ensure liveness.

        11.3.3: Byzantine Fault Tolerance

        Byzantine Generals Problem (Formal)

        $n$ generals must agree on a plan. Up to $f$ are traitors (Byzantine faults). A loyal general's message is always accurate. A traitor's message can be arbitrary.

        Goal: All loyal generals agree on the same plan (unanimity + consistency).

        Lower Bound Theorem: Byzantine consensus is impossible with $n \leq 3f$.

        Construction for $n = 3f+1$:

        The Oral Message (OM) algorithm:

        $\text{OM}(0)$: Loyal commander sends value; all generals use it.

        $\text{OM}(m)$: Commander sends values to $n-1$ lieutenants. Each lieutenant acts as commander for $\text{OM}(m-1)$ with remaining lieutenants. Each general uses the majority of received values.

        Round complexity: $O(n^{f+1})$ messages total.

        PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance):

        More efficient protocol for $n = 3f+1$ requiring $O(n^2)$ messages per consensus round:

        1. Client sends request to primary
        2. Primary multicasts PRE-PREPARE to all replicas
        3. Each replica multicasts PREPARE after verifying
        4. After $2f+1$ PREPARE messages, replica multicasts COMMIT
        5. After $2f+1$ COMMIT messages, replica executes and replies to client

          6. PBFT latency: 3 communication rounds in the happy path.

          Safety: No two correct replicas execute different requests for the same sequence number.

          Liveness: All correct replicas eventually execute the request (assuming bounded message delays).

          Q and A 11.3

          Q1: Three candidates A, B, C. Five agents have preferences: $(A > B > C), (A > B > C), (B > C > A), (C > A > B), (C > A > B)$. Who wins under plurality, Borda, and Condorcet?

          A: Plurality: A gets 2 votes, B gets 1, C gets 2. Tie between A and C.

          Borda (3 points for 1st, 2 for 2nd, 1 for 3rd): A: $3+3+1+2+2=11$. B: $2+2+3+1+1=9$. C: $1+1+2+3+3=10$. Winner: A (11 points).

          Condorcet: A vs B: agents 1,2,4,5 prefer A over B (4 vs 1): A wins. A vs C: agents 1,2,3 prefer A (3 vs 2): A wins. B vs C: agents 1,2,3 prefer B (3 vs 2): B wins. Condorcet winner: A (beats all others in pairwise majority). All three rules agree here: A wins, but this agreement is not guaranteed in general.

        Chapter 12: Security and Trust in MAS

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Apply cryptographic foundations to agent authentication and secure communication
        • Design reputation and trust systems with formal mathematical models
        • Identify and mitigate threats from malicious agents
        • Analyze privacy preservation and ethical requirements in MAS

        12.1: Security Fundamentals

        Security in MAS must address unique challenges: agents are autonomous, mobile, and operate in open environments where not all participants are trustworthy.

        12.1.1: Authentication

        Authentication verifies that an agent is who it claims to be.

        Cryptographic Primitives

        Hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^k$:

        • Preimage resistance: given $h$, hard to find $x$ with $H(x) = h$
        • Collision resistance: hard to find $x \neq y$ with $H(x) = H(y)$

        Digital signature scheme $(\text{KeyGen}, \text{Sign}, \text{Verify})$:

        • $\text{KeyGen}() \to (pk, sk)$ = key generation (public key, secret key)
        • $\text{Sign}(sk, m) \to \sigma$ = signature
        • $\text{Verify}(pk, m, \sigma) \to \{\text{valid}, \text{invalid}\}$ = verification

        Security requirement (EUF-CMA): No polynomial-time adversary can forge a valid signature on a new message even after seeing signatures on chosen messages.

        RSA Signature:

        • Key generation: $n = pq$ (large primes), $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$
        • $pk = (n, e)$, $sk = (n, d)$
        • Sign: $\sigma = H(m)^d \pmod{n}$
        • Verify: $\sigma^e \equiv H(m) \pmod{n}$

        Security relies on the hardness of integer factorization.

        Challenge-Response Authentication

        Agent $A$ proves identity to agent $B$ without revealing a secret:

        1. $B$ sends random challenge $c \xleftarrow{R} \{0,1\}^k$
        2. $A$ computes response $r = \text{Sign}(sk_A, c)$
        3. $B$ verifies $\text{Verify}(pk_A, c, r) = \text{valid}$

          4. Replay attack prevention: Freshness of challenge $c$ prevents replaying old responses.

          Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for MAS

          A Certificate Authority (CA) certifies agent identity:

          $$\text{Cert}(A) = \langle ID_A, pk_A, \text{validity}, \text{Sign}(sk_{CA}, \langle ID_A, pk_A \rangle) \rangle$$

          Any agent can verify the certificate using $pk_{CA}$ (publicly known).

          Agent authentication in MAS:

          1. Agent $A$ presents $\text{Cert}(A)$ to agent $B$
          2. $B$ verifies certificate with $pk_{CA}$
          3. $B$ challenges $A$ with nonce $c$
          4. $A$ signs $c$, $B$ verifies with $pk_A$ from certificate

        12.1.2: Authorization

        Authorization determines what actions an authenticated agent is permitted to perform.

        Access Control Models

        Discretionary Access Control (DAC):

        Resource owner specifies access permissions. Permission matrix $M_{ij}$:

        $$M_{ij} = \text{set of operations agent } j \text{ may perform on resource } i$$

        Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):

        $$\text{Agent} \to \text{Role} \to \text{Permission} \to \text{Resource}$$

        Roles aggregate permissions. An agent's permissions = union of its roles' permissions:

        $$\text{Perm}(A) = \bigcup_{r \in \text{Roles}(A)} \text{Perm}(r)$$

        Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC):

        Access policies are Boolean expressions over attributes:

        $$\text{Permit}(A, r, o) \iff \phi(\text{attr}(A), \text{attr}(r), \text{attr}(o), \text{env})$$

        Where $\phi$ is a policy expression evaluating subject, resource, object, and environment attributes.

        Example policy: $\text{Permit}$ iff $\text{clearance}(A) \geq \text{sensitivity}(r) \wedge \text{time} \in [09{:}00, 17{:}00]$.

        Capability-Based Authorization in MAS

        Agents hold capabilities (unforgeable tokens granting permission):

        $$\text{Cap}(A, r, O) = \text{Sign}(sk_{owner}, \langle A, r, O \rangle)$$

        Agent $A$ can delegate capability to agent $B$:

        $$\text{Cap}_{delegated}(B, r, O) = \text{Sign}(sk_A, \langle B, r, O, \text{Cap}(A, r, O) \rangle)$$

        Verification chains back to the original owner through delegation chain.

        12.1.3: Secure Communication

        Symmetric Encryption

        Both sender and receiver share secret key $K$:

        $$c = E_K(m), \quad m = D_K(c)$$

        AES (Advanced Encryption Standard): Block cipher with 128/192/256-bit keys. Block size = 128 bits.

        Security under IND-CPA (indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack): no polynomial-time adversary can distinguish $E_K(m_0)$ from $E_K(m_1)$.

        Key exchange problem: How do agents share $K$ securely over an open channel?

        Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange

        Agents $A$ and $B$ establish a shared secret without prior shared key:

        1. Public parameters: prime $p$, generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$
        2. $A$ chooses $a \xleftarrow{R} \mathbb{Z}_{p-1}$, sends $g^a \pmod{p}$
        3. $B$ chooses $b \xleftarrow{R} \mathbb{Z}_{p-1}$, sends $g^b \pmod{p}$
        4. Shared secret: $K = g^{ab} \pmod{p}$

          5. - $A$ computes $(g^b)^a = g^{ab}$

          - $B$ computes $(g^a)^b = g^{ab}$

          Security relies on the Discrete Logarithm Problem: given $g^a \pmod p$, computing $a$ is computationally hard.

          Man-in-the-middle attack: An attacker can impersonate both parties unless authentication is combined with DH (e.g., Station-to-Station protocol).

          TLS Protocol for Agent Communication

          TLS (Transport Layer Security) provides:

          • Confidentiality: AES encryption of payload
          • Integrity: HMAC authentication of messages
          • Authentication: Certificate-based server (and optionally client) authentication

          TLS Handshake Simplified:

          1. Client sends $\text{ClientHello}$ (supported cipher suites, random nonce $r_c$)
          2. Server sends $\text{ServerHello}$ (chosen cipher, random nonce $r_s$) + Certificate
          3. Client verifies certificate
          4. Key exchange (DH or RSA key encapsulation)
          5. Both derive session keys: $K = \text{PRF}(r_c, r_s, \text{shared_secret})$
          6. Encrypted communication begins

            7. Message Authentication Code (MAC):

            $$\text{MAC}_K(m) = H(K \| m) \text{ (HMAC)}$$

            The receiver verifies: $\text{MAC}_K(m) = ?$ -- provides integrity and authenticity.

            Q and A 12.1

            Q1: Why is challenge-response authentication more secure than password-based authentication in MAS?

            A: Password-based authentication requires the agent to transmit the password (or a hash of it) directly. Vulnerabilities include: (1) Eavesdropping -- an adversary monitoring the channel intercepts the password; (2) Replay -- an intercepted hash can be replayed; (3) Dictionary attacks -- weak passwords are guessable offline. Challenge-response authentication using digital signatures never transmits the secret key. The response $\sigma = \text{Sign}(sk, c)$ depends on a fresh random challenge $c$, so replaying an old response to a new challenge fails. Even if all past responses are recorded, the secret key cannot be recovered (under computational hardness assumptions). Security reduces to the security of the signature scheme, not the strength of a human-chosen password.

        12.2: Trust Models

        12.2.1: Reputation Systems

        Reputation systems aggregate community experience to evaluate agent trustworthiness.

        Centralized Reputation System

        A central authority maintains trust scores $T_j \in [0,1]$ for each agent $j$:

        $$T_j = f(\{r_{ij}\}_{i \in \mathcal{U}_j})$$

        Where $r_{ij}$ = rating agent $i$ gave to $j$, and $\mathcal{U}_j$ = set of agents who interacted with $j$.

        Simple average:

        $$T_j = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{U}_j|} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{U}_j} r_{ij}$$

        Weighted average (recent ratings weighted more):

        $$T_j = \frac{\sum_i w(t_i) r_{ij}}{\sum_i w(t_i)}, \quad w(t) = e^{-\lambda(t_{now} - t)}$$

        Exponential decay $\lambda > 0$ makes recent interactions more influential.

        Decentralized Reputation: Web of Trust

        In a decentralized MAS, agents maintain local trust views:

        Agent $A$'s trust in $B$ from agent $C$'s recommendation:

        $$T_A(B) = T_A(C) \cdot T_C(B)$$

        Trust transitivity: $A$ trusts $B$ proportionally to how much $A$ trusts $C$'s judgment times how much $C$ trusts $B$.

        Aggregating multiple paths:

        $$T_A(B) = 1 - \prod_{\text{path } p: A \to B} \left(1 - \prod_{(i,j) \in p} T_i(j)\right)$$

        This is the probabilistic combination: $P(\text{at least one path trusts B})$.

        12.2.2: Trust Evaluation

        Subjective Logic (Jsang)

        Josang's subjective logic represents beliefs as three-component opinions:

        $$\omega_A^B = (b, d, u, a)$$

        Where:

        • $b$ = belief (probability mass supporting trust)
        • $d$ = disbelief (probability mass supporting distrust)
        • $u$ = uncertainty (probability mass uncommitted)
        • $a$ = base rate (prior in absence of evidence)
        • Constraint: $b + d + u = 1$

        Projected probability (expected trustworthiness):

        $$P(\omega_A^B) = b + a \cdot u$$

        Updating with evidence:

        After $s$ successful interactions and $f$ failed ones (with prior $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$):

        $$b = \frac{s}{s + f + 2}, \quad d = \frac{f}{s + f + 2}, \quad u = \frac{2}{s + f + 2}$$

        As evidence accumulates ($s + f \to \infty$): $u \to 0$, opinion converges to frequency.

        Trust Transitivity in Subjective Logic:

        $$\omega_A^C = \omega_A^B \otimes \omega_B^C$$

        Transitivity operator: $b_{A}^C = b_A^B \cdot b_B^C$, $d_A^C = b_A^B \cdot d_B^C$, $u_A^C = d_A^B + u_A^B + b_A^B \cdot u_B^C$.

        Trust Fusion:

        Combining opinions from two independent sources $B_1$ and $B_2$:

        $$b^{fused} = \frac{b_1 u_2 + b_2 u_1}{u_1 + u_2 - u_1 u_2}, \quad u^{fused} = \frac{u_1 u_2}{u_1 + u_2 - u_1 u_2}$$

        12.2.3: Malicious Agents

        Taxonomy of Malicious Behavior

        TypeBehaviorDetection
        DishonestProvides false informationCompare with ground truth
        SelfishFree-rides, avoids contributionMonitor contribution rates
        CollusiveCoordinates with others to manipulateStatistical anomaly detection
        SybilCreates multiple fake identitiesIdentity proofing
        ByzantineArbitrary, unpredictable behaviorMajority voting, BFT protocols

        Whitewashing Attack

        A malicious agent with low reputation creates a new identity to reset its reputation:

        $$T_{new_ID} = T_{prior} \approx T_{default} \gg T_{bad_old_ID}$$

        Defense: Impose entry costs for new identities (e.g., proof-of-work, real-world credentials, social network connections).

        Collusion Detection

        Colluding agents inflate each other's reputation. Statistical test for collusion:

        Given agents $i$ and $j$ with correlation $\rho$ in their rating behaviors:

        $$\rho_{ij} = \frac{\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j)}{\sigma_{R_i} \sigma_{R_j}}$$

        High $\rho_{ij}$ (agents consistently rate the same targets similarly) suggests collusion.

        Sybil resistance via trust graph topology:

        Sybil identities form a weakly-connected cluster in the trust graph. SybilGuard and SybilLimit exploit the fact that honest regions are well-connected (fast-mixing random walks) while Sybil regions are poorly connected to the honest region.

        SybilLimit bound:

        $$\text{Number of accepted Sybil identities} \leq O(\sqrt{n} \cdot \log n)$$

        Where $n$ = number of honest agents.

        12.3: Privacy and Ethics

        12.3.1: Data Privacy

        Formal Privacy: Differential Privacy

        An algorithm $\mathcal{M}$ is $(\epsilon, \delta)$-differentially private if for all adjacent datasets $D, D'$ (differing in one record) and all output sets $S$:

        $$P(\mathcal{M}(D) \in S) \leq e^\epsilon \cdot P(\mathcal{M}(D') \in S) + \delta$$

        Interpretation: The output distribution changes by at most a factor $e^\epsilon$ when one individual's data is added or removed. No query on the aggregate can reveal whether any individual participated.

        Laplace Mechanism: For a real-valued query $f$ with sensitivity $\Delta f = \max_{D, D'} |f(D) - f(D')|$:

        $$\mathcal{M}(D) = f(D) + \text{Lap}\left(\frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}\right)$$

        Where $\text{Lap}(\lambda)$ = Laplace noise with scale $\lambda$.

        Accuracy: $\mathbb{E}[|\mathcal{M}(D) - f(D)|] = \frac{\Delta f}{\epsilon}$. Tighter privacy (smaller $\epsilon$) requires more noise.

        Composition: Sequential queries consume privacy budget:

        $$\text{Sequence of } k \text{ mechanisms, each } \epsilon_i\text{-DP} \Rightarrow \text{composition is } \sum_i \epsilon_i\text{-DP}$$

        Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving MAS

        Agents train machine learning models locally, sharing only model updates (gradients) rather than raw data:

        $$\theta_{global}^{t+1} = \theta_{global}^t - \eta \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{n_i}{n} g_i^t$$

        Where $g_i^t = \nabla_\theta L_i(\theta_{global}^t)$ = gradient computed by agent $i$ on its local data.

        Privacy guarantee: Raw data never leaves agent $i$'s device. Gradients may still leak information, requiring additional noise (DP-SGD).

        12.3.2: Ethical AI Agents

        Ethical Principles for MAS

        Beneficence: Agents act to benefit humans and society.

        Formally: $\mathbb{E}[U_{human} | \pi_{agent}] \geq \mathbb{E}[U_{human} | \pi_{baseline}]$

        Non-maleficence: Agents avoid causing harm.

        Formally: $P(\text{harm} \geq H_{threshold} | \pi_{agent}) \leq \delta$

        Autonomy: Agents respect human decision-making authority.

        Formally: humans can override any agent decision: $\forall t: \exists \text{ override signal}$.

        Justice/Fairness: Agent decisions do not discriminate unfairly.

        Formally (demographic parity): $P(\hat{Y} = 1 | A = 0) = P(\hat{Y} = 1 | A = 1)$

        Where $A$ = protected attribute (race, gender, etc.), $\hat{Y}$ = agent decision.

        Algorithmic Fairness Metrics

        MetricDefinitionFormula
        Demographic ParityEqual positive rates across groups$P(\hat{Y}=1|A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1|A=1)$
        Equal OpportunityEqual TPR across groups$P(\hat{Y}=1|Y=1,A=0) = P(\hat{Y}=1|Y=1,A=1)$
        Equalized OddsEqual TPR and FPRBoth TPR and FPR equal across groups
        CalibrationEqual precision across groups$P(Y=1|\hat{Y}=1,A=0) = P(Y=1|\hat{Y}=1,A=1)$

        Impossibility of Simultaneous Fairness (Chouldechova, 2017):

        When base rates differ across groups ($P(Y=1|A=0) \neq P(Y=1|A=1)$), it is impossible to achieve calibration, equal opportunity, AND demographic parity simultaneously.

        This theorem forces system designers to choose which fairness criterion to prioritize, a fundamentally value-laden choice.

        12.3.3: Responsible MAS

        Accountability Framework

        A MAS is accountable if for every outcome $o$:

        $$\exists i \in N: \text{Agent}_i \text{ is responsible for } o$$

        Responsibility requires: (1) causal contribution to $o$, (2) awareness of likely consequences, (3) ability to have acted differently.

        Causal responsibility (structural causal model):

        Agent $i$ is causally responsible for $o$ if in the actual causal model $M$:

        $$\text{DoCalc}: P(o | do(A_i = a_i^{actual})) > P(o | do(A_i = a_i^{counterfactual}))$$

        The intervention (do-calculus) distinguishes genuine causation from mere correlation.

        Human Oversight Requirements

        For high-stakes MAS (medical, legal, financial), human oversight is mandatory:

        Human-in-the-loop (HITL): Human approves every agent decision.

        • Safety: maximum
        • Efficiency: minimum (bottleneck)

        Human-on-the-loop (HOTL): Agent decides autonomously; human monitors and can intervene.

        • Safety: high (with timely monitoring)
        • Efficiency: high

        Human-in-the-command (HITC): Human sets high-level goals; agent implements.

        • Safety: moderate
        • Efficiency: highest

        Appropriate oversight level depends on:

        • Decision reversibility: irreversible decisions require HITL
        • Risk magnitude: high-risk decisions require HITL
        • Decision frequency: frequent decisions favor HOTL or HITC

        Q and A 12.3

        Q1: Why does differential privacy require a privacy budget, and what happens when the budget is exhausted?

        A: Each query on private data uses some privacy budget $\epsilon$. By the composition theorem, running $k$ queries each with parameter $\epsilon_i$ provides at most $\sum_i \epsilon_i$-differential privacy in total. The budget represents the total information leakage we are willing to accept. When the budget is exhausted: (1) No further queries can be answered with differential privacy guarantees; (2) Additional queries either violate privacy ($\epsilon$ becomes too large), must use so much noise as to be meaningless, or must be rejected. Budget management is a real operational challenge. Techniques like Renyi differential privacy and optimal composition allow more efficient budget accounting, getting more useful queries per unit of privacy budget.

        Q2: A MAS recommender system achieves 80% accuracy for group A but 65% for group B. Is this a fairness violation? How would you fix it?

        A: Yes, this violates equalized odds (unequal true positive rates across groups) and equal opportunity. The accuracy gap ($15\%$) suggests the model may have been trained on biased data (fewer examples from group B, or examples that do not represent group B well). Fixes include: (1) Resampling -- oversample group B in training to achieve balanced representation; (2) Reweighting -- weight group B examples more in the loss function; (3) Adversarial debiasing -- train a fairness discriminator that penalizes group-based accuracy differences; (4) Separate calibration -- calibrate the model's probability outputs separately for each group; (5) Data augmentation -- collect more diverse data from group B. The choice depends on which fairness criterion is legally and ethically required in the specific application.

        Chapter 13: MAS Frameworks and Technologies

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

        • Describe the JADE platform architecture and lifecycle model with precision
        • Explain FIPA-compliant messaging APIs and message routing
        • Contrast JADE, SPADE, and Jason across design philosophy, language, and capability
        • Analyse modern AI agent orchestration frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen)
        • Apply the correct framework to a given problem by matching requirements to capabilities

        13.1: JADE Framework

        JADE (Java Agent DEvelopment Framework) is the most widely adopted open-source platform for building FIPA-compliant multi-agent systems. It abstracts all low-level distribution concerns, allowing developers to focus entirely on agent behaviour.

        13.1.1: JADE Architecture

        Overall Platform Architecture

        JADE implements a three-tier architecture:

        $$\text{JADE Platform} = \langle \text{Main Container},\; \{\text{Container}_k\}_{k=1}^{m},\; \text{ACC} \rangle$$

        Where:

        • Main Container: The primary container that hosts two mandatory system agents and acts as the bootstrap point.
        • Peripheral Containers: One or more JVM processes that host user-defined agents; they connect to the Main Container on startup.
        • ACC (Agent Communication Channel): The message transport layer responsible for routing ACL messages between agents, within and across containers.

        System Agents Hosted in the Main Container

        AgentAcronymResponsibility
        Agent Management SystemAMSAuthoritative white-pages service; manages agent lifecycle
        Directory FacilitatorDFYellow-pages service; manages capability advertisements

        AMS white-pages record for agent $a$:

        $$\text{AMS-Entry}(a) = \langle \text{AID}(a),\; \text{state}(a),\; \text{AP-Name}(a) \rangle$$

        Where $\text{AID}(a)$ is the globally unique Agent Identifier (described below).

        DF yellow-pages record for agent $a$:

        $$\text{DF-Entry}(a) = \langle \text{AID}(a),\; \{s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k\},\; \text{lease-time} \rangle$$

        Where $\{s_i\}$ is the set of service descriptions the agent advertises.

        Agent Identifier (AID)

        Every JADE agent has a globally unique AID:

        $$\text{AID} = \langle \text{local-name},\; \text{AP-name},\; \{\text{address}_j\} \rangle$$

        The fully qualified name is:

        $$\text{FQAN} = \texttt{local-name@AP-name}$$

        Example: PriceAgent@jade-platform:1099/JADE

        The address set $\{\text{address}_j\}$ contains transport-layer URLs (IIOP, HTTP, etc.) through which the ACC can deliver messages.

        Container Registration Protocol

        When a peripheral container $C_k$ starts:

        1. $C_k$ contacts the Main Container at $\langle \text{host}, \text{port} \rangle$
        2. Main Container assigns container ID: $\text{CID}(C_k)$
        3. A shared IMTP (Internal Message Transport Protocol) channel is established
        4. $C_k$ is recorded in the platform registry

          5. Platform registry invariant:

          $$\text{Registry} = \{(a, C(a)) : a \in \text{AllAgents},\; C(a) = \text{container hosting } a\}$$

          Message Routing Model

          When agent $A$ sends a message $m$ to agent $B$:

          1. JADE's kernel checks if $B$ is local (same container as $A$) or remote
          2. Local delivery: Message passed directly via in-memory queue, $O(1)$
          3. Remote intra-platform delivery: Message sent over IMTP to $C(B)$, then to $B$'s queue
          4. Inter-platform delivery: Message forwarded through the ACC using FIPA-specified transport (IIOP or HTTP)

            5. Expected message latency model:

            $$\mathbb{E}[t_{deliver}] = \begin{cases} t_{local} & C(A) = C(B) \\ t_{local} + t_{IMTP} & C(A) \neq C(B), \text{same platform} \\ t_{local} + t_{IMTP} + t_{ACC} + t_{protocol} & \text{inter-platform} \end{cases}$$

            Typical magnitudes: $t_{local} \approx \mu\text{s}$, $t_{IMTP} \approx \text{ms}$, $t_{ACC+protocol} \approx \text{tens of ms}$.

            Q and A 13.1.1

            Q1: Why does JADE separate the AMS and DF into two distinct system agents instead of combining them?

            A: The separation follows the Single Responsibility Principle and mirrors the FIPA specification's conceptual distinction. The AMS is concerned with identity and lifecycle, enforcing platform-wide policies about who is allowed to exist and in what state. The DF is concerned with capability discovery, a purely informational service that agents voluntarily use. Combining them would couple lifecycle management to service advertisement, making it impossible to, for example, have an agent that exists but advertises no services, or to replace the DF with a custom discovery mechanism without touching lifecycle management. The separation also allows independent scaling and replication of each service.

            Q2: If a JADE platform has 4 containers and an agent $A$ in container 1 sends 100 messages to agents uniformly distributed across all 4 containers, what fraction of messages incur IMTP overhead?

            A: Agents are uniform across 4 containers, so $1/4$ of agents are in the same container as $A$ (container 1) and $3/4$ are in the other three containers. Of 100 messages: $100 \times (1/4) = 25$ are local (no IMTP overhead) and $100 \times (3/4) = 75$ incur IMTP overhead. The fraction is $3/4 = 0.75$, so 75 messages have IMTP cost. This motivates co-locating agents that communicate frequently in the same container.

        13.1.2: Agent Lifecycle

        JADE defines a precise finite-state machine for every agent. Understanding this model is essential for building reliable MAS.

        Lifecycle States

        $$\text{States} = \{\text{INITIATED},\; \text{ACTIVE},\; \text{SUSPENDED},\; \text{WAITING},\; \text{DELETED}\}$$

        INITIATED: Agent object has been created but setup() has not yet returned. The AMS has registered the agent.

        ACTIVE: The agent is scheduled for execution on the JADE thread pool. setup() has completed.

        SUSPENDED: Agent is stopped; no behaviours execute. State is preserved. Reactivated by resume().

        WAITING: Agent has voluntarily blocked, waiting for a message or event. Reactivated automatically when the awaited condition is met.

        DELETED: Agent has terminated. takeDown() has been called. AMS has deregistered the agent. The object will be garbage collected.

        Lifecycle Transition Function

        $$\delta : \text{States} \times \text{Events} \to \text{States}$$

        Current StateEventNext State
        INITIATEDsetup() returnsACTIVE
        ACTIVEdoSuspend()SUSPENDED
        ACTIVEdoWait()WAITING
        ACTIVEdoDelete()DELETED
        SUSPENDEDdoActivate()ACTIVE
        WAITINGmessage arrivesACTIVE
        ACTIVEunhandled exceptionDELETED

        Behaviour Model

        JADE agents execute through a cooperative multitasking behaviour scheduler. Each agent maintains a behaviour queue:

        $$\mathcal{B}(a) = \langle b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_k \rangle$$

        The JADE scheduler runs:

        $$\text{while Agent is ACTIVE}: \quad b = \text{dequeue}(\mathcal{B}(a));\; b.\text{action}();\; \text{if not } b.\text{done}(): \text{enqueue}(\mathcal{B}(a), b)$$

        Key invariant: Only one behaviour of a given agent executes at a time (cooperative, not preemptive). Long-running behaviours must yield by returning from action().

        Behaviour Taxonomy

        Behaviour ClassDescriptionExecution Pattern
        OneShotBehaviourExecutes action() exactly once$\text{done}() = \text{true}$ always
        CyclicBehaviourRepeats forever$\text{done}() = \text{false}$ always
        WakerBehaviourExecutes once after timeout $\tau$One-shot after delay
        TickerBehaviourRepeats every $\tau$ millisecondsCyclic with period
        FSMBehaviourFinite-state machine of sub-behaviours$\delta$ defines transitions
        ParallelBehaviourRuns sub-behaviours concurrentlyCompletes when termination policy met
        SequentialBehaviourRuns sub-behaviours in sequenceDone when last sub-behaviour done

        FSMBehaviour formal model:

        $$\text{FSM} = \langle Q, B, \delta_{FSM}, q_0, F \rangle$$

        Where $Q$ = states (strings), $B$ = sub-behaviours (one per state), $\delta_{FSM} : Q \times \mathbb{Z} \to Q$ maps (state, exit-value) to next state, $q_0$ = initial state, $F$ = set of final states.

        Scheduling throughput for agent with $k$ behaviours, each taking $t_b$ ms:

        $$\text{Round-trip time} = k \cdot t_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Max behaviour frequency} = \frac{1}{k \cdot t_b} \text{ Hz}$$

        This shows why long-running action() methods harm responsiveness of all other behaviours in the same agent.

        setup() and takeDown() Contract

        $$\text{Invariant: AMS registers agent before } \texttt{setup()} \text{ and deregisters after } \texttt{takeDown()}$$

        setup() must: register with the DF (if advertising services), initialise shared state, add initial behaviours.

        takeDown() must: deregister from the DF, release external resources, send farewell messages to collaborators.

        Failure during setup(): If setup() throws, JADE transitions the agent directly to DELETED, calling takeDown() for cleanup.

        Q and A 13.1.2

        Q1: An agent has 5 behaviours. Behaviour 3 enters a busy-wait loop polling a flag. What is the impact on the other behaviours?

        A: Because JADE uses cooperative multitasking, Behaviour 3 will never return from action() while spinning. The JADE scheduler cannot preempt it. All other behaviours (1, 2, 4, 5) will be permanently starved and never execute. The agent effectively freezes for all purposes except the spinning behaviour. The correct fix is to use a CyclicBehaviour that checks the flag and immediately returns from action() if not set, or to use doWait() / doActivate() with a notification mechanism. Blocking inside action() is the most common correctness error in JADE programming.

        Q2: Formally, why does WAITING automatically transition to ACTIVE when a message arrives, rather than requiring an explicit doActivate() call?

        A: WAITING is designed specifically for the pattern of blocking until a message arrives, which is the most common inter-agent synchronisation point. If it required explicit reactivation, the agent that sends the triggering message would also need to know which agent is waiting and call doActivate() on it, creating tight coupling and requiring the sender to have the receiver's agent reference. Instead, JADE's message queue implementation automatically notifies the scheduler when a message is enqueued for a WAITING agent, atomically transitioning it to ACTIVE. This decoupling matches the asynchronous, message-passing philosophy of FIPA ACL.

        13.1.3: Messaging APIs

        ACL Message Structure in JADE

        A JADE ACLMessage encapsulates a full FIPA ACL message:

        $$m = \langle \text{performative},\; \text{sender},\; \{\text{receivers}\},\; \text{content},\; \text{language},\; \text{ontology},\; \text{protocol},\; \text{conv-id},\; \text{reply-with},\; \text{in-reply-to},\; \text{reply-by} \rangle$$

        Performative takes values from the FIPA ACL performative set:

        $$\text{Performatives} \subset \{\texttt{INFORM},\; \texttt{REQUEST},\; \texttt{QUERY-IF},\; \texttt{PROPOSE},\; \texttt{ACCEPT-PROPOSAL},\; \texttt{REJECT-PROPOSAL},\; \ldots\}$$

        Approximately 22 FIPA-standardised performatives, each with formal modal-logic semantics.

        Message Templates for Filtering

        Agents do not poll their entire message queue; they specify a MessageTemplate that selects only relevant messages:

        $$T = \phi_1 \wedge \phi_2 \wedge \cdots \wedge \phi_k$$

        Where each $\phi_j$ is a predicate over a message field.

        Matching semantics:

        $$m \models T \iff \bigwedge_{j=1}^k \phi_j(m) = \text{true}$$

        Template combinators:

        CombinatorSemantics
        MatchPerformative(p)$m.\text{performative} = p$
        MatchSender(aid)$m.\text{sender} = \text{aid}$
        MatchConversationId(c)$m.\text{conv-id} = c$
        MatchOntology(o)$m.\text{ontology} = o$
        and(T1, T2)$T_1 \wedge T_2$
        or(T1, T2)$T_1 \vee T_2$
        not(T)$\neg T$

        Receive with timeout $\tau$:

        $$m = \texttt{blockingReceive}(T, \tau) = \begin{cases} m \in Q \text{ s.t. } m \models T & \text{if found within } \tau \text{ ms} \\ \texttt{null} & \text{timeout} \end{cases}$$

        FIPA Interaction Protocol Support

        JADE provides pre-built protocol handling via AchieveREInitiator and AchieveREResponder, encoding the FIPA Request Interaction Protocol:

        Request Protocol State Machine (Initiator side):

        $$\text{START} \xrightarrow{\text{send REQUEST}} \text{WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{receive AGREE}} \text{WAITING-RESULT} \xrightarrow{\text{receive INFORM}} \text{SUCCESS}$$

        $$\text{WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{receive REFUSE}} \text{FAILURE}$$

        $$\text{WAITING-RESULT} \xrightarrow{\text{receive FAILURE}} \text{FAILURE}$$

        $$\text{WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{timeout}} \text{FAILURE}$$

        The protocol behaviour handles timeouts, retry logic, and message correlation automatically.

        Content Language and Ontology

        JADE supports pluggable content languages:

        $$\text{Content} = \text{encode}(\text{ContentElement},\; \text{Language},\; \text{Ontology})$$

        SL (Semantic Language): FIPA's standard formal content language. Expressions are terms and propositions:

        $$\text{SL Expression} ::= \text{Term} \mid \text{Formula} \mid \text{Action}$$

        $$\text{Term} ::= \text{Constant} \mid \text{Variable} \mid \text{FunctionalTerm}$$

        $$\text{Formula} ::= \text{Atom} \mid \neg\phi \mid \phi \wedge \psi \mid \exists x \phi \mid B_i \phi \mid I_i \phi$$

        Ontology registration: Agents register their domain ontology so that JADE's codec can map between Java objects and SL expressions automatically:

        $$\text{Ontology}.\text{add}(\text{ConceptSchema}) \to \text{Schema-Registry}$$

        $$\text{encode}(obj) : \text{JavaObject} \to \text{SL-String}$$

        $$\text{decode}(str, type) : \text{SL-String} \to \text{JavaObject}$$

        Q and A 13.1.3

        Q1: Why are MessageTemplates important for performance in a high-traffic JADE agent?

        A: Without templates, an agent would have to inspect every message in its queue, read its content, and decide if it is relevant. At high message rates (thousands per second), this becomes a bottleneck. MessageTemplates push filtering into JADE's kernel layer, where it can use indexed data structures. Furthermore, behaviours waiting on specific templates block efficiently (the agent moves to WAITING state), consuming zero CPU. With templates, only messages that match the predicate wake the waiting behaviour, eliminating unnecessary wake-ups and re-enqueues. This transforms message handling from $O(n_{\text{queue}})$ per behaviour per cycle to effectively $O(1)$ for matched messages.

        13.2: SPADE and Jason

        13.2.1: SPADE Basics

        SPADE (Smart Python Agent Development Environment) is a Python-based MAS platform built on XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol), providing modern async agent development.

        Architecture

        $$\text{SPADE Platform} = \langle \text{XMPP Server},\; \{A_i\}_{i=1}^n,\; \text{Presence Service} \rangle$$

        Each agent is an XMPP client identified by a Jabber ID (JID):

        $$\text{JID}(A_i) = \texttt{agent_name@xmpp-server/resource}$$

        Transport layer: XMPP over TCP with TLS, providing:

        • Reliable message delivery (with acknowledgement)
        • Presence information (agent online / offline status)
        • Publish-subscribe patterns via XMPP PubSub extension

        Async Execution Model

        SPADE uses Python's asyncio event loop:

        $$\text{Event Loop} : \{\text{coroutines}\} \to \text{interleaved execution}$$

        Each behaviour is an async coroutine scheduled by the event loop. Unlike JADE's thread-pool model, SPADE uses cooperative multitasking at the language level:

        $$\texttt{await asyncio.sleep(0)} \implies \text{yield control to event loop}$$

        Concurrency model comparison:

        PropertyJADE (Java threads)SPADE (Python asyncio)
        ParallelismTrue parallel (multi-core)Single-threaded concurrent
        Blocking call riskThread blocked (others run)Event loop blocked (all stop)
        Memory per agentThread stack (~512 KB)Coroutine frame (~few KB)
        Max agents per processHundredsThousands
        I/O performanceGoodExcellent (async I/O)

        Behaviour Model in SPADE

        Similar to JADE, but implemented as Python coroutines:

        $$B = \langle \texttt{async run}(),\; \texttt{is_killed}() \rangle$$

        CyclicBehaviour loop:

        $$\texttt{while not self.is_killed(): await self.run(); await asyncio.sleep(0)}$$

        Template-based message reception:

        $$m = \texttt{await self.receive(timeout=}t\texttt{)}$$

        Returns next matching message or None after $t$ seconds.

        Presence-based awareness: SPADE agents can subscribe to presence events, receiving notification when other agents go online or offline, providing implicit group membership information:

        $$\text{Presence event: } (A_j, \text{status}) \text{ received by all subscribed agents}$$

        Q and A 13.2.1

        Q1: SPADE agents run on a single Python thread via asyncio. How does this affect CPU-intensive behaviours?

        A: CPU-intensive behaviours (heavy numerical computation, image processing, etc.) block the asyncio event loop entirely because they do not yield with await. While one behaviour is computing, all other behaviours, message reception, and network I/O in the same process stop. The correct approach is to offload CPU work to a thread pool or process pool using asyncio.runinexecutor(), which runs the computation in a separate thread or process and returns an awaitable future. The event loop continues serving other coroutines while the computation runs in parallel. This is a fundamental pattern for mixing I/O-bound async code with CPU-bound computation in SPADE.

        13.2.2: Jason Platform

        Jason is an interpreter for an extended version of AgentSpeak(L), a logic-programming agent language that implements the BDI architecture natively.

        AgentSpeak(L) Formal Syntax

        An AgentSpeak agent is a set of beliefs, goals, and plans:

        $$\text{Agent} = \langle \mathcal{BB},\; \mathcal{G},\; \mathcal{PL} \rangle$$

        Belief Base $\mathcal{BB}$: Ground atoms representing the agent's current world model.

        $$\mathcal{BB} = \{p_1(t_1, \ldots), p_2(\ldots), \ldots\}$$

        Goals:

        • Achievement goal: $!g$ (make $g$ true)
        • Test goal: $?g$ (query current beliefs for $g$)

        Plans:

        $$\pi = \langle te,\; cg,\; pb \rangle$$

        • $te$ = triggering event (e.g., $+!g$ = achievement goal added, $+p$ = belief added)
        • $cg$ = context guard (logical condition over $\mathcal{BB}$; must hold to select plan)
        • $pb$ = plan body (sequence of actions, goals, tests)

        $$\pi ::= te\; [:\; cg]\; \leftarrow\; pb.$$

        Example Plan (price negotiation):

        $$+!\text{negotiate}(\text{item}, P) : \text{budget}(B) \wedge P < B \leftarrow \text{accept}(\text{item}, P).$$

        $$+!\text{negotiate}(\text{item}, P) : \text{budget}(B) \wedge P \geq B \leftarrow \text{counteroffer}(\text{item}, B \times 0.9).$$

        Jason Reasoning Cycle

        Jason's execution follows a formal 10-step reasoning cycle at each reasoning step $rs$:

        $$rs_1: \text{Perceive environment} \to \Delta_{percept}$$

        $$rs_2: \text{Update } \mathcal{BB} \text{ with } \Delta_{percept}$$

        $$rs_3: \text{Receive messages} \to M_{inbox}$$

        $$rs_4: \text{Process } M_{inbox} \to \text{update } \mathcal{BB} \text{ and } \mathcal{G}$$

        $$rs_5: \text{Select event} \; e \text{ from Event Queue}$$

        $$rs_6: \text{Retrieve relevant plans} \; \mathcal{PL}_{rel} = \{\pi \in \mathcal{PL} : te(\pi) \text{ unifies with } e\}$$

        $$rs_7: \text{Filter applicable plans} \; \mathcal{PL}_{app} = \{\pi \in \mathcal{PL}_{rel} : \mathcal{BB} \models cg(\pi)\}$$

        $$rs_8: \text{Select plan} \; \pi^* \in \mathcal{PL}_{app} \text{ (by selection function } \text{Sel}_{plan}\text{)}$$

        $$rs_9: \text{Push intention} \; pb(\pi^*) \text{ onto Intention Stack}$$

        $$rs_{10}: \text{Execute one step of top intention}$$

        Selection functions $\text{Sel}_{event}$ and $\text{Sel}_{plan}$ are programmer-defined meta-level functions, giving fine-grained control over event and plan selection.

        Formal Semantics: Operational Transition System

        Jason's semantics is defined as a transition system over agent states:

        $$\Sigma = \langle \mathcal{BB},\; \mathcal{G},\; \mathcal{PL},\; I,\; E \rangle$$

        Where $I$ = intention stack (set of active intentions), $E$ = event queue.

        Transition: $\Sigma \xrightarrow{rs} \Sigma'$

        The full transition rules (Bordini et al., 2007) specify how each reasoning step modifies $\Sigma$.

        Q and A 13.2.2

        Q1: In Jason, what happens when no applicable plan exists for a triggered goal?

        A: When no applicable plan is found (either no relevant plans for the event, or all relevant plans have failing context guards), Jason generates a failure event: $-!\text{goal}(\text{args})$. If the programmer has defined a failure recovery plan with triggering event $-!g$, that plan is selected and executed. If no failure plan exists, the intention that triggered the goal is dropped and an error is generated. This is analogous to an unhandled exception in object-oriented programming. Good Jason programming practice requires defining failure handling plans for every critical goal, particularly when network or resource failures may cause action execution to fail.

        13.2.3: Comparative Analysis

        Framework Comparison Matrix

        DimensionJADESPADEJason
        Implementation languageJavaPythonJava (AgentSpeak interpreter)
        Agent programming paradigmImperative OOPAsync coroutinesDeclarative BDI (AgentSpeak)
        FIPA complianceFullPartial (via XMPP)Partial
        Agent reasoning modelManual (programmer-defined)ManualBDI (formal)
        Learning curveModerateLowHigh (new language)
        Scalability (single host)Hundreds of agentsThousands of agentsHundreds of agents
        Formal verification supportNoneNoneYes (via AAMAS tools)
        Industry adoptionHigh (enterprise)Growing (IoT, AI)Academic
        Best suited forFIPA-compliant enterprise MASLightweight, IoT, Python ecosystemFormal BDI research and applications

        Performance Model

        Message throughput for a platform with $n$ agents, each exchanging $r$ messages per second:

        $$\text{Total message rate} = n(n-1) \cdot r \text{ (fully connected)}$$

        $$\text{or} \quad n \cdot r \text{ (star topology)}$$

        For JADE (JVM overhead per message $\approx 50 \mu s$):

        $$\text{Max throughput} \approx \frac{1}{50 \mu s} = 20{,}000 \text{ messages/second/agent}$$

        For SPADE (Python asyncio overhead $\approx 20 \mu s$ for local):

        $$\text{Max throughput} \approx \frac{1}{20 \mu s} = 50{,}000 \text{ messages/second}$$

        Note: SPADE's single-threaded model means this throughput is shared across all agents in one process, not per agent.

        Choosing a Framework: Decision Criteria

        $$\text{Framework}^* = \arg\max_F \sum_j w_j \cdot \text{score}(F, \text{criterion}_j)$$

        Key criteria and their weights for common use cases:

        Use CaseTop CriteriaRecommended Framework
        FIPA-standards-based enterpriseCompliance, robustnessJADE
        IoT, chatbots, Python ML integrationEase of use, ecosystemSPADE
        Formal agent reasoning researchBDI fidelity, verificationJason
        Rapid prototypingDevelopment speedSPADE
        Safety-critical certified MASFormal semanticsJason

        Q and A 13.2.3

        Q1: A team must build a MAS that interoperates with an existing FIPA-compliant system AND uses Python machine learning libraries. Which framework should they use, and how?

        A: Neither JADE nor SPADE is a perfect fit alone. The recommended architecture is a bridge pattern: use JADE as the FIPA-compliant backbone for inter-agent messaging, and expose a REST or gRPC interface from JADE. Build Python SPADE agents (or plain Python services) that consume this interface to run ML inference. A lightweight JADE agent acts as a proxy: it receives FIPA ACL messages, translates them to REST calls, receives ML results, and sends FIPA INFORM replies. This way, the FIPA-compliant outer interface is preserved while Python ML libraries are fully available. The bridge agent's overhead is one additional network hop, which is acceptable for most use cases where ML inference already dominates latency.

        13.3: Modern AI Agent Frameworks

        The rise of Large Language Models has produced a new generation of agent frameworks that use LLMs as the cognitive core rather than symbolic reasoning engines.

        13.3.1: LangChain Agents

        LangChain provides abstractions for building LLM-powered agents that can use tools, maintain memory, and chain reasoning steps.

        Core Abstraction: Agent Loop

        The LangChain agent loop formalises the ReAct (Reason + Act) pattern:

        $$\text{AgentLoop}(q) : q \xrightarrow{\text{LLM}} (\text{thought}, \text{action}, \text{input}) \xrightarrow{\text{Tool}} \text{observation} \xrightarrow{\text{LLM}} \ldots \xrightarrow{\text{LLM}} \text{final answer}$$

        Formal trace:

        $$\tau = (q, t_1, a_1, o_1, t_2, a_2, o_2, \ldots, t_k, \text{Final Answer}: ans)$$

        Where $t_i$ = thought, $a_i$ = action (tool name), $o_i$ = observation (tool output).

        At each step, the LLM receives the full trace so far and generates the next thought-action pair:

        $$P(t_{i+1}, a_{i+1} | q, t_1, a_1, o_1, \ldots, o_i) = \text{LLM}_\theta(q, t_1, a_1, o_1, \ldots, o_i)$$

        Tool Integration

        Tools are functions with typed signatures:

        $$\text{Tool} = \langle \text{name},\; \text{description},\; f : \text{Input} \to \text{Output} \rangle$$

        The LLM selects tools by parsing its own output. The tool's name and description are provided in the system prompt to guide selection.

        Tool selection accuracy depends on description quality. For a set of $k$ tools with description vectors $\{d_j\}$:

        $$P(\text{correct tool} | \text{query}) \propto \text{Similarity}(d_{j^*}, \text{query embedding})$$

        Where $j^* = $ the correct tool. Better descriptions increase similarity to relevant queries and decrease similarity to irrelevant ones.

        Memory in LangChain

        LangChain provides multiple memory types (covered in detail in Chapter 14), plugged into the agent loop:

        $$\text{Context}_{t+1} = \text{Memory.update}(\text{Context}_t, (q, \tau_t))$$

        The context window length constraint:

        $$|\text{Context}_t|_{tokens} \leq L_{max}$$

        Where $L_{max}$ is the LLM's maximum context window (e.g., 128,000 tokens for GPT-4o). When exceeded, memory summarisation or retrieval is triggered.

        13.3.2: CrewAI

        CrewAI is a framework for orchestrating role-playing agents into structured workflows where each agent has a defined role, goal, and backstory.

        Crew Formal Model

        $$\text{Crew} = \langle \{A_i\}_{i=1}^n,\; \{T_j\}_{j=1}^m,\; \text{Process},\; \text{Manager} \rangle$$

        Agent definition:

        $$A_i = \langle \text{role}_i,\; \text{goal}_i,\; \text{backstory}_i,\; \text{tools}_i,\; \text{LLM}_i \rangle$$

        • $\text{role}_i$: Natural language description of the agent's function (e.g., "Senior Financial Analyst")
        • $\text{goal}_i$: What the agent is trying to achieve
        • $\text{backstory}_i$: Context that shapes the agent's perspective and reasoning style
        • $\text{tools}_i$: Set of tools the agent can use
        • $\text{LLM}_i$: The underlying language model (may differ per agent)

        Task definition:

        $$T_j = \langle \text{description}_j,\; \text{agent}(T_j),\; \text{context}(T_j),\; \text{output_format}_j \rangle$$

        • $\text{description}_j$: What must be accomplished
        • $\text{agent}(T_j)$: Which agent is responsible (or auto-assigned)
        • $\text{context}(T_j) \subseteq \{T_k : k < j\}$: Prior tasks whose outputs feed this task
        • $\text{output_format}_j$: Expected output structure (Pydantic model, free text, etc.)

        Process Types

        Sequential Process:

        $$T_1 \to T_2 \to \cdots \to T_m$$

        Tasks execute in order. The output of $T_j$ is automatically passed as context to $T_{j+1}$.

        Hierarchical Process:

        A manager agent (powered by an LLM) receives the overall goal and dynamically assigns tasks to agents:

        $$\text{Manager} \xrightarrow{\text{assign}} T_j \xrightarrow{\text{execute}} A_{k(j)} \xrightarrow{\text{result}} \text{Manager}$$

        The manager decides task order, delegation, and when the goal is complete. Formally equivalent to a Dec-POMDP with the manager as a meta-planner.

        Task Dependency Graph

        $$G_{task} = (T, E)$$

        Where $(T_j, T_k) \in E$ iff $T_j \in \text{context}(T_k)$.

        Information flow: The output of $T_j$ becomes part of the input context for $T_k$ when $(T_j, T_k) \in E$.

        Context accumulation for task $T_k$:

        $$\text{Input}(T_k) = \text{description}(T_k) + \sum_{T_j \in \text{context}(T_k)} \text{Output}(T_j)$$

        The total token count:

        $$|\text{Input}(T_k)|_{tokens} = |\text{description}(T_k)| + \sum_{T_j \in \text{context}(T_k)} |\text{Output}(T_j)|$$

        Must satisfy $|\text{Input}(T_k)| \leq L_{max}$. Deep dependency chains risk exceeding context limits.

        13.3.3: AutoGen

        AutoGen (Microsoft, 2023) enables multi-agent conversations where agents collaborate through structured dialogue to solve complex tasks.

        Conversational Agent Model

        $$\text{AutoGen Agent} = \langle \text{name},\; \text{system_message},\; \text{LLM_config},\; \text{human_input_mode},\; \text{code_execution_config} \rangle$$

        Conversation between agents $A$ and $B$:

        $$\mathcal{C} = \langle m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_k \rangle \quad m_i = \langle \text{sender},\; \text{content},\; \text{timestamp} \rangle$$

        Each agent receives the full conversation history and generates its next message:

        $$m_{k+1} = \text{LLM}_{A}(\text{system}_{A},\; m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_k) \quad \text{if sender of next turn is } A$$

        Group Chat Model

        Multiple agents participate in a single conversation:

        $$\text{GroupChat} = \langle \{A_i\}_{i=1}^n,\; \text{max_rounds},\; \text{speaker_selection} \rangle$$

        Speaker selection policies:

        PolicyRule
