        round_robin$A_1 \to A_2 \to \cdots \to A_n \to A_1 \to \ldots$
        random$A_{next} \sim U(\{A_i\})$
        autoGroupChatManager LLM selects next speaker based on context
        manualHuman selects next speaker

        Auto speaker selection:

        $$A_{next} = \text{LLM}_{manager}(\text{system},\; \mathcal{C},\; \{A_i\}_{i=1}^n)$$

        The manager reads the conversation and picks the most contextually appropriate next speaker.

        Code Execution in AutoGen

        AutoGen allows agents to write and execute code in a sandboxed environment:

        $$\text{Code block in message} \xrightarrow{\text{UserProxyAgent}} \text{Executor} \xrightarrow{\text{result}} \text{Execution result}$$

        UserProxyAgent acts as the human proxy: it receives LLM-generated code, executes it, and returns the output as the next message.

        Formal code execution loop:

        $$\text{AssistantAgent generates code} \to \text{UserProxyAgent executes code} \to \text{AssistantAgent reads output} \to \text{(iterate)}$$

        This loop continues until the AssistantAgent generates a message containing no code (signalling task completion) or max_rounds is reached.

        Safety: Code is executed in a Docker container or local process with restricted permissions. The executor captures stdout, stderr, and return codes.

        Comparison of Modern Frameworks

        PropertyLangChainCrewAIAutoGen
        Primary abstractionAgent loop with toolsRole-playing crew workflowMulti-agent conversation
        Task structureSingle agent with tool chainPredefined roles and tasksEmergent through dialogue
        Human-in-loopOptionalOptionalNative (UserProxyAgent)
        Code executionVia toolVia toolNative
        Coordination styleTool orchestrationManager or sequentialConversational
        Best forTool-augmented single agent tasksStructured multi-role workflowsCollaborative problem-solving with code

        Q and A 13.3

        Q1: In AutoGen's group chat with auto speaker selection, what are the failure modes and how are they mitigated?

        A: Failure modes include: (1) Looping: two agents keep responding to each other in a cycle without progress. Mitigation: max_rounds terminates the conversation after a fixed number of turns. (2) Off-topic diversion: the manager LLM selects an irrelevant agent because of prompt confusion. Mitigation: careful system message engineering for the GroupChatManager that explicitly instructs it to select based on task relevance. (3) Premature termination: an agent generates a termination signal before the task is complete. Mitigation: define explicit termination conditions (e.g., require a specific keyword only when all subtasks are verified complete). (4) Context overflow: the full conversation history exceeds the context window. Mitigation: implement conversation summarisation before each LLM call.

        Q2: How does CrewAI's hierarchical process relate to the classical MAS concept of a manager agent?

        A: CrewAI's hierarchical process is a direct implementation of the manager-worker pattern from classical MAS. The manager agent in CrewAI corresponds to a deliberative coordinator that: (a) receives a complex high-level goal, (b) decomposes it into subtasks (task decomposition), (c) assigns subtasks to the most capable agents (task allocation, analogous to Contract Net Protocol), (d) monitors execution and integrates results (coordination). The key difference from classical MAS is that the manager is an LLM, so its decomposition and allocation decisions are not computed by a formal algorithm but generated by language model inference. This gives flexibility (natural language task descriptions, no formal ontology required) at the cost of determinism and formal correctness guarantees.

        Chapter 14: LLM-Based Multi-Agent Systems

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Formally model the architecture of LLM-powered autonomous agents
        • Analyse planner-executor patterns, reflection loops, and workflow graphs
        • Mathematically characterise short-term and long-term memory systems
        • Understand tool-using agents and their interaction with external APIs
        • Design and evaluate retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems

        14.1: Autonomous AI Agents

        14.1.1: Planner-Executor Systems

        A Planner-Executor system separates the task of generating a plan from the task of executing individual plan steps, allowing each concern to be handled by the most appropriate component.

        Formal Architecture

        $$\text{System} = \langle \text{Planner},\; \text{Executor},\; \text{Environment},\; \text{Memory} \rangle$$

        Planner:

        $$\text{Plan} = \text{Planner}(G,\; \text{Context},\; \text{Tools})$$

        $$\text{Plan} = (s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_k)$$

        Where $G$ is the goal, context includes relevant memory, tools is the set of available actions, and $s_j$ is step $j$.

        Executor:

        $$o_j = \text{Executor}(s_j,\; \text{Context}_j)$$

        Each step $s_j$ is executed to produce observation $o_j$. The context for step $j$ includes all prior observations $\{o_1, \ldots, o_{j-1}\}$.

        Replanning trigger:

        $$\text{Replan if} \quad o_j \not\models \text{expected}(s_j) \quad \text{or} \quad \text{PlanValid}(\text{Plan}, \{o_l\}_{l \leq j}) = \text{False}$$

        Task Decomposition with LLM Planner

        The LLM planner converts a natural language goal into a structured plan:

        $$G_{NL} \xrightarrow{\text{LLM}_\theta} \{(s_j, \text{dep}(s_j), \text{tool}(s_j), \text{arg}(s_j))\}_{j=1}^k$$

        Where:

        • $\text{dep}(s_j)$ = set of steps that must complete before $s_j$ starts
        • $\text{tool}(s_j)$ = tool to invoke for step $s_j$
        • $\text{arg}(s_j)$ = arguments (possibly referencing earlier step outputs)

        Dependency DAG:

        $$G_{plan} = (S, E_{dep})$$

        $$E_{dep} = \{(s_i, s_j) : s_i \in \text{dep}(s_j)\}$$

        Steps with no predecessors in $G_{plan}$ can execute in parallel.

        Parallel speedup:

        $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{\sum_j t_j}{\text{CriticalPath}(G_{plan})}$$

        Where $t_j$ = execution time of step $s_j$.

        Plan Evaluation Metrics

        Plan quality:

        $$Q(\text{Plan}) = \frac{\text{Steps achieving goal}}{|\text{Plan}|} - \lambda \cdot |\text{Plan}| \cdot c_{\text{per-step}}$$

        Task completion rate over a benchmark of $N$ tasks:

        $$TCR = \frac{|\{G \in \mathcal{G} : \text{Plan}(G) \text{ successfully achieves } G\}|}{N}$$

        Average step efficiency:

        $$\eta = \frac{\text{Optimal steps}}{\text{Average steps taken}}$$

        Q and A 14.1.1

        Q1: Why might a Planner-Executor system outperform a single ReAct agent on long-horizon tasks?

        A: In a single ReAct agent, the plan emerges step-by-step: at each turn the agent decides the next action based on all prior observations. For long-horizon tasks (many steps), two problems arise. First, the agent may lose track of the overall goal as the context fills with step details, causing drift. Second, opportunities for parallelism are missed because the agent always reasons about the next single step. A Planner-Executor system addresses both: the planner sees the full goal and produces a structured plan with explicit parallel branches before execution begins. The executor handles individual steps with focused prompts. The planner can also represent dependencies explicitly, enabling concurrent execution of independent steps and reducing total wallclock time by the parallelism factor $\sum t_j / \text{CriticalPath}$.

        14.1.2: Reflection Mechanisms

        Reflection is the ability of an agent to evaluate and improve its own outputs or reasoning before finalising a response or action.

        Self-Reflection Loop (Shinn et al., Reflexion)

        The Reflexion framework adds a reflection step after each trial:

        $$\text{Trial}_{t+1} = \text{Agent}(G,\; \text{Memory} + \text{Reflection}_t)$$

        $$\text{Reflection}_t = \text{LLM}(\text{Task},\; \text{Trajectory}_t,\; \text{Outcome}_t)$$

        The reflection summarises what went wrong and what to do differently.

        Formal improvement model:

        Let $P_t$ = probability of task success at trial $t$.

        With reflection:

        $$P_{t+1} \geq P_t + \Delta_{\text{reflection}}$$

        Where $\Delta_{\text{reflection}} > 0$ is the improvement from incorporating the reflected lesson.

        Convergence bound:

        $$P_t \to P^* \leq 1 \quad \text{as } t \to \infty$$

        The limit $P^*$ depends on whether the task is achievable with the available tools and LLM capability. Empirically, Reflexion achieves significant gains in 1--3 trials on benchmarks like HotpotQA and AlfWorld.

        Critic-Actor Pattern

        A separate Critic agent evaluates the Actor's output:

        $$\text{Critique} = \text{Critic}(\text{Output}_{\text{Actor}},\; \text{Rubric})$$

        $$\text{Revised Output} = \text{Actor}(\text{Original Task},\; \text{Critique})$$

        Rubric-based evaluation:

        $$\text{Score} = \sum_{c \in \text{Criteria}} w_c \cdot \text{score}(c, \text{Output})$$

        Where $w_c$ = weight of criterion $c$ and $\text{score}(c, \cdot) \in [0,1]$.

        Multi-round refinement:

        $$O_0 = \text{Actor}(G)$$

        $$\text{for } t = 0, 1, \ldots, T:$$

        $$Cr_t = \text{Critic}(O_t, \text{Rubric})$$

        $$O_{t+1} = \text{Actor}(G, O_t, Cr_t)$$

        The loop terminates when $\text{Score}(O_t) \geq \theta$ (threshold) or $t = T$.

        Expected quality improvement:

        $$\mathbb{E}[\text{Score}(O_T)] \geq \mathbb{E}[\text{Score}(O_0)] \quad \text{(assuming non-trivial critic)}$$

        Strict improvement requires that the critic provides actionable feedback that the actor can use to correct specific deficiencies.

        Tree of Thoughts (ToT)

        ToT extends chain-of-thought by maintaining a tree of reasoning paths:

        $$\mathcal{T} = (V, E) \quad V = \{\text{thoughts}\}, \quad E = \{\text{continuation relationships}\}$$

        Search over the tree:

        At each node $v \in V$ with current thought $t_v$:

        1. Generate $k$ child thoughts: $\{t_{v,1}, \ldots, t_{v,k}\} = \text{LLM}(t_v)^k$
        2. Evaluate each child: $\text{score}(t_{v,j}) = \text{Evaluator}(t_{v,j})$
        3. Select top-$b$ children (beam search) or continue all (BFS) or sample (MCTS)

          4. Best-first search on ToT:

          $$v^* = \arg\max_{v \in \text{leaves}(\mathcal{T})} \text{score}(v)$$

          MCTS applied to ToT:

          $$\text{UCT}(v) = \text{score}(v) + c\sqrt{\frac{\ln N(parent(v))}{N(v)}}$$

          Where $N(v)$ = number of times node $v$ has been visited.

          Q and A 14.1.2

          Q1: In the Critic-Actor loop, what prevents the loop from cycling without improvement?

          A: Two mechanisms prevent cycling. First, the critic should provide concrete, actionable feedback rather than vague assessments. Specifically, the critic must identify (a) what is wrong, (b) where it is wrong, and (c) how to fix it. Vague critiques like "needs improvement" give the actor nothing to act on. Second, a termination condition based on score threshold or maximum iterations prevents infinite loops. Formally, if the critic's feedback is randomly generated rather than ground-truth-aligned, the loop may cycle. Ensuring the critic uses a consistent, rubric-grounded evaluation function that is correlated with actual quality is essential. In practice, grounding the critic with few-shot examples of good and bad outputs significantly improves the signal quality.

        14.1.3: Agent Workflows

        Agent workflows define the structured coordination pattern for multi-agent systems built on LLMs.

        Workflow Graph Formalism

        $$\mathcal{W} = (N, E, \text{entry}, \text{terminals})$$

        • $N$ = set of nodes (agents, tools, conditional branches, aggregators)
        • $E \subseteq N \times N$ = directed edges (data flow)
        • $\text{entry} \in N$ = starting node
        • $\text{terminals} \subseteq N$ = terminal nodes (workflow ends when reached)

        Node types:

        Node TypeFunctionFormal Model
        Agent NodeLLM-powered agent$o = A_i(\text{inputs})$
        Tool NodeAPI or function call$o = f(\text{inputs})$
        Router NodeConditional branching$\text{next} = \text{condition}(\text{inputs})$
        Aggregator NodeMerge multiple inputs$o = \text{agg}(\{o_i\})$
        Human NodeHuman-in-the-loop approval$o = \text{human_review}(\text{inputs})$

        Sequential Workflow

        $$\text{Entry} \to N_1 \to N_2 \to \cdots \to N_k \to \text{Terminal}$$

        Total latency:

        $$L_{seq} = \sum_{j=1}^k t_{N_j}$$

        Where $t_{N_j}$ = processing time of node $N_j$.

        Parallel Workflow

        Independent branches execute simultaneously:

        $$\text{Entry} \to \{N_1^a, N_1^b, N_1^c\} \xrightarrow{\text{parallel}} \text{Aggregator} \to \text{Terminal}$$

        Latency:

        $$L_{par} = \max(t_{N_1^a}, t_{N_1^b}, t_{N_1^c}) + t_{\text{Agg}}$$

        Speedup over sequential:

        $$S = \frac{t_{N_1^a} + t_{N_1^b} + t_{N_1^c} + t_{\text{Agg}}}{L_{par}} = \frac{\sum t_j + t_{\text{Agg}}}{\max t_j + t_{\text{Agg}}}$$

        Map-Reduce Workflow

        Map phase: Distribute input chunks to parallel agents.

        $$\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\} = \text{Split}(\text{input})$$

        $$o_i = A_i(c_i) \quad \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\}$$

        Reduce phase: Aggregate results.

        $$\text{output} = \text{Reduce}(o_1, o_2, \ldots, o_n)$$

        Map-reduce is appropriate for tasks decomposable into independent chunks: document analysis, multi-source research, batch classification.

        Total latency:

        $$L_{MR} = t_{\text{split}} + \max_i t_{A_i} + t_{\text{reduce}}$$

        Throughput scaling: Adding $n$ parallel agents processes $n$ chunks simultaneously, reducing latency from $O(n \cdot t_A)$ to $O(t_A + t_{\text{split}} + t_{\text{reduce}})$.

        14.2: Memory and Tool Usage

        14.2.1: Short-Term Memory

        Short-term memory in LLM agents is the information maintained within a single conversation context window.

        Context Window as Working Memory

        $$\text{Context} = [\text{System Prompt},\; h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_t]$$

        Where $h_i = (role_i, content_i)$ is the $i$-th message in the conversation history.

        Token budget:

        $$\sum_{i=0}^{t} |h_i|_{\text{tokens}} \leq L_{\max}$$

        When the budget is exhausted, older history must be dropped, summarised, or compressed.

        Attention as Memory Retrieval

        Transformer self-attention computes:

        $$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

        Every token in the context can attend to every other token. This is the mechanism by which the model "remembers" earlier parts of the conversation.

        Memory capacity: The model can attend to all $L_{\max}$ tokens simultaneously, but the softmax normalisation means that attention weight on any single early token decreases as more tokens are added. This creates a soft forgetting effect for very long contexts.

        Attention pattern for memory:

        Let the current query be at position $t$ and a relevant memory be at position $j \ll t$:

        $$\alpha_{t,j} = \frac{\exp(q_t \cdot k_j / \sqrt{d_k})}{\sum_{l=1}^{t} \exp(q_t \cdot k_l / \sqrt{d_k})}$$

        If $t$ is large and many distractors exist, $\alpha_{t,j}$ may be negligibly small even if $k_j$ is highly relevant.

        Context Compression Strategies

        Summarisation: Replace a block of $b$ messages with a summary of $s$ tokens ($s \ll b \cdot \bar{l}$):

        $$h_{1:b} \xrightarrow{\text{LLM}} \text{Summary}$$

        $$\text{Compressed Context} = [\text{Summary}, h_{b+1}, \ldots, h_t]$$

        Compression ratio: $r = \frac{s}{\sum_{i=1}^b |h_i|}$. Typically $r < 0.2$ is achievable while preserving key facts.

        Information loss:

        $$\text{Loss} = H(\text{original messages}) - H(\text{summary})$$

        Measured by the entropy reduction. Perfect compression retains all decision-relevant information; practical compression loses some detail.

        Sliding window: Keep only the last $W$ messages:

        $$\text{Context}_t = h_{t-W+1}, h_{t-W+2}, \ldots, h_t$$

        Simple but discards potentially relevant early information entirely.

        Q and A 14.2.1

        Q1: An agent's context window is 8,192 tokens and average message length is 200 tokens. After how many turns does the agent need to start compressing?

        A: Available tokens for conversation: $8192 - |\text{System Prompt}|$. Assuming system prompt uses 500 tokens, available = $7692$ tokens. Messages per turn: 2 (user + assistant), so tokens per turn $\approx 400$. Maximum turns before overflow: $\lfloor 7692 / 400 \rfloor = 19$ turns. The agent should start compressing after approximately 15--17 turns (leaving buffer for the current turn's generation). A practical trigger: compress when remaining tokens $< 2 \times \bar{l} \times k$ where $\bar{l}$ = average message length and $k$ = messages to keep uncompressed for immediate context.

        14.2.2: Long-Term Memory

        Long-term memory stores information that persists across conversation sessions and is retrieved on demand.

        Memory Architecture

        $$\text{Long-Term Memory} = \langle \text{Episodic},\; \text{Semantic},\; \text{Procedural} \rangle$$

        Episodic Memory: Records of specific past interactions.

        $$\text{EpisodicStore} = \{(q_i, \tau_i, \text{outcome}_i, t_i)\}_{i=1}^N$$

        Where $\tau_i$ = trajectory (tool calls, observations) of past interaction $i$.

        Semantic Memory: Extracted facts and knowledge.

        $$\text{SemanticStore} = \{(\text{fact}_j, \text{confidence}_j, \text{source}_j)\}_{j=1}^M$$

        Procedural Memory: Learned skills or plans.

        $$\text{ProceduralStore} = \{(\text{trigger}_k, \text{plan}_k)\}_{k=1}^P$$

        Memory Encoding

        Memories are encoded as dense vectors using an embedding model $\phi$:

        $$\mathbf{m}_i = \phi(\text{memory content}_i) \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

        Common embedding dimensions: $d \in \{384, 768, 1536, 3072\}$ depending on model.

        Memory Retrieval

        Given a query $q$, retrieve top-$K$ memories:

        $$\text{Retrieve}(q, K) = \text{top-}K_{i} \; \text{sim}(\phi(q), \mathbf{m}_i)$$

        Cosine similarity:

        $$\text{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\| \|\mathbf{v}\|}$$

        Retrieval precision:

        $$\text{Precision@}K = \frac{|\text{retrieved} \cap \text{relevant}|}{K}$$

        Retrieval recall:

        $$\text{Recall@}K = \frac{|\text{retrieved} \cap \text{relevant}|}{|\text{relevant}|}$$

        Memory Decay and Forgetting

        Memories may become stale. A time-decayed relevance score:

        $$\text{score}(m_i, q, t) = \text{sim}(\phi(q), \mathbf{m}_i) \cdot e^{-\lambda (t - t_i)}$$

        Where $\lambda > 0$ = decay rate and $t_i$ = creation timestamp.

        Spaced Repetition Reinforcement: Accessed memories have their decay reset:

        $$t_i \leftarrow t \quad \text{whenever memory } i \text{ is retrieved}$$

        This models how frequently accessed information remains more accessible than rarely used information.

        14.2.3: Tool-Using Agents

        Tool-using agents extend their capabilities beyond text generation by calling external functions, APIs, and services.

        Formal Tool Call Model

        $$\text{Tool Call} = \langle \text{tool_name},\; \text{arguments},\; \text{call_id} \rangle$$

        $$\text{Tool Result} = \langle \text{call_id},\; \text{content},\; \text{status} \rangle$$

        Tool schema: Defined in JSON Schema or equivalent:

        $$\text{Schema}(T) = \langle \text{name},\; \text{description},\; \text{parameters},\; \text{returns} \rangle$$

        The LLM uses the schema to construct syntactically valid tool calls.

        Function Calling Probability Model

        Given a user query $q$ and available tools $\{T_1, \ldots, T_k\}$, the LLM assigns:

        $$P(\text{call } T_j | q, \{T_i\}) \propto \exp\left(\text{LLM_score}(T_j, q)\right)$$

        The LLM score is a soft measure of tool relevance to the query, computed internally by the model's learned representations.

        Tool selection accuracy on a benchmark of $N$ queries:

        $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{|\{q \in \mathcal{Q} : \text{selected tool}(q) = \text{ground-truth tool}(q)\}|}{N}$$

        Parallel Tool Calling

        Modern LLMs support calling multiple tools in a single turn:

        $$\text{Calls}_t = \{(T_{j_1}, \arg_{j_1}), (T_{j_2}, \arg_{j_2}), \ldots\}$$

        All calls execute in parallel:

        $$\text{Results}_t = \{(T_{j_l}, f_{T_{j_l}}(\arg_{j_l}))\}$$

        Latency reduction: Instead of $\sum_l t_{T_{j_l}}$ (sequential), parallel calling achieves $\max_l t_{T_{j_l}}$.

        Error Handling in Tool Use

        Tool calls can fail (network error, invalid arguments, rate limits). A robust agent implements:

        $$\text{for each tool call failure}:$$

        $$\text{Retry if } \text{status} \in \{\text{TIMEOUT, RATE_LIMIT}\} \quad \text{up to } n_{\text{retry}} \text{ times}$$

        $$\text{Abort and report if } \text{status} \in \{\text{INVALID_ARGS, NOT_FOUND}\}$$

        Exponential backoff for retries:

        $$t_{\text{wait}}^{(k)} = \min(\text{cap},\; t_0 \cdot b^k + \text{jitter})$$

        Where $b$ = backoff base (typically 2), $t_0$ = initial wait, $k$ = retry number, jitter $\sim U(0, t_0)$ prevents thundering herd.

        Q and A 14.2.3

        Q1: An agent has access to 50 tools. Why might tool selection accuracy decrease compared to having 10 tools, and how can this be mitigated?

        A: With more tools, the LLM must distinguish among more candidates, and the probability mass for any single tool decreases. Similar tools cause confusion (e.g., searchweb vs searchacademic vs search_news). The softmax over 50 tool scores is more diffuse, making confident selection harder. Additionally, the system prompt describing all 50 tools may exceed the context budget, forcing truncation. Mitigations include: (1) Hierarchical tool selection: first select a tool category, then the specific tool within that category, reducing each selection from 50 to $\sqrt{50} \approx 7$ options; (2) Retrieval-based tool selection: embed all tool descriptions and retrieve the top-$k$ most relevant before presenting them to the LLM, so the LLM always sees fewer than $k$ candidates; (3) Tool documentation quality: ensure each tool has a clearly differentiated description with explicit use cases and exclusion criteria.

        14.3: Retrieval-Augmented Generation

        Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) extends LLMs by dynamically retrieving relevant documents from an external knowledge base before generating a response.

        14.3.1: Vector Databases

        Vector databases store and index high-dimensional embedding vectors for efficient similarity search.

        Data Model

        A vector database stores a collection of items:

        $$\mathcal{D} = \{(id_i, \mathbf{v}_i, \text{metadata}_i) : i = 1, \ldots, N\}$$

        Where $\mathbf{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ = embedding vector and $\text{metadata}_i$ = associated text, source, timestamp, etc.

        Approximate Nearest Neighbour (ANN) Search

        Exact nearest neighbour search is $O(N \cdot d)$ per query (brute force). For large $N$, ANN algorithms trade small accuracy loss for large speed gains.

        HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World):

        HNSW builds a layered graph:

        • Layer 0: all nodes, dense connections
        • Layer $l > 0$: randomly sampled subset of nodes, sparser connections

        Search algorithm: Start at top layer, greedily navigate toward query, descend to lower layers when stuck.

        HNSW complexity:

        $$\text{Query time} = O(\log N) \quad \text{(average)}$$

        $$\text{Index build time} = O(N \log N)$$

        $$\text{Memory} = O(N \cdot M) \quad \text{where } M = \text{neighbours per node}$$

        Recall-speed trade-off parameter $ef$:

        $$\text{Recall}(ef) = \frac{|ANN_ef(q) \cap NN(q)|}{k}$$

        Larger $ef$ = higher recall, slower search. Typical operating point: $ef = 100$, recall $> 0.95$.

        IVF (Inverted File Index)

        Partition the embedding space into $n_{\text{lists}}$ Voronoi cells via k-means:

        $$\text{Cell}(c) = \{i : c = \arg\min_j \|\mathbf{v}_i - \mu_j\|\}$$

        Query: Find the $n_{\text{probe}}$ nearest centroids $\{\mu_{j_1}, \ldots, \mu_{j_{n_{probe}}}\}$, then search only within those cells.

        $$\text{Query time} = O(n_{\text{probe}} \cdot |\text{Cell}|)$$

        $$\approx O\left(\frac{n_{\text{probe}}}{n_{\text{lists}}} \cdot N\right)$$

        For $n_{\text{probe}} = 10$, $n_{\text{lists}} = 1000$: query searches $1\%$ of the database.

        Q and A 14.3.1

        Q1: A RAG system embeds 1 million documents using a 1536-dimensional embedding model. How much memory is required for the raw vectors, and how does HNSW reduce query time compared to brute-force?

        A: Raw vector memory: $N \times d \times 4$ bytes (float32) $= 10^6 \times 1536 \times 4 = 6.144 \times 10^9$ bytes $\approx 6.1$ GB. With HNSW (16 neighbours per node), additional graph memory: $N \times M \times 8$ bytes $= 10^6 \times 16 \times 8 \approx 0.128$ GB. Total HNSW index $\approx 6.3$ GB. Brute-force query: $N \times d = 1.536 \times 10^9$ multiplications per query. HNSW query: $O(\log N) = O(20)$ graph hops, each examining $M = 16$ candidates, so approximately $20 \times 16 = 320$ distance computations. Speedup $\approx 10^6 / 320 \approx 3125 \times$ faster, at typically $> 95\%$ recall.

        14.3.2: Knowledge Retrieval

        RAG Pipeline

        $$q \xrightarrow{\phi} \mathbf{q} \xrightarrow{\text{ANN}} \{(d_i, \mathbf{v}_i)\}_{i \in \text{top-}K} \xrightarrow{\text{LLM}} \text{Answer}$$

        Retrieval step: $\{d_i\}_{i \in \text{top-}K} = \text{ANN}(\phi(q), K)$

        Generation step: $\text{Answer} = \text{LLM}(q, d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_K)$

        Where the retrieved documents $\{d_i\}$ are prepended to the query as additional context.

        Chunking Strategy

        Long documents must be split into chunks before embedding:

        $$D = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_M) \quad |c_j|_{\text{tokens}} \leq L_{\text{chunk}}$$

        Fixed-size chunking:

        $$c_j = D[j \cdot s : j \cdot s + L_{\text{chunk}}]$$

        Where $s$ = stride (number of tokens stepped between chunks). Overlap $= L_{\text{chunk}} - s$ prevents boundary information loss.

        Semantic chunking: Split at natural boundaries (paragraphs, sentences) rather than fixed token counts. Preserves semantic coherence.

        Optimal chunk size trade-off:

        $$\text{Retrieval precision} \uparrow \text{ as chunk size } \downarrow \quad \text{(smaller chunks = more focused)}$$

        $$\text{Retrieval recall} \uparrow \text{ as chunk size } \uparrow \quad \text{(larger chunks = more context per chunk)}$$

        Optimal $L_{\text{chunk}}^* = \arg\max_{L} F_1(L) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision}(L) \cdot \text{Recall}(L)}{\text{Precision}(L) + \text{Recall}(L)}$

        Typical empirical optimum: $L_{\text{chunk}} \in [200, 500]$ tokens.

        Re-ranking

        After initial retrieval, a cross-encoder re-ranker re-scores all $K$ candidates:

        $$\text{score}(d_i | q) = \text{CrossEncoder}(q, d_i)$$

        Cross-encoders process query and document together, producing more accurate relevance scores than bi-encoders (which embed query and document independently).

        $$\text{Final ranking} = \text{sort by score}(d_i | q), \quad i \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$$

        Bi-encoder vs. cross-encoder:

        PropertyBi-encoderCross-encoder
        Query latency$O(d)$ (precomputed doc embeddings)$O(|q| \cdot |d|)$ (full attention)
        ScalabilityIndex $N$ docs, query is fastMust score each candidate at query time
        AccuracyGood for retrievalBetter for ranking
        Typical useFirst-stage retrievalRe-ranking top-$K$

        14.3.3: Context Management

        Context management determines how retrieved information is presented to the LLM to maximise answer quality while respecting token constraints.

        Context Window Allocation

        The total context has $L_{\max}$ tokens. Allocate across components:

        $$L_{\max} = L_{\text{system}} + L_{\text{retrieved}} + L_{\text{history}} + L_{\text{query}} + L_{\text{generation}}$$

        Retrieved context budget:

        $$L_{\text{retrieved}} = K \cdot L_{\text{chunk}} \leq L_{\max} - L_{\text{system}} - L_{\text{history}} - L_{\text{query}} - L_{\text{generation}}$$

        Maximum retrievable chunks:

        $$K_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\text{system}} - L_{\text{history}} - L_{\text{query}} - L_{\text{generation}}}{L_{\text{chunk}}} \right\rfloor$$

        Lost-in-the-Middle Problem

        Research (Liu et al., 2023) shows LLM performance degrades when relevant information is in the middle of a long context:

        $$\text{Performance}(\text{position}) \approx \begin{cases} \text{High} & \text{position near start} \\ \text{Low} & \text{position near middle} \\ \text{High} & \text{position near end} \end{cases}$$

        This U-shaped performance curve suggests placing the most important retrieved chunks at the beginning or end of the retrieved context.

        Optimal placement:

        $$\text{ordered context} = [d_{\text{top-1}}, d_{\text{top-3}}, \ldots, d_{\text{top-}K}, \ldots, d_{\text{top-4}}, d_{\text{top-2}}]$$

        Interleave most-relevant chunks at edges and less-relevant in the middle.

        Faithfulness and Groundedness Metrics

        Faithfulness: Is the generated answer supported by the retrieved context?

        $$\text{Faithfulness} = \frac{|\text{claims in answer supported by context}|}{|\text{total claims in answer}|}$$

        Answer Relevance: Does the answer actually address the query?

        $$\text{Relevance} = \text{sim}(\phi(\text{answer}), \phi(\text{query}))$$

        Context Precision: Are the retrieved chunks relevant to the query?

        $$\text{Context Precision@}K = \frac{|\{d_i \in \text{retrieved} : d_i \text{ is relevant}\}|}{K}$$

        RAGAS Score (composite):

        $$\text{RAGAS} = \frac{4}{\frac{1}{\text{Faithfulness}} + \frac{1}{\text{Relevance}} + \frac{1}{\text{Context Precision}} + \frac{1}{\text{Context Recall}}}$$

        This harmonic mean rewards systems that are simultaneously faithful, relevant, precise, and recall-complete.

        Q and A 14.3.3

        Q1: A RAG system has faithfulness 0.7, relevance 0.9, context precision 0.8, and context recall 0.6. Compute the RAGAS score and identify the weakest component.

        A: $\text{RAGAS} = \frac{4}{\frac{1}{0.7} + \frac{1}{0.9} + \frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.6}} = \frac{4}{1.429 + 1.111 + 1.25 + 1.667} = \frac{4}{5.457} \approx 0.733$. The weakest component is Context Recall = 0.6, which means 40\% of relevant documents are not being retrieved. Improving recall requires either increasing $K$ (retrieve more chunks), improving the embedding model's ability to capture query semantics, adding query expansion (generate multiple query variations and retrieve for each), or improving chunking to avoid splitting relevant information across chunk boundaries.

        Chapter 15: Applications and Case Studies

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Analyse real-world MAS deployments in robotics, smart systems, and business
        • Apply formal models from earlier chapters to concrete application domains
        • Evaluate the design trade-offs made in each application
        • Extract design patterns transferable to new MAS applications

        15.1: Robotics and Automation

        15.1.1: Autonomous Robots

        Multi-Robot System Formal Model

        $$\text{MRS} = \langle \mathcal{R},\; \mathcal{E},\; \mathcal{T},\; \mathcal{C} \rangle$$

        • $\mathcal{R} = \{R_1, \ldots, R_n\}$ = fleet of robots
        • $\mathcal{E}$ = shared physical environment
        • $\mathcal{T}$ = set of tasks to accomplish
        • $\mathcal{C}$ = communication network

        Each robot $R_i$ is an agent with:

        $$R_i = \langle \text{sensors}_i,\; \text{actuators}_i,\; \pi_i,\; \text{capabilities}_i \rangle$$

        Navigation and Localisation

        Each robot maintains a belief state over its position:

        $$b_i(x, y, \theta) = P(\text{pose} = (x, y, \theta) | o_{1:t}^i)$$

        Extended Kalman Filter localisation update:

        Prediction (motion model):

        $$\mu_t^- = f(\mu_{t-1}, u_t)$$

        $$\Sigma_t^- = F_t \Sigma_{t-1} F_t^T + Q_t$$

        Correction (observation model):

        $$K_t = \Sigma_t^- H_t^T (H_t \Sigma_t^- H_t^T + R_t)^{-1}$$

        $$\mu_t = \mu_t^- + K_t (z_t - h(\mu_t^-))$$

        $$\Sigma_t = (I - K_t H_t) \Sigma_t^-$$

        Where $F_t = \nabla_x f|_{\mu_{t-1}, u_t}$, $H_t = \nabla_x h|_{\mu_t^-}$, $Q_t$ = process noise, $R_t$ = measurement noise.

        Multi-Robot Task Allocation (MRTA)

        MRTA problem classification (Gerkey and Mataric):

        $$\text{MRTA} \in \{ST\text{-}SR\text{-}IA, \; ST\text{-}SR\text{-}TA, \; ST\text{-}MR\text{-}TA, \; MT\text{-}MR\text{-}TA\}$$

        • $ST/MT$ = Single-Task / Multi-Task robots (robot does one or multiple tasks at a time)
        • $SR/MR$ = Single-Robot / Multi-Robot tasks (task needs one or multiple robots)
        • $IA/TA$ = Instantaneous Assignment / Time-extended Assignment

        Optimal assignment (ST-SR-IA): Solvable by the Hungarian Algorithm in $O(n^3)$.

        Utility matrix:

        $$U_{ij} = \text{utility of assigning robot } R_i \text{ to task } T_j$$

        Common utility: $U_{ij} = -d(R_i, T_j)$ (negative distance = minimise travel time).

        Assignment optimisation:

        $$\{x_{ij}^*\} = \arg\max_{x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}} \sum_i \sum_j U_{ij} x_{ij}$$

        Subject to $\sum_j x_{ij} = 1$ (each robot assigned exactly one task) and $\sum_i x_{ij} = 1$ (each task assigned to exactly one robot).

        Collective Transport

        Multiple robots cooperate to move an object too heavy for a single robot:

        Force equilibrium condition:

        $$\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{F}_i = \mathbf{F}_{required}$$

        Each robot $i$ applies force $\mathbf{F}_i$. The system must coordinate forces to avoid cancellation:

        $$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\|\mathbf{F}_{required}\|}{\sum_i \|\mathbf{F}_i\|}$$

        Maximum efficiency = 1 (all forces perfectly aligned). Poor coordination wastes force.

        Formation constraint: Robots must maintain contact with the object and not collide with each other:

        $$d(R_i, R_j) \geq d_{\min} \quad \forall i \neq j$$

        $$d(R_i, \text{object}) = d_{\text{contact}} \quad \forall i$$

        Q and A 15.1.1

        Q1: Why is the Hungarian Algorithm limited to ST-SR task allocation, and what approach handles MT-MR allocation?

        A: The Hungarian Algorithm solves a bipartite matching problem: it assigns one robot to one task (ST-SR). For MT-MR tasks, a single task requires multiple robots and a single robot may work on multiple tasks simultaneously. This creates a combinatorial allocation problem where the utility of a coalition of robots on a task is not simply the sum of individual utilities (synergies and conflicts exist). The appropriate formulation is a combinatorial auction or a coalition formation problem (cooperative game theory). Algorithms like CBBA (Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm) extend to MT-MR by having each robot iteratively build a bundle of tasks and reach consensus through decentralised communication, converging to conflict-free allocations in $O(n \cdot m)$ communication rounds where $n$ = robots and $m$ = tasks.

        15.1.2: Drone Coordination

        Drone swarms are a canonical MAS application combining real-time constraints, 3D navigation, and emergent collective behaviour.

        Swarm Kinematics

        Each drone $i$ has state $(x_i, y_i, z_i, \dot{x}_i, \dot{y}_i, \dot{z}_i)$ and obeys:

        $$\ddot{\mathbf{p}}_i = \frac{1}{m}(\mathbf{F}_i^{thrust} + \mathbf{F}_i^{drag} + \mathbf{F}_i^{gravity})$$

        Simplified discrete-time model:

        $$\mathbf{p}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{p}_i(t) + \mathbf{v}_i(t) \Delta t$$

        $$\mathbf{v}_i(t+1) = \mathbf{v}_i(t) + \mathbf{u}_i(t) \Delta t$$

        Where $\mathbf{u}_i(t)$ = control input (acceleration command).

        Collision Avoidance: Velocity Obstacles

        The Velocity Obstacle (VO) of drone $i$ induced by drone $j$ is the set of velocities of $i$ that will cause collision within time horizon $\tau$:

        $$VO_{ij}^{\tau} = \left\{\mathbf{v} : \exists t \in [0, \tau],\; \|\mathbf{p}_i + \mathbf{v} t - (\mathbf{p}_j + \mathbf{v}_j t)\| < r_i + r_j\right\}$$

        ORCA (Optimal Reciprocal Collision Avoidance):

        Each drone chooses velocity outside the union of all VOs:

        $$\mathbf{v}_i^{new} = \arg\min_{\mathbf{v} \notin \bigcup_j VO_{ij}^{\tau}} \|\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{v}_i^{preferred}\|^2$$

        Where $\mathbf{v}_i^{preferred}$ = velocity toward goal ignoring others. ORCA guarantees collision-free motion in $O(n^2)$ computation per time step.

        Formation Flying

        Desired formation: Relative positions $\{\mathbf{d}_{ij}\}$ specifying desired offset of drone $i$ from drone $j$:

        $$\mathbf{p}_i^{des} = \mathbf{p}_{leader} + \mathbf{d}_{i,leader}$$

        Formation error:

        $$\mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{p}_i^{des}$$

        PD controller for formation maintenance:

        $$\mathbf{u}_i = -K_p \mathbf{e}_i - K_d \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i$$

        Formation stability (Lyapunov analysis):

        Let $V = \frac{1}{2} \sum_i (\mathbf{e}_i^T \mathbf{e}_i + \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i^T \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i)$.

        $$\dot{V} = \sum_i (\mathbf{e}_i^T \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i + \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i^T \ddot{\mathbf{e}}_i) = \sum_i \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i^T (\dot{\mathbf{e}}_i + \ddot{\mathbf{e}}_i)$$

        With $\ddot{\mathbf{e}}_i = \mathbf{u}_i = -K_p \mathbf{e}_i - K_d \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i$:

        $$\dot{V} = \sum_i \dot{\mathbf{e}}_i^T ((1 - K_d)\dot{\mathbf{e}}_i - K_p \mathbf{e}_i)$$

        For $K_d > 1$ and $K_p > 0$: $\dot{V} < 0$ whenever $\mathbf{e}_i \neq 0$, proving asymptotic stability.

        15.1.3: Warehouse Automation

        Amazon Robotics (Kiva systems) uses hundreds of mobile robots in a coordinated MAS to automate order fulfilment.

        Warehouse MAS Architecture

        $$\text{WMS} = \langle \text{Central Planner},\; \{R_i\}_{i=1}^n,\; \text{Pod Inventory},\; \text{Stations} \rangle$$

        • Central Planner: Assigns pods to stations and robots to pod-retrieval tasks
        • Robots: Carry pods from storage locations to picking stations
        • Pods: Mobile shelving units containing inventory
        • Stations: Fixed picking/packing locations

        Traffic Management Problem

        Robots navigate on a grid graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ = grid intersections and $E$ = corridors.

        Multi-Robot Path Planning (MRPP): Find collision-free paths for $n$ robots:

        $$\Pi^* = \arg\min_{\Pi} \sum_i |\Pi_i| \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \forall t, i \neq j: \mathbf{p}_i(t) \neq \mathbf{p}_j(t) \text{ and no edge conflicts}$$

        MRPP optimality is NP-hard (even checking whether a solution exists in sub-optimal makespan is PSPACE-hard).

        Practical algorithm: CBS (Conflict-Based Search):

        CBS uses a two-level approach:

        • Low level: plan shortest path for each robot individually ($A^*$)
        • High level: detect conflicts, add constraints, replan conflicting robots

        CBS complexity: Optimal but exponential in the number of conflicts. Effective for dozens to hundreds of robots in structured environments.

        Throughput Optimisation

        System throughput (orders per hour):

        $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{n \cdot \text{robot speed} \cdot \text{utilisation}}{\text{avg trip length}}$$

        $$\text{Utilisation} = 1 - \text{Idle fraction} - \text{Waiting fraction (traffic)}$$

        Pod placement optimisation: Frequently co-ordered items should be on nearby pods to minimise total travel distance. This is analogous to the quadratic assignment problem (QAP):

        $$\min_{\sigma} \sum_{i,j} f_{ij} d_{\sigma(i)\sigma(j)}$$

        Where $f_{ij}$ = flow (frequency of co-ordering items $i$ and $j$) and $d_{ab}$ = distance between storage locations $a$ and $b$. QAP is NP-hard; simulated annealing or genetic algorithms are used.

        15.2: Smart Systems

        15.2.1: Smart Cities

        A smart city is a MAS at urban scale, with millions of agents (sensors, vehicles, buildings, services) interacting to optimise resource use, safety, and quality of life.

        Layered Smart City Architecture

        $$\text{SmartCity} = \langle L_{sense},\; L_{network},\; L_{compute},\; L_{service} \rangle$$

        • $L_{sense}$: Physical sensors (IoT devices, cameras, meters)
        • $L_{network}$: Communication infrastructure (5G, Wi-Fi, LoRaWAN)
        • $L_{compute}$: Edge and cloud computing resources
        • $L_{service}$: Application services (traffic, energy, emergency response)

        City as a Hierarchical MAS

        $$\text{Agents} = \text{Device Agents} \cup \text{District Agents} \cup \text{City Agents}$$

        Device Agents (e.g., smart streetlights): Local control with minimal coordination.

        $$a_i = \pi(o_i) \quad \text{(purely reactive to local sensor)}$$

        District Agents: Coordinate device agents within a zone.

        $$a_j = \pi_j\left(\{o_i : i \in \text{zone}(j)\}\right) \quad \text{(aggregate local information)}$$

        City Agents: High-level resource allocation across districts.

        $$\pi_{city} : \{s_j^{district}\}_{j=1}^k \to \{a_j^{allocation}\}_{j=1}^k$$

        Urban Energy Optimisation

        City-level energy balance:

        $$\sum_{i \in \text{generators}} P_i^{gen}(t) + P_{\text{grid}}(t) = \sum_{j \in \text{consumers}} P_j^{con}(t) + P_{\text{loss}}(t)$$

        Demand Response MAS: Consumer agents adjust demand in response to real-time price signal $\lambda(t)$:

        $$P_j^{con}(t, \lambda) = P_j^{baseline} - \alpha_j (\lambda(t) - \lambda^{ref})$$

        Where $\alpha_j > 0$ is demand elasticity. The market clears when supply equals demand.

        Dynamic pricing:

        $$\lambda(t+1) = \lambda(t) + \rho \left(\sum_i P_i^{gen}(t) - \sum_j P_j^{con}(t, \lambda(t))\right)$$

        This gradient ascent on the excess demand drives price toward market clearing equilibrium.

        15.2.2: Smart Traffic Management

        Traffic Network as a MAS

        $$\text{TrafficMAS} = \langle \mathcal{V},\; \mathcal{E},\; \{I_k\},\; \{R_l\} \rangle$$

        • $\mathcal{V}$ = intersections (nodes)
        • $\mathcal{E}$ = road segments (edges)
        • $\{I_k\}$ = intersection controller agents
        • $\{R_l\}$ = route-choosing vehicle agents (or platoons)

        Macroscopic Traffic Flow Model (LWR)

        The Lighthill-Whitham-Richards model describes aggregate traffic flow:

        $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial q}{\partial x} = 0$$

        Where $\rho(x,t)$ = vehicle density (vehicles/km) and $q(x,t) = \rho \cdot v$ = flow (vehicles/hour).

        Fundamental diagram:

        $$q = \rho \cdot v(\rho) = \rho \cdot v_f \left(1 - \frac{\rho}{\rho_j}\right)$$

        Where $v_f$ = free-flow speed, $\rho_j$ = jam density. Flow is maximised at critical density $\rho^* = \rho_j / 2$.

        Capacity of road segment:

        $$q_{\max} = \rho^* \cdot v_f / 2$$

        Adaptive Signal Control

        A signal controller agent at intersection $k$ controls phase $\phi(t) \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (which directions have green).

        Webster's optimal cycle length:

        $$C^* = \frac{1.5L + 5}{1 - Y}$$

        Where:

        • $L$ = total lost time per cycle (startup delay + clearance time)
        • $Y = \sum_i y_i$ = sum of critical lane volume-to-capacity ratios
        • $y_i = q_i / s_i$ = flow ratio for phase $i$ ($s_i$ = saturation flow rate)

        Reinforcement Learning for signal control:

        State: $s_t = (\text{queue lengths}, \text{waiting times}, \text{current phase})$

        Action: $a_t = $ next phase and duration

        Reward: $r_t = -\sum_l \text{delay}_l(t)$ (negative total vehicle delay)

        $$Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha [r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)]$$

        Multi-intersection coordination: Neighbouring signal agents cooperate to create green waves:

        $$\text{offset}(k, k+1) = \frac{d(k, k+1)}{v_{\text{platoon}}}$$

        Where offset = phase delay between consecutive signals to allow a platoon travelling at $v_{\text{platoon}}$ to see continuous green.

        15.2.3: IoT Coordination

        IoT MAS Architecture

        $$\text{IoT-MAS} = \langle \{D_i\}_{i=1}^N,\; \{G_j\}_{j=1}^M,\; \text{Cloud},\; \mathcal{C} \rangle$$

        • $\{D_i\}$ = IoT devices (sensors, actuators)
        • $\{G_j\}$ = gateway agents (edge nodes)
        • Cloud = centralised analytics and storage
        • $\mathcal{C}$ = communication substrate (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP)

        Data aggregation hierarchy:

        $$\text{Device} \xrightarrow{\text{raw data}} \text{Gateway} \xrightarrow{\text{aggregated}} \text{Cloud} \xrightarrow{\text{decisions}} \text{Gateway} \xrightarrow{\text{commands}} \text{Device}$$

        MQTT Publish-Subscribe Model

        MQTT is the dominant IoT messaging protocol:

        $$\text{Topic hierarchy} : \texttt{home/room1/temperature}$$

        Publish: Device $D_i$ publishes message $m$ to topic $\tau$:

        $$D_i \xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH}(\tau, m)} \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{forward}} \{G_j : G_j \text{ subscribed to } \tau\}$$

        QoS levels:

        QoS LevelDelivery GuaranteeOverhead
        0 (At most once)Fire and forgetMinimum
        1 (At least once)With acknowledgementModerate
        2 (Exactly once)Two-phase commitMaximum

        Message latency model:

        $$\mathbb{E}[t_{deliver}^{QoS2}] = 2 \cdot RTT_{D-Broker} + 2 \cdot RTT_{Broker-G}$$

        Energy-Constrained Agent Design

        IoT devices often have battery constraints. Each sensing and transmission action has energy cost:

        $$E_i^{budget} = E_i^{sense} + E_i^{transmit} + E_i^{compute} + E_i^{idle}$$

        Lifetime of device $i$ with battery capacity $B_i$:

        $$T_i^{life} = \frac{B_i}{P_i^{avg}} = \frac{B_i}{\sum_a p_a \cdot E_i^a / \Delta t_a}$$

        Where $p_a$ = fraction of time in activity $a$ and $E_i^a$ = energy per activity instance.

        Duty cycling: Reduce $p_{\text{active}}$ to extend lifetime at cost of reduced responsiveness:

        $$T_i^{life} \propto \frac{1}{p_{\text{active}}}$$

        Optimal sensing rate (maximise information gain per joule):

        $$r^* = \arg\max_r \frac{I(r)}{E(r)}$$

        Where $I(r)$ = information gained per time unit at rate $r$ and $E(r)$ = energy cost per time unit.

        15.3: Business and Healthcare Applications

        15.3.1: Financial Trading Systems

        Algorithmic Trading MAS

        $$\text{TradingMAS} = \langle \{A_i^{trader}\},\; \text{Exchange},\; \text{DataFeeds},\; \text{RiskManager} \rangle$$

        Agent types in financial MAS:

        AgentRoleDecision Model
        Market MakerProvides liquidity by posting bid/askInventory management, spread optimisation
        Trend FollowerBuys rising assets, sells fallingMomentum signal + moving averages
        Mean ReversionTrades reversal from extremesStatistical arbitrage
        ArbitrageurExploits price discrepanciesCross-market or cross-asset pricing
        Risk ManagerEnforces portfolio constraintsPosition limits, VaR monitoring

        Limit Order Book (LOB) as Shared Environment

        The LOB is the shared data structure all trading agents interact with:

        $$\text{LOB} = \langle \text{Bids}(p_b, q_b),\; \text{Asks}(p_a, q_a) \rangle$$

        Bid-ask spread:

        $$\text{Spread} = p_a^{best} - p_b^{best}$$

        Mid-price:

        $$p_{mid} = \frac{p_a^{best} + p_b^{best}}{2}$$

        Order types:

        • Market order: Execute immediately at best available price
        • Limit order: Post at specified price; waits for counterpart
        • Cancel: Remove a previously posted limit order

        Queue priority: Price-time priority -- better price wins; equal-price orders served FIFO.

        Market Impact Model

        An agent trading large volume $V$ impacts price:

        $$\Delta p = \eta \cdot \text{sgn}(V) \cdot |V|^{\beta}$$

        Where $\eta > 0$ = market impact coefficient and $\beta \in (0.5, 1)$ = concavity (empirically $\approx 0.6$).

        Optimal execution (Almgren-Chriss):

        Sell $V$ shares over $T$ periods. Each period trade $v_t$ shares. Minimise:

        $$\min_{\{v_t\}} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{t=1}^T p_t \cdot v_t\right] + \lambda \cdot \text{Var}\left[\sum_{t=1}^T p_t \cdot v_t\right]$$

        Subject to $\sum_{t=1}^T v_t = V$.

        Optimal solution (risk-neutral $\lambda = 0$): Execute uniformly, $v_t = V/T$.

        Risk-averse solution: Execute faster early (front-load) when $\lambda > 0$, because later periods accumulate more price risk.

        15.3.2: Healthcare Agents

        Clinical Decision Support MAS

        $$\text{ClinMAS} = \langle A_{patient},\; A_{physician},\; A_{diagnosis},\; A_{treatment},\; A_{monitor} \rangle$$

        • $A_{patient}$: Represents patient preferences and health data
        • $A_{physician}$: Models physician expertise and responsibilities
        • $A_{diagnosis}$: Infers diagnoses from symptoms and test results
        • $A_{treatment}$: Recommends treatment plans
        • $A_{monitor}$: Tracks patient response and alerts for deterioration

        Diagnostic Reasoning (Probabilistic)

        Differential diagnosis using Bayesian inference:

        $$P(D_i | S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k) = \frac{P(S_1, \ldots, S_k | D_i) P(D_i)}{P(S_1, \ldots, S_k)}$$

        With conditional independence assumption (naive Bayes):

        $$P(D_i | S_1, \ldots, S_k) \propto P(D_i) \prod_{j=1}^k P(S_j | D_i)$$

        Most probable diagnosis:

        $$D^* = \arg\max_{i} P(D_i) \prod_{j=1}^k P(S_j | D_i)$$

        The agent ranks diagnoses by posterior probability and presents top-$k$ with confidence scores.

        Treatment Recommendation as POMDP

        Patient health state $s_t$ is unobservable (POMDP). Physician observes $o_t$ (test results, symptoms). Agent maintains belief $b_t = P(s_t | o_{1:t})$ and recommends treatment to maximise expected health outcome:

        $$\pi^* = \arg\max_{\pi} \mathbb{E}_{b}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t R(s_t, a_t)\right]$$

        Where $R(s_t, a_t)$ = health reward (e.g., quality-adjusted life years, QALY).

        QALY metric:

        $$\text{QALY} = \sum_t w_t \cdot q_t$$

        Where $w_t$ = duration in health state $t$ (in years) and $q_t \in [0,1]$ = quality weight for that state ($0$ = death, $1$ = perfect health).

        ICU Monitoring Agent

        Early Warning Score (EWS):

        $$\text{EWS} = \sum_v f_v(\text{vital}_v)$$

        Where $f_v$ maps vital sign $v$ (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, oxygen saturation) to an integer score based on deviation from normal range.

        Alert threshold: EWS $\geq \theta$ triggers escalation alert to clinical staff.

        MAS coordination: Multiple monitoring agents (one per patient) send alerts to a ward coordinator agent, which prioritises and assigns nursing response based on EWS and staff availability.

        15.3.3: Supply Chain Optimisation

        Supply Chain as MAS

        $$\text{SC-MAS} = \langle \{S_i^{supplier}\},\; \{M_j^{manufacturer}\},\; \{D_k^{distributor}\},\; \{R_l^{retailer}\} \rangle$$

        Each node is an autonomous agent with private inventory, demand forecasts, and ordering policies.

        Bullwhip Effect

        Definition: Demand variability amplifies as it moves upstream in the supply chain.

        If retailer demand variance is $\sigma_R^2$ and lead time is $L$:

        $$\sigma_{manufacturer}^2 \geq \sigma_R^2 \cdot (1 + \frac{2L}{p} + \frac{2L^2}{p^2})$$

        Where $p$ = review period. The amplification factor grows with $L$.

        Bullwhip causes:

        1. Demand signal processing: agents forecast based on observed orders (not end demand)
        2. Order batching: agents accumulate orders to reduce transaction costs
        3. Price fluctuations: agents order extra during promotions
        4. Shortage gaming: agents inflate orders expecting rationing

          5. Mitigation through information sharing:

          If all agents share point-of-sale (POS) data:

          $$\sigma_{manufacturer}^2|_{\text{POS sharing}} = \sigma_R^2 \cdot (1 + \frac{2L^*}{p})$$

          Where $L^*$ = true manufacturing lead time (reduced because retailer-to-manufacturer lead time estimate $L$ is replaced by actual $L^* < L$). Information sharing reduces bullwhip.

          Multi-Echelon Inventory Optimisation

          Stochastic inventory problem at agent $i$:

          $$\min_{s_i, S_i} \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_t (h_i \cdot \text{inventory}^+(t) + b_i \cdot \text{backlog}^-(t) + c_i^{order} \cdot q_i(t))\right]$$

          Where $h_i$ = holding cost, $b_i$ = backlog cost, $c_i^{order}$ = ordering cost.

          Decentralised policy: Each agent uses an $(s_i, S_i)$ policy:

          $$q_i(t) = \begin{cases} S_i - \text{inventory}(t) & \text{if inventory}(t) \leq s_i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

          Optimal $(s^*, S^*)$ for single agent with Poisson demand $\lambda$ and lead time $L$:

          $$s^* = \text{Quantile}_{(b)/(b+h)}(\text{Poisson}(\lambda L))$$

          $$S^* = s^* + \text{economic order quantity} = s^* + \sqrt{\frac{2K\lambda}{h}}$$

          Where $K$ = fixed ordering cost.

          Q and A 15.3

          Q1: How does the bullwhip effect worsen when more agents are in the supply chain, and what formal condition minimises it?

          A: Each tier in the supply chain observes the orders from the tier below (not end-consumer demand) and forecasts based on this noisy signal. If each tier adds variance amplification factor $\alpha > 1$, then with $n$ tiers:

          $$\sigma_n^2 = \alpha^{n-1} \sigma_R^2$$

          For $\alpha = 1.5$ and $n = 5$: $\sigma_5^2 = 1.5^4 \sigma_R^2 = 5.06 \sigma_R^2$ -- a five-fold variance amplification. The formal condition for zero bullwhip effect is that every agent in the supply chain has access to end-consumer demand $d_R(t)$ directly and orders only based on this signal (not upstream orders). This is achieved through centralised demand signal sharing (e.g., CPFR - Collaborative Planning, Forecasting and Replenishment), which broadcasts POS data to all tiers simultaneously, reducing each tier's forecast error to the minimum achievable given actual demand uncertainty.

        Chapter 16: Future Trends and Research Directions

        Chapter Learning Objectives

        • Understand emerging trends shaping the next generation of MAS
        • Formally characterise open research challenges in scalability, explainability, and interoperability
        • Design capstone projects that synthesise concepts from all chapters
        • Develop a research paper development methodology applicable to MAS research

        16.1: Emerging Trends

        16.1.1: Generative AI Agents

        The integration of generative AI (large language models, diffusion models, code generators) into agent architectures is the most significant recent development in MAS.

        Formal Model: Generative Agent

        $$A_{gen} = \langle \text{LLM}_\theta,\; \mathcal{M},\; \mathcal{T},\; \pi_{plan} \rangle$$

        • $\text{LLM}_\theta$: Language model with parameters $\theta$ serving as cognitive core
        • $\mathcal{M}$: Memory system (short-term context + long-term retrieval)
        • $\mathcal{T}$: Tool set (web search, code execution, API calls)
        • $\pi_{plan}$: Planning mechanism (ReAct, ToT, Planner-Executor)

        Emergence of Agent Personas

        In Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023), agents maintain a rich persona and simulate human-like daily life:

        Agent state:

        $$\sigma_i = \langle \text{name}_i,\; \text{traits}_i,\; \text{memories}_i,\; \text{current_plan}_i,\; \text{observations}_i \rangle$$

        Memory retrieval score (combines recency, importance, relevance):

        $$\text{score}(m_k, q, t) = \alpha_1 \cdot \text{recency}(m_k, t) + \alpha_2 \cdot \text{importance}(m_k) + \alpha_3 \cdot \text{relevance}(m_k, q)$$

        $$\text{recency}(m_k, t) = e^{-\lambda(t - t_k)}$$

        $$\text{importance}(m_k) = \text{LLM}(\text{"Rate importance of: "} + m_k) / 10$$

        $$\text{relevance}(m_k, q) = \text{cos-sim}(\phi(m_k), \phi(q))$$

        Reflection: Periodically, the agent synthesises memories into higher-level insights:

        $$\text{Insight} = \text{LLM}(\text{"What can be inferred from: "} + \text{top-}K \text{ memories})$$

        Insights are stored as new memories with high importance scores.

        Multi-Agent Generative Society

        A society of $n$ generative agents creates emergent social dynamics:

        $$\text{Social dynamics} = \mathcal{F}(\{A_{gen}^i\}_{i=1}^n) \quad \text{(not analytically predictable)}$$

        Emergent phenomena observed in simulation:

        • Spontaneous information spread (rumours, norms)
        • Social network formation
        • Collective decision making
        • Role differentiation (leaders, followers)

        Formal complexity: Because each agent conditions on all interactions, the joint distribution over agent states is intractable:

        $$P(\sigma_1^t, \ldots, \sigma_n^t) = \prod_i P(\sigma_i^t | \sigma_{-i}^{t-1}, \mathcal{E}^{t-1}) \quad \text{(factored approximation)}$$

        True joint distribution requires exponential representation in $n$.

        Q and A 16.1.1

        Q1: What are the principal reliability challenges when using LLMs as agent cognitive cores, and how might future systems address them?

        A: The main challenges are: (1) Hallucination: LLMs generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect content. In an agent context, hallucinated tool arguments or reasoning steps can cause compounding errors. Mitigation: ground every factual claim through tool calls (retrieval, calculation), add verification agents that fact-check outputs before committing actions. (2) Inconsistency: LLM outputs for identical inputs vary (temperature > 0) and may contradict each other across turns. Mitigation: use deterministic decoding (temperature = 0) for critical decisions, or ensemble multiple LLM calls and take majority. (3) Context sensitivity: agent behaviour changes unpredictably when context changes slightly. Mitigation: standardise prompt templates, use few-shot examples pinned in the system prompt, and test across diverse contexts. Future directions include formal verification of LLM agent behaviours, neurosymbolic architectures that combine LLM flexibility with symbolic guarantees, and training paradigms specifically optimising for consistency and factual grounding.

        16.1.2: Human-Agent Collaboration

        Human-Agent Collaboration (HAC) studies how humans and AI agents work together, each contributing complementary strengths.

        Complementarity Model

        Humans and agents have complementary capability profiles:

        $$\text{Capability}_{human} = \langle \text{common sense},\; \text{creativity},\; \text{ethical judgment},\; \text{domain expertise} \rangle$$

        $$\text{Capability}_{agent} = \langle \text{scale},\; \text{speed},\; \text{consistency},\; \text{data processing} \rangle$$

        Team performance model:

        $$P_{team} = P_{human} \oplus P_{agent} - \text{coordination cost}$$

        Where $\oplus$ is a complementarity function. In the ideal case:

        $$P_{team} > \max(P_{human}, P_{agent})$$

        Allocation of Decisions

        Task allocation by capability:

        Given tasks $\{T_j\}$ each requiring capability profile $c_j$:

        $$\text{Allocate}(T_j) = \begin{cases} \text{Agent} & \text{if } \text{cap}_{agent}(c_j) \gg \text{cap}_{human}(c_j) \\ \text{Human} & \text{if } \text{cap}_{human}(c_j) \gg \text{cap}_{agent}(c_j) \\ \text{Shared} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

        Automation level spectrum:

        $$\text{Level}_0: \text{Human does everything}$$

        $$\text{Level}_k: \text{Agent suggests } k\text{th-level decisions; human decides}$$

        $$\text{Level}_{10}: \text{Agent does everything; human not informed}$$

        Appropriate level $k^*$ depends on:

        $$k^* = g(\text{task risk},\; \text{agent reliability},\; \text{human availability},\; \text{regulatory requirement})$$

        High-risk tasks (medical, legal, safety-critical) require lower $k^*$ (more human control) regardless of agent capability.

        Trust Calibration

        Over-trust: Human accepts agent outputs without critical evaluation.

        $$P(\text{accept}|\text{agent wrong}) \to 1 \quad \text{(harmful)}$$

        Under-trust: Human ignores agent assistance.

        $$P(\text{accept}|\text{agent correct}) \to 0 \quad \text{(wastes capability)}$$

        Calibrated trust:

        $$P(\text{accept}|\text{agent correct}) \approx \text{agent accuracy}$$

        $$P(\text{accept}|\text{agent wrong}) \approx 1 - \text{agent accuracy}$$

        Calibration is achieved through: transparency (explaining agent reasoning), track record (showing historical accuracy per task type), and uncertainty quantification (agent communicates its confidence).

        XAI contribution to trust calibration:

        An explainable agent provides:

        $$(\hat{y},\; e) = A(x)$$

        Where $\hat{y}$ = prediction and $e$ = explanation. Humans who understand $e$ can better assess whether $\hat{y}$ is trustworthy in the current context.

        16.1.3: Digital Twins

        A digital twin is a real-time virtual replica of a physical system (asset, process, city, human), enabling simulation, monitoring, and what-if analysis.

        Formal Digital Twin Model

        $$DT = \langle \mathcal{P},\; \mathcal{V},\; \Phi,\; \text{Sync} \rangle$$

        • $\mathcal{P}$: Physical system with state $s_P$
        • $\mathcal{V}$: Virtual model with state $s_V$
        • $\Phi$: Mapping (physical to virtual): $s_V = \Phi(s_P)$
        • $\text{Sync}$: Synchronisation mechanism (how often $s_V$ is updated from $s_P$)

        Fidelity of digital twin:

        $$\text{Fidelity}(DT) = 1 - \frac{\|s_V - \Phi(s_P)\|}{\|\Phi(s_P)\|}$$

        High-fidelity twins closely track physical state. Synchronisation lag $\Delta t_{sync}$ reduces fidelity for fast-changing systems.

        Digital Twin as MAS Hub

        In a MAS context, the digital twin is a shared virtual environment:

        $$\text{Agents} \xrightarrow{\text{read}} DT \xrightarrow{\text{reflects}} \text{Physical System}$$

        Agents query the twin for current state, run simulations on the twin to evaluate actions before committing:

        $$\hat{o}_j = \text{Simulate}(DT,\; a_j,\; \Delta t) \quad \text{(before actual execution)}$$

        Only execute $a_j$ if $\hat{o}_j$ meets criteria. This is analogous to model-based RL, where the twin is the learned model.

        Predictive maintenance using digital twin:

        $$P(\text{failure within } \tau | s_V(t)) = \text{SurvivalModel}(s_V(t), \tau)$$

        Agents monitoring the twin trigger maintenance actions when $P(\text{failure}) \geq \theta$.

        Fleet of digital twins (MAS of twins):

        Each physical asset $k$ has a twin $DT_k$. A fleet manager agent coordinates across twins:

        $$\text{Fleet Manager}: \{DT_k\}_{k=1}^N \to \text{Resource Allocation Plan}$$

        Optimising, for example, maintenance schedules to minimise total fleet downtime.

        Q and A 16.1.3

        Q1: What is the synchronisation frequency required for a digital twin of an autonomous vehicle to be useful for real-time safety decisions?

        A: Autonomous vehicle safety decisions operate at 10--100 Hz (every 10--100 ms). A digital twin used for real-time safety must therefore synchronise at the same frequency: $f_{sync} \geq 10$ Hz, corresponding to $\Delta t_{sync} \leq 100$ ms. At 60 mph (27 m/s), a 100 ms lag means the virtual twin's position is 2.7 m behind the physical vehicle -- significant for collision avoidance at close range. For safety-critical decisions at that speed, $f_{sync} \geq 50$ Hz ($\Delta t_{sync} \leq 20$ ms, positional lag $\leq 0.54$ m) would be required. This imposes strict requirements on the sensor-to-twin communication latency, typically requiring dedicated edge computing with sub-10 ms network latency.

        16.2: Research Challenges

        16.2.1: Scalability

        Scalability is the ability of a MAS to maintain acceptable performance as the number of agents, tasks, or interactions grows.

        Formal Scalability Definitions

        Agent scalability: System performance metric $M$ satisfies:

        $$M(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \quad \text{for some desired growth function } f$$

        Strong scalability (fixed total work): Doubling agents halves completion time.

        $$T(2n) = T(n) / 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = C/n$$

        Weak scalability (proportional work): Doubling agents and work keeps time constant.

        $$T(2n, 2W) = T(n, W)$$

        Communication Complexity Bottleneck

        In a fully connected MAS, communication scales as $O(n^2)$:

        $$|\mathcal{C}_{messages}| = n(n-1)/2 = O(n^2)$$

        Breaking the bottleneck: Structured communication topologies:

        TopologyLinksDiameterFault Tolerance
        Complete graph$n(n-1)/2$1High
        Ring$n$$n/2$Low
        Tree$n-1$$O(\log n)$None (bridges)
        Hypercube$n \log n / 2$$\log n$Good
        Random regular$kn/2$$O(\log n)$$k$-connected

        Sparse communication bound: The minimum number of links for diameter $d$ in a $\Delta$-regular graph (Moore bound):

        $$n \leq 1 + \Delta \sum_{k=0}^{d-1} (\Delta - 1)^k$$

        Random $\Delta$-regular graphs achieve $O(\log n)$ diameter with $O(n)$ links -- near-optimal trade-off.

        Computational Scalability Challenges

        Multi-agent planning complexity:

        $$\text{Dec-POMDP optimal planning} \in \text{NEXP-complete}$$

        Exact Nash Equilibrium computation:

        $$\text{Nash Equilibrium finding} \in \text{PPAD-complete}$$

        PPAD-complete problems are believed to require superpolynomial time in the worst case. Research directions:

        1. Approximate NE: Find $\epsilon$-NE where no agent gains more than $\epsilon$ by deviating. Polynomial algorithms exist for constant $\epsilon$.
          1. Restricted game classes: Potential games, zero-sum games, and network games have polynomial-time NE algorithms.
            1. Scalable MARL: Multi-agent RL avoids explicit NE computation by converging to equilibrium through repeated play.
              1. Mean Field Games (MFG): For large $n$, approximate all other agents as a continuous mass. The $n$-agent game is replaced by a single-agent optimal control problem against the mean field distribution.

                2. MFG formal model:

                Agent optimises against the distribution $m_t$ of all agents:

                $$V(x, t) = \sup_{a} \mathbb{E}\left[\int_t^T r(x_s, a_s, m_s) ds + g(x_T, m_T)\right]$$

                The distribution $m_t$ satisfies a continuity equation driven by the optimal policy:

                $$\partial_t m + \nabla \cdot (f(x, \pi^*(x,t)) m) = \sigma^2 \Delta m$$

                The coupled system $(V, m)$ is an MFG. Solving it gives approximate equilibrium for the $n$-agent game as $n \to \infty$.

        16.2.2: Explainability

        Explainable AI (XAI) in MAS requires not only explaining individual agent decisions but also explaining how collective outcomes emerge from agent interactions.

        Levels of Explanation in MAS

        Level 1: Individual agent explanation

        $$E_{A_i}(a) = \text{Why did agent } A_i \text{ take action } a \text{ in state } s?$$

        Addressed by standard XAI methods: LIME, SHAP, attention visualisation.

        Level 2: Interaction explanation

        $$E_{A_i, A_j}(\text{interaction}) = \text{Why did } A_i \text{ and } A_j \text{ coordinate as they did?}$$

        Level 3: Emergent behaviour explanation

        $$E_{system}(\text{outcome}) = \text{Why did the global outcome } O \text{ emerge from individual behaviours?}$$

        Level 3 is the hardest and least addressed.

        SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) for Feature Attribution

        For a model $f$ and instance $x$, SHAP values $\phi_j$ explain feature $j$'s contribution:

        $$\phi_j(f, x) = \sum_{S \subseteq F \setminus \{j\}} \frac{|S|!(|F|-|S|-1)!}{|F|!} \left[f(S \cup \{j\}) - f(S)\right]$$

        Where $F$ = set of all features and $f(S)$ = model output using only features in $S$.

        SHAP efficiency property: $\sum_j \phi_j = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]$

        The SHAP values sum to the model's output minus its expected value over the data distribution.

        SHAP complexity: Computing exact SHAP values is $O(2^{|F|})$. Approximate methods (TreeSHAP for tree models: $O(|F| \cdot D)$ where $D$ = tree depth; KernelSHAP using weighted linear regression: $O(|S| \cdot |F|^2)$ where $|S|$ = number of coalition samples) make it tractable.

        Causal Explanation in MAS

        Actual causation (Halpern-Pearl):

        Agent $A_i$'s action $a_i$ is an actual cause of outcome $O$ iff:

        1. $a_i$ and $O$ both occurred (actuality)
        2. There exists a contingency $\mathbf{u}$ such that under $\mathbf{u}$: $O$ occurs when $a_i = a_i^{actual}$ and $O$ does not occur when $a_i \neq a_i^{actual}$ (counterfactual dependence under $\mathbf{u}$)

          3. This definition handles complex causal structures including preemption, overdetermination, and joint causation.

          Multi-agent causal attribution:

          $$\text{ResponsibilityShare}(A_i, O) = \frac{|\text{contingencies where } A_i \text{ is actual cause}|}{|\text{all contingencies}|}$$

        16.2.3: Interoperability

        Interoperability is the ability of agents built by different developers, on different platforms, and using different ontologies to meaningfully communicate and cooperate.

        Semantic Interoperability Stack

        $$\text{Interoperability} = \text{Syntactic} + \text{Semantic} + \text{Pragmatic} + \text{Organisational}$$

        LayerChallengeSolution
        SyntacticDifferent message formatsStandard protocols (ACL, JSON-LD, RDF)
        SemanticDifferent concept meaningsOntology alignment, shared upper ontology
        PragmaticDifferent interaction conventionsStandard protocols (FIPA, OpenAPI)
        OrganisationalDifferent governance, trust domainsFederated identity, legal frameworks

        Ontology Alignment

        Given two ontologies $O_1$ and $O_2$, compute alignment $\mathcal{A}$:

        $$\mathcal{A} = \{(e_1, e_2, r, c) : e_1 \in O_1,\; e_2 \in O_2,\; r \in \{\equiv, \sqsubseteq, \sqsupseteq\},\; c \in [0,1]\}$$

        Each alignment cell $(e_1, e_2, r, c)$ states that concept $e_1$ from $O_1$ has relation $r$ with concept $e_2$ from $O_2$ with confidence $c$.

        Alignment quality:

        $$\text{Precision} = \frac{|\mathcal{A} \cap \mathcal{A}^*|}{|\mathcal{A}|}$$

        $$\text{Recall} = \frac{|\mathcal{A} \cap \mathcal{A}^*|}{|\mathcal{A}^*|}$$

        Where $\mathcal{A}^*$ = ground-truth alignment (established by domain experts).

        Embedding-based alignment:

        Embed all concepts using language model:

        $$\mathbf{e}_1 = \phi(\text{label}(e_1) + \text{context}(e_1))$$

        $$\text{sim}(e_1, e_2) = \cos(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2)$$

        $$\mathcal{A} = \{(e_1, e_2, \equiv, \cos(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2)) : \cos(\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2) \geq \theta\}$$

        Protocol Interoperability via Mediation

        A mediator agent $M$ sits between two agents with different protocols $P_1$ and $P_2$:

        $$A_1 \xrightarrow{P_1} M \xrightarrow{P_2} A_2$$

        Mediator maintains translation mappings:

        $$\text{Translate}: P_1.\text{messages} \to P_2.\text{messages}$$

        $$\text{Translate}(m_1) = m_2 \text{ s.t. } \text{semantics}(m_1) = \text{semantics}(m_2)$$

        The mediator introduces $2 \times RTT$ additional latency. For latency-sensitive applications, compile-time protocol translation (code generation) is preferred.

        Q and A 16.2

        Q1: Why is explaining emergent MAS behaviour harder than explaining individual agent decisions?

        A: Individual agent explanations address a single input-output function: given features $x$, why did the agent output $a$? This is a well-defined attribution problem with established methods (SHAP, LIME). Emergent behaviour arises from nonlinear interactions among agents -- the global outcome $O$ is not a function of any single agent's decision but of the joint history of all agents' decisions and the environment's responses. Attribution is complicated by: (1) Joint causation: multiple agents simultaneously cause the outcome; (2) Indirect effects: $A_i$'s action affects $A_j$ which affects $O$ through a chain of interactions; (3) Counterfactual complexity: the relevant counterfactual is not just one agent behaving differently, but all agents responding to that change differently; (4) Non-linearity: small changes in one agent's policy can cause large qualitative changes in emergent behaviour (butterfly effect). Research approaches include causal Shapley values for multi-agent systems, emergent description learning, and simulation-based explanation.

        16.3: Capstone Projects

        16.3.1: MAS Simulation Projects

        A MAS simulation project demonstrates understanding by implementing and evaluating a complete multi-agent system.

        Project Methodology Framework

        Phase 1: Problem Specification (Week 1-2)

        Define the MAS problem formally:

        $$\Pi = \langle N,\; E,\; G,\; C,\; M \rangle$$

        • $N$: Number and types of agents
        • $E$: Environment model (type, state space, dynamics)
        • $G$: Goals (individual, team, or social welfare)
        • $C$: Constraints (resources, communication, time)
        • $M$: Performance metrics

        Phase 2: Agent Design (Week 3-4)

        Design each agent type with formal specification:

        $$A_i = \langle \text{Architecture},\; \text{Perception},\; \text{Action},\; \text{Reasoning},\; \text{Communication} \rangle$$

        Specify the agent's decision model: reactive rules, MDP policy, BDI plans, or LLM-based reasoning.

        Phase 3: Coordination Design (Week 5)

        Choose coordination mechanisms:

        $$\text{Coordination} = \langle \text{Protocol},\; \text{Allocation},\; \text{Conflict Resolution} \rangle$$

        Justify each choice with reference to formal properties (completeness, optimality, complexity).

        Phase 4: Implementation (Week 6-8)

        Select platform (JADE, SPADE, NetLogo, Mesa, custom). Implement agents, environment, and coordination.

        Phase 5: Evaluation (Week 9-10)

        Design controlled experiments varying:

        $$\text{Independent variables}: \{n, \text{environment type, coordination mechanism}\}$$

        $$\text{Dependent variables}: M = \{M_1 \text{ (performance)}, M_2 \text{ (efficiency)}, M_3 \text{ (robustness)}\}$$

        Statistical analysis: Report mean and standard deviation across $K \geq 30$ simulation runs to account for stochasticity.

        Confidence interval for metric $M$:

        $$CI_{95\%} = \bar{M} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\sigma_M}{\sqrt{K}}$$

        Phase 6: Report (Week 11-12)

        Follow ACM/IEEE paper structure (see 16.3.3).

        Suggested Simulation Projects by Difficulty

        LevelProjectKey MAS Concepts
        IntroductorySchelling Segregation ModelReactive agents, emergence
        IntermediateMulti-Robot Search and RescueTask allocation, DCOP, communication
        AdvancedAuction-Based Resource MarketMechanism design, game theory
        ExpertMulti-Echelon Supply Chain with RLMARL, decentralised optimisation

        16.3.2: Real-World Deployment

        Deploying a MAS in production introduces engineering challenges beyond simulation.

        Deployment Architecture

        $$\text{Deployment} = \langle \text{Infrastructure},\; \text{Monitoring},\; \text{Fault Handling},\; \text{Governance} \rangle$$

        Infrastructure requirements:

        RequirementMetricTypical Threshold
        AvailabilityUptime$\geq 99.9\%$ (8.76 hrs downtime/year)
        LatencyP99 responseTask-dependent
        ThroughputMessages/second$\geq$ peak load $\times$ 1.5 (safety margin)
        Fault toleranceRecovery time$\leq$ max tolerable downtime

        Availability calculation:

        $$\text{Availability} = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$$

        Where MTTF = mean time to failure, MTTR = mean time to repair.

        For a system with $n$ independent agents each with $\text{Avail}_i = 0.999$, requiring $k$ of $n$ to function:

        $$\text{System Availability} = \sum_{j=k}^{n} \binom{n}{j} (0.999)^j (0.001)^{n-j}$$

        Monitoring and Observability

        A deployed MAS must be instrumented with:

        Metrics: Quantitative measurements (message throughput, latency, resource usage)

        $$\text{Golden signals}: \{\text{Latency},\; \text{Traffic},\; \text{Errors},\; \text{Saturation}\}$$

        Logging: Structured event records for post-hoc analysis

        $$\text{Log entry}: (\text{timestamp},\; \text{agent_id},\; \text{event_type},\; \text{payload})$$

        Tracing: End-to-end trace of a request across multiple agents

        $$\text{Trace}: \text{span}_1 \to \text{span}_2 \to \cdots \to \text{span}_k$$

        Each span has start time, end time, parent span, and metadata.

        Alert threshold (statistical process control):

        $$\text{Alert if} \quad |M_t - \bar{M}| > k \cdot \sigma_M$$

        Where $k = 3$ (three-sigma rule). This triggers on genuine anomalies while limiting false positives.

        Governance and Accountability

        A deployed MAS must have clear accountability structures:

        $$\text{For every decision } a \text{ by agent } A_i:\; \exists \text{ responsible human or organisation}$$

        Audit trail: Every agent decision is logged with:

        • Agent identity and version
        • Input state and observations
        • Decision and reasoning (if explainable)
        • Outcome and consequences

        Regulatory compliance: Domain-specific regulations apply:

        • Financial MAS: MiFID II (EU), Regulation AT (US) -- real-time monitoring, kill switches
        • Healthcare MAS: FDA 510(k) (US), MDR (EU) -- clinical validation, safety analysis
        • Autonomous vehicles: ISO 26262 (functional safety), ISO 21448 (SOTIF)

        16.3.3: Research Paper Development
