        Research Paper Structure for MAS

        A complete MAS research paper follows this structure:

        1. Title and Abstract (200 words)

        The title should identify: (a) the problem, (b) the approach, (c) the key result.

        Template: "[Novel Method] for [MAS Problem]: [Key Result]"

        The abstract must answer: What is the problem? Why is it important? What did we do? What did we find?

        2. Introduction (1-2 pages)

        • Motivate the problem with a concrete scenario
        • State the research gap clearly: "No existing work addresses X"
        • Summarise contributions as a bulleted list
        • Outline the paper structure

        3. Related Work (1-2 pages)

        Organise related work by theme, not chronologically. For each related work, state: what they do, how it differs from your approach, and what gap remains.

        Related work gap analysis:

        $$\text{Gap} = \text{Our Problem} \setminus \bigcup_i \text{Coverage}(\text{paper}_i)$$

        4. Problem Formulation (1-2 pages)

        State the problem precisely using formal notation introduced in this course. Define all mathematical objects. State assumptions explicitly.

        5. Proposed Approach (3-5 pages)

        Describe your algorithm or system in full detail. Every step must be precisely specified. Provide pseudocode for algorithms. Prove key theoretical properties (correctness, complexity, convergence).

        Complexity analysis format:

        $$\text{Theorem}: \text{Algorithm X has time complexity } O(f(n)) \text{ and space complexity } O(g(n)).$$

        $$\text{Proof}: \ldots$$

        6. Experimental Evaluation (3-4 pages)

        $$\text{Research questions}: \{RQ_1, RQ_2, \ldots, RQ_k\}$$

        For each RQ, design an experiment that answers it. Report baselines (state-of-the-art methods) for comparison.

        Statistical comparison:

        Use paired $t$-test (parametric) or Wilcoxon signed-rank test (non-parametric) for significance:

        $$H_0: \mu_{proposed} = \mu_{baseline}$$

        $$H_1: \mu_{proposed} > \mu_{baseline}$$

        Report $p$-value and reject $H_0$ if $p < 0.05$.

        Effect size (Cohen's $d$):

        $$d = \frac{\bar{M}_{proposed} - \bar{M}_{baseline}}{s_{pooled}}$$

        $d > 0.8$ = large effect; $0.5 < d \leq 0.8$ = medium; $0.2 < d \leq 0.5$ = small.

        7. Discussion (1-2 pages)

        Interpret results in relation to research questions. Explain unexpected findings. Acknowledge limitations.

        8. Conclusion (0.5-1 page)

        Summarise contributions and findings. State future work.

        9. References

        Follow ACM or IEEE citation format.

        Research Methodology: MAS-Specific Considerations

        Reproducibility: MAS simulations involve stochasticity. Always:

        • Fix random seeds for reproducibility
        • Report results over $\geq 30$ independent runs
        • Release code and configuration publicly

        Ablation study: Systematically remove components to understand their contribution:

        $$\text{contribution}(C_j) = M(\text{full system}) - M(\text{system without } C_j)$$

        Scalability study: Report metrics as $n$ (number of agents) varies:

        $$\{M(n) : n \in \{10, 50, 100, 500, 1000\}\}$$

        Plot $M(n)$ vs $n$ on log-log scale to identify scaling exponent $\alpha$ where $M \propto n^\alpha$.

        Research Questions That Drive MAS Research

        A research paper must make a clear, falsifiable contribution. Contribution types:

        Contribution TypeExample Claim
        New algorithm"Algorithm X achieves $O(n \log n)$ vs $O(n^2)$ for existing method Y"
        Theoretical result"Theorem: property P holds for all games in class C"
        Empirical finding"In domain D, method X achieves 30% higher throughput than Y"
        New framework"Framework F unifies methods A, B, C under a common formalism"
        Negative result"No algorithm can achieve property P in class C (impossibility)"

        Open MAS research questions (as of 2025-2026):

        1. How can emergent behaviour in LLM-based MAS be formally predicted or bounded?
        2. What is the optimal human-agent collaboration interface for high-stakes decisions?
        3. Can Byzantine-fault-tolerant consensus be achieved with $O(n \log n)$ messages (vs $O(n^2)$ for PBFT)?
        4. How do we align multi-agent systems with human values when agents interact with each other?
        5. Can mean field game approximations be extended to heterogeneous agent populations without loss of accuracy?
        6. What are the necessary and sufficient conditions for emergent cooperation in open MAS without central coordination?

        Q and A 16.3

        Q1: A simulation study runs 30 trials and finds mean performance of 0.82 with standard deviation 0.12 for the proposed method, versus 0.71 with standard deviation 0.15 for the baseline. Is the difference statistically significant? Compute Cohen's $d$.

        A: Compute Cohen's $d$: pooled standard deviation $s = \sqrt{(0.12^2 + 0.15^2)/2} = \sqrt{(0.0144 + 0.0225)/2} = \sqrt{0.01845} = 0.1358$. $d = (0.82 - 0.71)/0.1358 = 0.11/0.1358 \approx 0.81$. This is a large effect size ($d > 0.8$). For significance, compute the $t$-statistic for independent samples: $t = \frac{0.82 - 0.71}{\sqrt{0.12^2/30 + 0.15^2/30}} = \frac{0.11}{\sqrt{0.000480 + 0.000750}} = \frac{0.11}{\sqrt{0.00123}} = \frac{0.11}{0.0351} \approx 3.13$. With $df \approx 58$ (two-sample) and $t = 3.13$, the $p$-value is approximately $0.003 < 0.05$. Yes, the difference is statistically significant with a large effect size. The proposed method meaningfully outperforms the baseline.

        Q2: A student claims their MAS research contribution is "we implemented a smart traffic system using JADE." Why is this insufficient as a research contribution, and how should it be reformulated?

        A: An implementation alone is not a research contribution because it does not advance scientific knowledge -- it demonstrates that something can be built, not that a new insight, algorithm, or theory has been discovered. Research contributions must be novel (not previously known), significant (meaningfully advance the field), and verifiable (reproducible by others). The student's claim should be reformulated to identify: (a) the specific problem their system addresses that existing systems cannot, (b) the novel algorithmic or architectural choice they made, and (c) the quantitative evidence that their approach works better. For example: "We propose a decentralised reinforcement learning algorithm for adaptive traffic signal control that reduces average vehicle delay by 23% compared to Webster's fixed-time method in simulation studies, while requiring no inter-intersection communication during execution (reducing infrastructure cost)." This identifies the problem, method, result, and advantage -- a complete research contribution.

        Chapter Summary and Course Conclusion

        Summary of Key Mathematical Results

        TopicKey ResultFormula
        Q-Learning convergenceConverges to $Q^*$ if all states visited$\|Q_t - Q^*\|_\infty \leq \gamma^t C$
        UCB regretNear-optimal exploration$\text{Regret}(T) = O(\sqrt{kT \ln T})$
        Nash ExistenceEvery finite game has NE$\exists \sigma^* : u_i(\sigma^{i*}, \sigma^{-i*}) \geq u_i(\sigma^i, \sigma^{-i*})$
        Shapley ValueUnique fair payoff$\phi_i = \sum_S \frac{|S|!(n-|S|-1)!}{n!}[v(S \cup \{i\}) - v(S)]$
        Byzantine toleranceMinimum agents needed$n \geq 3f + 1$
        Arrow's ImpossibilityNo perfect voting rule$\nexists f$ satisfying all four axioms for $|X| \geq 3$
        Differential PrivacyPrivacy guarantee$P(\mathcal{M}(D) \in S) \leq e^\epsilon P(\mathcal{M}(D') \in S)$
        HNSW query complexityEfficient ANN$O(\log N)$ per query
        Bullwhip amplificationVariance grows upstream$\sigma_n^2 \geq \sigma_R^2 \cdot (1 + 2L/p + 2L^2/p^2)$
        Mean Field GameScalable equilibriumCoupled PDE system in $(V, m)$

        Connections Across Chapters

        The following conceptual thread runs through the entire course:

        An agent must sense, decide, act, and communicate.

        • Sensing: Chapter 4 (environment modelling), Chapter 14 (RAG, memory)
        • Deciding: Chapters 2-3 (architectures), Chapter 9 (learning), Chapter 11 (game theory)
        • Acting: Chapter 10 (planning), Chapter 15 (applications)
        • Communicating: Chapters 5-7 (communication, negotiation, conflict)
        • Doing all of this at scale, securely, and correctly: Chapters 6, 8, 12, 13, 16

        Every real MAS application instantiates all four capabilities simultaneously. Mastery of MAS requires holding all of them in mind together.

        This concludes the Multi-Agent Systems comprehensive learning notebook.

        The formal models and design patterns introduced here form the foundation for both research and engineering in this rapidly evolving field.
